title: Recovering Edge Vision Accuracy
scenario: An autonomous delivery drone runs a 50M parameter object detection model at 30 FPS. To meet the 5W power limit of the edge NPU, the model must execute entirely in INT8. Standard Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) achieves the power and latency targets, but the mAP (mean Average Precision) drops from 0.82 to 0.65, causing missed detections of pedestrians.
common_mistake: Switching back to FP16 or Mixed Precision, which immediately violates the strict 5W power budget and edge NPU silicon constraints (which may only have INT8 ALUs).
realistic_solution: Architect a Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) pipeline. PTQ fails because static calibration cannot adequately capture and mitigate the clipping errors from activation outliers in the compact vision model. QAT simulates the quantization effects (clipping and rounding) during the training forward pass using FakeQuant nodes. The gradients are approximated using the Straight-Through Estimator (STE). By fine-tuning the model for a few epochs with QAT, the weights adjust to accommodate the restricted INT8 dynamic range, effectively pushing the Pareto curve to recover the mAP to ~0.80 while strictly
napkin_math: Edge NPU budget: 5W. FP32 MAC energy ~ 3.7pJ. INT8 MAC energy ~ 0.2pJ (18x lower). 50M params * 30 FPS * ~100 MACs/param = 150 GigaMACs/sec. FP32 power = 150G * 3.7pJ = 0.55W (compute only, memory is 10x higher, easily exceeding 5W). INT8 compute power = 150G * 0.2pJ = 0.03W. Thus, INT8 is physically required to stay under the thermal envelope.

title: Systematic Activation Outliers
scenario: You are deploying a 13B LLM for a high-throughput summarization task. To maximize compute efficiency on A100s, you target W8A8 (INT8 weights and activations) utilizing INT8 Tensor Cores. However, standard W8A8 PTQ causes zero-shot accuracy to plummet by 25%. Analysis shows massive, systematic outliers in specific activation channels (up to 100x the median value).
common_mistake: Using dynamic per-token activation quantization without addressing the channel outliers, which adds severe runtime overhead and still struggles with channel-wise variance.
realistic_solution: Implement SmoothQuant or a similar mathematically equivalent transformation. In LLMs, activation outliers are highly systematic (they appear in specific channels across all tokens), while weights are relatively uniform. Standard per-tensor activation quantization clips these outliers or destroys the precision of non-outliers. SmoothQuant introduces a per-channel scaling factor that mathematically migrates the quantization difficulty from the activations to the weights. By dividing the activation channels by a scale factor and multiplying the corresponding weight channels by the same factor (Y
napkin_math: A100 FP16 dense peak is 312 TFLOPS. INT8 dense peak is 624 TOPS (2x compute speedup). If an activation channel has max value 500 and median 5, an INT8 range [-127, 127] gives a scale of 500/127 = 3.93. The median 5 becomes 5/3.93 = 1.27 (rounded to 1), losing massive precision. SmoothQuant scales the 500 down to ~50, reducing the scale factor to 0.39, preserving the precision of the median values.

title: RecSys Network Bottlenecks
scenario: A Deep Learning Recommendation Model (DLRM) uses a 200GB embedding table sharded across 8 GPUs (25GB per GPU) via model parallelism. At 10,000 QPS, the P99 latency is 60ms (budget is 30ms). Profiling reveals that the compute time is only 5ms; the bottleneck is the All-to-All network communication transferring gathered FP32 embedding vectors across the PCIe/NVLink bus.
common_mistake: Attempting to quantize the MLP weights, which does nothing to solve the All-to-All network bandwidth bottleneck caused by the massive embedding vector transfers.
realistic_solution: Architect a solution applying Row-wise PTQ or QAT to the embedding tables, compressing them from FP32 to INT8 or INT4. In DLRM model parallelism, GPUs look up embeddings locally and then must exchange them (All-to-All) to assemble the full feature vector before the top MLP. By storing and transmitting the embeddings in INT8, you immediately reduce the network payload by 4x. The vectors are only dequantized to FP16/FP32 immediately before the top MLP compute phase. Row-wise quantization (calculating scale/zero-point per embedding vector) is critical here because embedding vectors have highly in
napkin_math: 10,000 QPS. Assume 200 features per request, embedding dimension 128. Payload per request = 200 * 128 * 4 bytes (FP32) = 102.4 KB. Total network traffic = 10,000 * 102.4 KB = ~1.02 GB/s per GPU. However, at peak burst, the All-to-All latency dominates. Compressing to INT8 reduces payload to 25.6 KB. Transfer time drops by 4x, reducing the network phase from ~45ms down to ~11ms, easily meeting the 30ms P99 budget.

title: Static Calibration Distribution Shift
scenario: A transformer translation model runs W8A8 static PTQ at 200 QPS. The static calibration was done on a dataset of short conversational sentences (avg 10 tokens). In production, the model receives complex legal documents (avg 500 tokens). Latency is optimal (40ms), but the BLEU score on production data drops by 15 points due to severe activation clipping.
common_mistake: Increasing the number of calibration samples of the same short-sentence dataset, which fails to capture the long-context activation distributions.
realistic_solution: The architecture suffers from calibration distribution mismatch. In transformers, activation magnitudes (especially in attention layers and residual streams) scale with sequence length. Static ranges `[-max, max]` calculated on 10-token sequences are far too narrow for 500-token sequences, leading to severe clipping of valid activations. The solution is twofold: 1) Re-calibrate the static PTQ using a representative dataset that matches production sequence lengths (legal documents), and 2) If variance remains high, shift the architecture from Static PTQ to Dynamic Activation Quantization, where
napkin_math: If max activation for length 10 is 15.0, static scale is 15.0/127 = 0.118. For length 500, attention sums grow, max activation hits 60.0. Using the static scale of 0.118, the INT8 value would need to be 60.0 / 0.118 = 508, which clips to 127. The dequantized value is 127 * 0.118 = 15.0. An error of 45.0 (75% error) is injected into the residual stream per layer, destroying the representation.

title: Mixed-Precision Fleet Integration
scenario: You manage a global serving fleet of 1,000 GPUs for a 50B parameter model. 300 are H100s (FP8 capable), and 700 are older A10Gs (only INT8 capable). You must deploy a single model artifact from CI/CD to simplify versioning, but you need to maximize hardware utilization to hit 50,000 QPS fleet-wide. Storing multiple 50GB artifacts in the registry is prohibited.
common_mistake: Quantizing the universal artifact to INT8, which wastes the specialized FP8 tensor cores and higher precision capabilities of the H100 GPUs.
realistic_solution: Design a Just-In-Time (JIT) quantization architecture or a universal precision-aware artifact. Store the base model weights in the registry in BF16 (or a highly compressed format like FP4 with metadata if storage is the absolute bottleneck, though BF16 is standard). During the deployment phase (container initialization), the inference server queries the local hardware topology. If an H100 is detected, the server dynamically applies FP8 (E4M3) PTQ using pre-computed calibration scales stored as lightweight metadata in the artifact. If an A10G is detected, it applies INT8 SmoothQuant using its r
napkin_math: A10G INT8 TOPS = 250. H100 FP8 TOPS = 3,958. If forced to INT8, H100 falls back to INT8 TOPS (1,979), leaving ~2,000 TOPS unused. By using hardware-aware JIT quantization, 300 H100s provide 300 * 3958 = 1.18 ExaFLOPS, while 700 A10Gs provide 700 * 250 = 175 PetaOPS. Total fleet compute is optimized, and artifact storage is kept to 1 model * 50B * 2 bytes = 100GB (plus <1GB for calibration metadata).

title: LLM Decode Batch Size Limits
scenario: You are optimizing a Llama-70B serving cluster on 4x 80GB A100 GPUs. At batch size 1, the decode phase achieves 18 tokens/s per user. To hit a throughput target of 2000 tokens/s, the team proposes aggressively increasing the batch size to 256. However, at batch size 64, latency already spikes beyond the 100ms/token SLA, and throughput plateaus. Explain this bottleneck using the roofline model.
common_mistake: Assuming that increasing batch size linearly increases throughput until you run out of compute TFLOPS.
realistic_solution: The decode phase of LLM generation is fundamentally memory-bandwidth bound. At batch size 1, you must read the entire 140GB model from HBM to generate a single token, resulting in an extremely low arithmetic intensity (I ≈ 2 FLOPs/byte). As you increase batch size, you reuse the weights across multiple requests, increasing arithmetic intensity (I ≈ 2 * B). This moves the workload to the right on the Roofline model. However, increasing batch size also increases the KV cache size. Before you ever reach the compute-bound ridge point, you hit a memory capacity bound. The KV cache fills the remaini
napkin_math: A100 Ridge Point: 312 TFLOPS / 2 TB/s ≈ 156 FLOPs/byte. 70B model in FP16 = 140GB weights. Distributed across 4 GPUs = 35GB/GPU. Max BW = 2 TB/s per GPU. Weight load time = 35GB / 2TB/s = 17.5ms per token. At BS=1, I = 2. At BS=64, I = 128 (still on the memory-bound slope!). KV Cache for BS=64 at 2k context = 64 * 2000 * 70 layers * 64 heads * 128 dim * 2 bytes * 2 (K+V) ≈ 29GB. Total VRAM used: 35GB (weights) + 29GB (KV) + activations. You are out of VRAM before hitting the compute ridge.

title: The Quantization Roofline Paradox
scenario: Your team quantizes an RNN-based sequence model from FP16 to INT8. The target accelerator provides 4x more INT8 TOPS than FP16 TFLOPS, and the memory footprint is halved. The team expects a 3x-4x latency reduction, but the end-to-end latency only improves by 18%. Identify the discrepancy using workload characteristics.
common_mistake: Assuming that because hardware TOPS increase by 4x, the workload execution speed will scale proportionally.
realistic_solution: RNNs process tokens sequentially, requiring the entire weight matrix to be loaded from HBM for every token. This makes them heavily memory-bandwidth bound (Arithmetic Intensity I ≈ 2 FLOPs/byte). When you move from FP16 to INT8, you halve the memory traffic, which theoretically doubles the speed of the memory-bound portion. However, the 4x increase in compute TOPS is completely irrelevant because the ALUs were already starving for data; raising the compute ceiling doesn't help a workload stuck on the memory bandwidth slope. Furthermore, the 18% actual improvement (instead of 50%) is due to two
napkin_math: Original FP16: 10ms total latency (9ms memory transfer, 1ms compute). INT8 halves memory transfer: 9ms / 2 = 4.5ms. INT8 compute is 4x faster: 1ms / 4 = 0.25ms. Add 0.5ms quantization overhead. New latency = 4.5 + 0.25 + 0.5 = 5.25ms. Expected speedup: 10 / 5.25 ≈ 1.9x. If actual is only 1.18x (8.4ms), kernel launch overheads (~3ms total) are dominating the critical path.

title: Recomputation and Arithmetic Intensity
scenario: While training a foundation model with a 32k context window, standard attention consumes 65% of the step time. You implement FlashAttention, which actually increases the total number of FLOPs performed by ~15% due to recomputation in the backward pass. Despite doing more math, the attention phase executes 4x faster. Explain the physical hardware mechanism enabling this.
common_mistake: Explaining that FlashAttention reduces memory footprint without connecting it to arithmetic intensity and HBM bandwidth ceilings.
realistic_solution: Standard attention materializes the massive N x N attention score matrix to High Bandwidth Memory (HBM). For N=32k, this requires reading and writing gigabytes of data per layer, forcing the operation deep into the memory-bandwidth bound region of the Roofline model. FlashAttention uses tiling to fuse the Softmax and matrix multiplications, keeping the intermediate N x N matrix entirely in fast, on-chip SRAM. By doing so, it drastically reduces HBM reads/writes. Even though the backward pass recomputes the forward pass (increasing total FLOPs), the Arithmetic Intensity (FLOPs per byte of HBM t
napkin_math: Seq len N=32768, heads=32. Standard attention reads/writes O(N^2) to HBM. N^2 matrix = 32768 * 32768 * 2 bytes ≈ 2.1 GB per head. For 32 heads, that's 67 GB of HBM traffic per layer just for attention scores. FlashAttention only reads/writes O(N * d) to HBM. 32768 * 128 * 2 bytes ≈ 8 MB per head. HBM traffic drops from 67 GB to ~256 MB. Arithmetic intensity shifts from I ≈ 1 to I ≈ 128, moving from bandwidth-starved to compute-saturated.

title: MoE vs Dense Arithmetic Intensity
scenario: You are replacing a 30B parameter dense LLM with an 8x10B Mixture of Experts (MoE) model. The MoE model routes each token to 2 experts, resulting in roughly 20B active parameters per token. Since 20B < 30B, the MoE model requires fewer FLOPs per token. However, during serving at a batch size of 64, the MoE model's decode throughput (tokens/sec) is 30% lower than the dense model on the same hardware. Why?
common_mistake: Blaming the routing network's compute overhead or network communication latency without analyzing the memory bandwidth degradation.
realistic_solution: The bottleneck is the drastic reduction in effective Arithmetic Intensity. In a dense model, the 30B parameters are loaded from HBM once and applied to all 64 tokens in the batch. In the MoE model, tokens route to different experts. To process the batch, the hardware must load the weights for all activated experts. At batch size 64, it is highly probable that almost all experts (80B parameters total) are activated by at least one token. Therefore, the system loads ~80B parameters from HBM to do the math for 64 tokens, whereas the dense model loads 30B parameters for 64 tokens. The MoE model's
napkin_math: Dense 30B: Batch size 64. Weights read = 60GB (FP16). FLOPs = 2 * 30B * 64 = 3.84 TFLOPs. Intensity I = 3.84e12 / 60e9 = 64 FLOPs/byte. MoE 8x10B (2 active): Batch size 64. Assume all 8 experts are hit across the batch. Weights read = 160GB (80B params). FLOPs = 2 * 20B * 64 = 2.56 TFLOPs. Intensity I = 2.56e12 / 160e9 = 16 FLOPs/byte. The MoE model requires 2.6x more memory bandwidth per step, reducing throughput by ~30-60% depending on HBM limits.

title: DLRM Heterogeneous Bottlenecks
scenario: Your massive click-through rate (CTR) model processes 15,000 QPS. It consists of 600GB of sparse embedding tables and a dense 6-layer MLP. To scale to 30,000 QPS, you must choose between upgrading to GPUs with 3.2 TB/s HBM (but only 80GB capacity per GPU), or moving to dual-socket CPUs with 12 channels of DDR5 (300 GB/s) and 2TB of capacity. Evaluate the tradeoffs using roofline principles.
common_mistake: Assuming GPUs are always better due to higher bandwidth, or putting the whole model on CPU because of capacity.
realistic_solution: DLRMs exhibit a split-personality Roofline profile. The MLP layers are dense matrix multiplications with high arithmetic intensity, placing them firmly in the compute-bound regime where GPUs excel. Conversely, the embedding lookups are highly sparse, requiring random memory accesses. They have an arithmetic intensity of practically zero (just a gather/sum operation) and are bound by memory capacity and memory latency/transaction rate, not peak sequential bandwidth. Upgrading to 80GB GPUs requires sharding the 600GB tables across 8+ GPUs, incurring massive PCIe/NVLink all-to-all communication o
napkin_math: MLP compute: 15,000 QPS * 50M FLOPs/query = 750 GFLOPs (trivial for GPU, slow on CPU). Embeddings: 200 features/query * 15,000 QPS = 3M lookups/sec. Each lookup fetches 128 bytes. Total bandwidth = 3M * 128B = 384 MB/s. This is tiny compared to peak bandwidth! The bottleneck is memory capacity (600GB > 80GB) and transaction latency. CPU DDR5 handles millions of random accesses well and has 2TB capacity. PCIe Gen4 x16 gives 32 GB/s, easily handling the 384 MB/s gathered vectors passed to the GPU.

title: Kernel Fusion Roofline Shift
scenario: Optimizing a real-time speech recognition model, profiling shows 40% of inference time is spent in a sequence of element-wise operations (LayerNorm -> ReLU -> Dropout -> Scale). A junior engineer argues that fusing these kernels won't improve performance because these operations account for less than 1% of the model's total FLOPs. Counter this argument quantitatively.
common_mistake: Agreeing that low-FLOP operations don't impact execution time, ignoring the memory bandwidth wall.
realistic_solution: Execution time is dictated by the Roofline model, not just total FLOPs. Element-wise operations have an Arithmetic Intensity of roughly 0.5 to 1 FLOPs/byte, placing them deep in the memory-bandwidth bound regime. Every individual kernel call (LayerNorm, ReLU, etc.) must read the activation tensor from HBM, perform a trivial calculation, and write it back to HBM. The hardware's ALUs sit idle waiting for memory. By fusing these four operations into a single kernel, the tensor is read from HBM once into SRAM, all four operations are computed in registers, and the result is written back to HBM onc
napkin_math: Tensor size: 10MB (FP16). Unfused (4 ops): Each op reads 10MB and writes 10MB. Total HBM traffic = 4 * 20MB = 80MB. At 1 TB/s HBM bandwidth, time = 80us. Fused (1 op): Read 10MB, compute all 4 ops in SRAM, write 10MB. Total HBM traffic = 20MB. Time = 20us. Add kernel launch overhead (~5us per launch). Unfused = 80us + (4 * 5us) = 100us. Fused = 20us + 5us = 25us. Speedup is 4x on that 40% of the model.

title: Diffusion Resolution Scaling Cliff
scenario: You are upgrading a text-to-image diffusion model from generating 512x512 images to 1024x1024 images. The pixel count increases by 4x, and the theoretical FLOPs increase by ~4.5x. However, your profiling on A100 GPUs shows that latency per denoising step increases by nearly 9x. Diagnose the architectural bottleneck causing this super-linear scaling.
common_mistake: Assuming latency scales strictly with FLOPs, or missing the quadratic sequence length scaling of attention mechanisms in the UNet.
realistic_solution: The bottleneck lies in the self-attention layers within the diffusion UNet. When resolution doubles (512 to 1024), the sequence length (N = H * W) of the flattened feature maps increases by 4x. Standard self-attention has O(N^2) memory complexity for the attention matrix. Therefore, the memory read/write traffic for the attention scores increases by 16x! This massive explosion in memory traffic violently shifts the arithmetic intensity of the attention layers to the left. A workload that was previously sitting comfortably near the compute-bound ridge point at 512x512 suddenly falls off the cli
napkin_math: At 512x512, downsampled by 8 in latent space = 64x64 latent. Sequence length N = 4096. Attention matrix size = 4096 * 4096 * 2 bytes ≈ 33 MB per head. At 1024x1024, latent is 128x128. Sequence length N = 16384. Attention matrix size = 16384 * 16384 * 2 bytes ≈ 536 MB per head. Traffic increases by 16x. If attention was 20% of the latency at 512x512 (e.g., 20ms out of 100ms) and convolutions were 80% (80ms). New conv time = 80 * 4 = 320ms. New attention time = 20 * 16 = 320ms (assuming bandwidth bound). Total latency = 640ms. 640 / 100 = 6.4x increase. With HBM thrashing, this easily hits 9x.

title: Multi-Camera NPU PCIe Bottleneck
scenario: You are architecting a retail edge server processing 16 concurrent 1080p video streams at 30fps. The system uses a host CPU connected to an edge NPU via PCIe Gen3 x4 (theoretical 3.9 GB/s). Inference latency spikes to 200ms when all streams are active, despite the NPU reporting only 40% compute utilization. Memory profiling shows 99% PCIe bus saturation.
common_mistake: Upgrading to a faster NPU or reducing the batch size to lower latency, ignoring the I/O bottleneck.
realistic_solution: The system is bottlenecked by the PCIe memory hierarchy. You must redesign the architecture to use 'Zero-Copy' and edge-native decoding. Instead of the host CPU decoding video and sending uncompressed RGB frames over PCIe, pass the compressed H.264/H.265 streams directly to the NPU's hardware video decoder (if available) or a unified memory space. Furthermore, fuse preprocessing (resize, normalize) directly into the early layers of the NPU graph so the massive uncompressed data remains entirely in the NPU's high-bandwidth local SRAM/LPDDR, avoiding costly round-trips to the host CPU's main mem
napkin_math: 16 streams * 1920*1080 pixels * 3 channels * 30fps = ~2.98 GB/s of raw RGB data. PCIe Gen3 x4 real-world bandwidth is ~3.2 GB/s. Transmitting uncompressed frames consumes 93% of the bus, causing severe queueing delays.

title: Wearable SRAM Weight Pinning
scenario: You are designing an always-on keyword spotting system for a smartwatch with a strict 5mW active power budget. The microcontroller has 512KB of SRAM and 4MB of eXecute-In-Place (XIP) Flash memory. Your float32 audio model is 1.2MB. Initial testing shows the system drawing 15mW during inference, draining the battery in hours.
common_mistake: Attempting to use DMA to page weights from Flash to SRAM on demand, which still incurs the massive Flash power penalty.
realistic_solution: The excessive power drain is caused by fetching weights from high-latency, high-power Flash memory for every inference. To meet the 5mW requirements, you must architect the system to fit entirely within the 512KB SRAM. Apply INT8 Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) or Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) to compress the 1.2MB model down to ~300KB. Pin these quantized weights permanently in SRAM. Design a ring buffer in the remaining 200KB SRAM for audio feature extraction and intermediate activations, ensuring absolute zero Flash memory accesses during the steady-state inference loop.
napkin_math: Reading from Flash costs ~20 pJ/bit, while SRAM costs ~2 pJ/bit. A 1.2MB model = 9.6M bits. Flash read per inference: 9.6M * 20pJ = 192 uJ. At 50 inferences/sec, memory fetches alone consume 9.6mW > 5mW total budget.

title: On-Device LLM KV Cache Spilling
scenario: Deploying a 3B parameter LLM on a premium smartphone (8GB unified LPDDR5 RAM at 51.2 GB/s). Initially, text generation achieves 18 tokens/sec. However, when the context window reaches 2000 tokens, generation speed plummets to 3 tokens/sec, and the OS occasionally kills the background app.
common_mistake: Reducing the vocabulary size or hidden dimension of the model, which degrades quality without solving the dynamic scaling issue.
realistic_solution: The performance cliff is due to the KV cache exceeding the NPU/GPU's localized SRAM tier and competing with the OS for unified DRAM, causing severe page thrashing. To fix this, you must change the architecture to aggressively manage memory locality. First, implement 4-bit KV cache quantization to reduce the footprint by 75%. Second, implement a chunked attention mechanism (like Flash-Decoding) that loads smaller blocks of the KV cache into the fast SRAM compute cores, performing local reductions before a final global reduction, minimizing DRAM round-trips and avoiding OS-level page faults.
napkin_math: 3B model = 3GB (INT8). KV cache for 2000 tokens (fp16, d_model=3072, 32 layers) = 2*2*3072*32 * 2000 = ~786MB. Total footprint = 3.78GB. Generating 1 token requires reading 3.78GB. At 51.2 GB/s, max theoretical throughput is 51.2 / 3.78 = 13.5 tok/sec. Dropping to 3 tok/sec implies OS swapping.

title: AR Glasses Memory Fragmentation
scenario: An AR headset runs continuous hand-tracking and SLAM. After 45 minutes of operation, the system crashes with a `std::bad_alloc` OOM error. Diagnostics show 1.2GB of physical RAM is still technically 'free' out of the 4GB total, but tensor allocation latency has spiked from 1us to 3ms right before the crash.
common_mistake: Writing a background garbage collection thread to defragment memory, which would ruin real-time 60fps latency guarantees.
realistic_solution: The system is suffering from catastrophic memory fragmentation caused by standard `malloc`/`free` handling variable-sized tensor allocations over long periods. The architecture must be redesigned to use a static Memory Pool or Arena Allocator. Perform an offline analysis of the model's computational graph to determine the maximum liveness footprint (the peak memory required at any point in the graph). Pre-allocate a single, contiguous block of memory (e.g., 50MB) at startup. Reuse offsets within this arena for intermediate activations, guaranteeing zero fragmentation and O(1) allocation time.
napkin_math: At 60fps, dynamic sequence lengths generate ~150 variable-sized allocations per frame. 150 * 60 * 60 * 45 = ~24.3 million allocations over 45 mins. A 15% fragmentation waste on 2.5GB used = 375MB scattered in tiny holes, failing contiguous 5MB tensor requests.

title: ADAS SoC L3 Cache Contention
scenario: You are designing an automotive ADAS SoC with an 8-core CPU, a shared 16MB L3 cache, and a discrete NPU sharing LPDDR5 DRAM. When running a Vision Transformer (ViT) on the NPU in isolation, latency is 15ms. When simultaneously running LiDAR point-cloud preprocessing on the CPU, the ViT latency degrades to 42ms.
common_mistake: Lowering the CPU priority of the LiDAR thread, which doesn't stop the cache eviction when the thread actually is scheduled to run.
realistic_solution: The degradation is caused by cache contention and thrashing in the shared L3 cache and DRAM bus. The CPU's random memory access patterns for LiDAR preprocessing are evicting the NPU's localized tensor data. The architectural solution requires strict memory isolation. Implement Cache Quality of Service (QoS) / Cache Allocation Technology (CAT) to hard-partition the L3 cache (e.g., 8MB locked for NPU, 8MB for CPU). Additionally, map the NPU's large intermediate activation buffers as 'non-cacheable' to bypass the L3 entirely, forcing them directly to LPDDR5 via DMA, preserving the L3 for latency-
napkin_math: ViT needs 8MB working set; LiDAR needs 12MB. 8+12 = 20MB > 16MB L3. Thrashing causes DRAM trips (100ns vs 15ns L3). 10M memory accesses * 85ns penalty = 0.85ms per layer * 32 layers = ~27ms extra latency.

title: Drone Fused Layer Tiling
scenario: A drone is running a high-resolution (4K) object detection CNN on a custom edge accelerator. Processing takes 200ms per frame. The accelerator has 4MB of SRAM and LPDDR4 memory with 25 GB/s bandwidth. Hardware monitors show the compute units (MACs) are only 18% utilized.
common_mistake: Applying channel pruning or weight quantization, which reduces compute and weight footprint but barely impacts the massive activation memory bottleneck.
realistic_solution: The model is severely memory-bandwidth bound due to low arithmetic intensity in the early high-resolution layers. The intermediate activations vastly exceed the 4MB SRAM, causing continuous spilling to DRAM. You must architect a 'fused layer tiling' (depth-first) memory strategy. Instead of computing layer 1 entirely and writing to DRAM, then reading back for layer 2, divide the 4K image into smaller spatial patches (e.g., 256x256). Push each patch through multiple layers sequentially, keeping all intermediate activations strictly within the 4MB SRAM, and only write the heavily downsampled out
napkin_math: 4K image * 32 channels * 2 bytes (fp16) = ~530MB activation size for early layers. Transferring 530MB to/from DRAM at 25 GB/s takes ~42ms per layer. For 4 early layers, that's 168ms of pure DRAM wait time, explaining the 200ms latency.

title: Smart Camera Zero-Copy Pipeline
scenario: A smart camera pipeline processes 12MP (4000x3000) frames. The Image Signal Processor (ISP) outputs YUV420. The CPU converts this to RGB (takes 18ms), copies it to the NPU's reserved memory (takes 12ms), and the NPU runs inference (takes 10ms). Total latency is 40ms, failing the 33ms (30fps) requirement.
common_mistake: Multi-threading the CPU's YUV to RGB conversion, which might speed up compute but still incurs the massive 12ms memory copy penalty.
realistic_solution: The architecture is bottlenecked by inefficient memory movement and format conversions across the memory hierarchy (ISP -> Main RAM -> CPU Cache -> Main RAM -> NPU RAM). The solution is a zero-copy, edge-native pipeline. First, allocate a contiguous dmabuf (Direct Memory Access buffer) shared between the ISP and NPU MMUs. Configure the ISP to write the YUV420 frames directly into this buffer. Second, retrain the NPU model to natively ingest YUV420 channels, or add a hardware-accelerated 1x1 convolution at the start of the NPU graph to handle the YUV-to-RGB conversion, completely bypassing the
napkin_math: 12MP in RGB = 36MB. CPU copying 36MB at 3 GB/s DMA takes 12ms. YUV420 is 1.5 bytes/pixel = 18MB. By skipping the CPU conversion and copy, we save 30ms, bringing latency from 40ms down to 10ms.

title: H100 FP8 Migration Throughput Cliff
scenario: You migrate a 70B parameter LLM serving stack from 4x A100s (INT8 W8A8) to 2x H100s (FP8 W8A8). Theoretical TOPS suggest a >2.5x throughput gain, but your actual QPS only increases from 12 QPS to 14 QPS at batch size 64. Profiling shows high SM idle time despite FP8 Tensor Cores.
common_mistake: Assuming the H100 clock speed is throttling and attempting to increase batch size further, which just causes an OOM.
realistic_solution: The issue is KV cache memory bandwidth bottlenecking the FP8 compute. H100's FP8 Tensor Cores are incredibly fast, but if the KV cache remains in FP16, the memory bandwidth required to fetch it for batch size 64 starves the compute units. The solution requires transitioning to FP8 KV caching. Furthermore, FP8 requires scaling factors (per-tensor or per-channel). If these scaling factors are not fused with the attention kernels (like FlashAttention-3 with FP8 support), the round-trip to global memory for dequantization destroys the latency.
napkin_math: 70B model FP8 weights = 70GB. BS=64, Seq=2048. KV cache in FP16 = 2 * 2 * 64 * 2048 * 80 layers * 8192 dim = 343GB. Fetching 343GB at H100 bandwidth (3.3 TB/s) takes ~104ms per token. FP8 compute for BS64 takes only ~2ms. System is 50x memory bound.

title: W8A8 PTQ Extreme Activation Outliers
scenario: You deploy a 130B parameter LLM for cloud summarization. A naive INT8 PTQ (W8A8) reduces the memory footprint from 260GB to 130GB, fitting on 2x A100 80GB. However, perplexity spikes from 5.4 to 18.2. You find that 0.1% of activation channels have values up to 120.0, while the rest are in [-2, 2].
common_mistake: Clipping the activations dynamically at runtime, which completely destroys the semantic information in the outlier channels.
realistic_solution: Naive per-tensor or per-token activation quantization fails because massive outliers in specific channels force the quantization scale to be huge, crushing the precision of the remaining 99.9% of channels into a single bucket. The architectural solution is to migrate the quantization difficulty from activations to weights (e.g., SmoothQuant). If we scale outliers by 1/10, new range is [-12, 12]. Scale = 12/127 = 0.094. Value 1.5 becomes 15.9 -> 16. 16 * 0.094 = 1.504. Error is < 0.3%.

title: Long-Context INT4 KV Cache Degradation
scenario: Serving a 7B LLM with a 128k context window. At FP16, the KV cache is 32GB per request, heavily limiting concurrency. INT4 has only 16 buckets. When quantizing Key vectors, the dot product precision with the Query vector degrades, causing attention scores to blur across the context length. The architecture needs a mixed-precision or grouped quantization strategy. You should implement Grouped-Quantization (e.g., group size 64 or 128) to capture local variance. Alternatively, keep the most recent tokens (e.g.

Group size 128 adds 16-bit scales per group: 128k * 4096/128 * 32 * 2 * 2 = 524 MB of scale metadata, minimal memory overhead but drastically reduces quantization error.

title: CTR Embedding QAT Pipeline Bottleneck

In QAT for embeddings, simulating quantization on every forward pass is unnecessary. The architectural fix is to implement a delayed or stochastic QAT. Instead of FakeQuant on every lookup, you maintain FP32 master weights but only update the INT8 materialized weights periodically (e.g., every 1000 steps), or write a custom fused CUDA kernel that performs the emb

Requires 3 kernel launches. Custom fused kernel: Read FP32, scale/round in registers, return simulated FP32. Cuts memory traffic by 66% and kernel launch overhead by 3x.

title: Multi-Tenant LoRA Base Model Calibration
scenario: You host a multi-tenant service with 1 base 13B LLM and 500 active LoRA adapters (code, medical, chat). To save GPU memory, you quantize the base model to INT8 (W8A8). realistic_solution: The activation distributions of the base model change significantly depending on the specific downstream task the LoRA adapter is driving. Calibrating the base model's activation scales using Wikipedia creates clipping/rounding errors for the specific activation pathways activated by code-generation inputs. You cannot use a single global static activation scale for all LoRAs. You must either: 1) Switch to dynamic activation quantization (W8A8 dynamic), computing scales on-the-fly per token (costing some compute overhead but fixing accuracy), or 2) If using static PTQ, collect calibration data
napkin_math: Dynamic activation overhead: For a 1x4096 vector, finding max requires 4096 comparisons. On A100 (108 SMs), a block reduction takes <1 microsecond. Matrix multiply 4096x4096x1 takes ~10 microseconds in INT8. Dynamic overhead is ~10%, but saves 50% memory and preserves code-gen accuracy across all tenant adapters.

title: W4A16 Batch-Size Latency Inversion
scenario: Serving a 70B model via GPTQ (INT4 weights, FP16 activations) on 2x A100. At batch size 1, latency is 15ms/token. At batch size 128, latency is 120ms/token, which is actually 20% SLOWER than the pure FP16 model at the same batch size. Explain the architecture cause.

Fetching INT4 weights (35GB) is 4x faster than FP16 weights (140GB). However, W4A16 requires dequantizing the weights to FP16 in registers before the FP16 Tensor Cores can perform the MACs. At batch size 128, the operation becomes compute-bound. The FP16 model just does FP16 MACs. The W4A16 model has to do the INT4->FP16 cast AND the FP16 MACs. The overhead of bit-shifting and dequantizing the weights inside the SMs consumes ALU cycles, making the total compute time longer tha

title: FP8 Distributed Training Divergence
scenario: Training a 30B MoE model from scratch on 1024 H100s using FP8. To maximize throughput, you set all forward and backward passes to use E4M3. At step 4,500, the loss suddenly spikes to NaN. Checkpointing shows the gradients in the routing layers underflowed to zero just before divergence.

E4M3 has higher precision but a smaller dynamic range (max value ~448). E5M2 has lower precision but a larger dynamic range and can represent much smaller numbers before underflowing (like FP16). In deep neural network training, activations and weights generally require higher precision (E4M3), but gradients, especially during the backward pass of complex layers like MoE routers, have a much wider dynamic range and are prone to underflow. The architectural fix is to use E4M3
napkin_math: E4M3 min normal > 2^-6 (approx 0.015). E5M2 min normal > 2^-14 (approx 0.00006). If a gradient value is 0.001, E4M3 quantizes it to 0 (underflow). E5M2 safely represents it. Accumulated 0-gradients in the MoE router cause routing collapse, sending all tokens to one expert, spiking the loss.

title: Diffusion Model INT8 PTQ Artifacts
scenario: You deploy an SDXL text-to-image service. To hit a sub-second SLA, you apply INT8 PTQ. You calibrate the model using 500 high-quality images from the target domain. Latency hits 0.8s, but the output images have severe color banding and structural artifacts. FP16 output is perfect.

realistic_solution: Diffusion models are iterative and their activation distributions change drastically across the denoising timesteps. At T=1000, activations represent mostly noise (Gaussian distribution). At T=0, activations represent sharp image features (highly skewed, localized outliers). Calibrating on raw images or a random subset of timesteps creates a single set of quantization scales that fits neither extreme well. The artifacts occur because the static scales clip the outliers at early timesteps and lose precision at late timesteps. The architecture requires a Timestep-Aware Calibration strategy: eith
napkin_math: If activation variance at T=1000 is 5.0 and at T=10 is 50.0. A static max calibration of 50.0 means at T=1000, the effective INT8 range used is only [-12, 12] out of [-127, 127] (wasting 3 bits of precision). This quantization noise injected at T=1000 compounds over the next 49 steps, causing the color banding.

title: Massive Context KV Cache Paging
scenario: You are designing an inference engine to serve a 1-million context window Llama-3 8B model. The hardware is a single node with 8x H100 (80GB) GPUs, 2TB Host DRAM, and 4x Gen5 NVMe drives. The KV cache for a single request exceeds the available HBM. Design a paging architecture to stream KV cache across the memory hierarchy while maintaining at least 10 tokens/sec decoding speed.

realistic_solution: Design an explicit, software-managed 3-tier memory hierarchy (NVMe -> Host DRAM -> HBM). Divide the KV cache into fixed-size block chunks (e.g., 256 tokens). Pin a sliding window of the most recent blocks in HBM. As generation proceeds, use dedicated background CUDA streams to prefetch upcoming KV blocks from Host DRAM via PCIe, while simultaneously evicting older blocks. Use Host DRAM as an L2 cache, pulling from NVMe asynchronously via io_uring or SPDK to bypass OS page cache overhead. Compute and I/O must be perfectly overlapped.
napkin_math: 1M tokens KV cache = 1M * 32 layers * 8 KV heads * 128 dim * 4 bytes (FP16 K+V) = 131GB. With 80GB HBM, only ~40GB is available for KV cache after model weights. We must page ~90GB. To sustain 10 tokens/sec, the attention operation needs to process 1M tokens per 100ms. PCIe Gen5 x16 bandwidth = 64GB/s. We can transfer 6.4GB per 100ms step. Therefore, we cannot fetch the entire 131GB per token; we must use a Ring Attention or FlashAttention-style block-wise compute that keeps older blocks in Host DRAM and only streams them into HBM in chunks of <6.4GB during the attention pass.

title: Long-Running Inference OOM Death
scenario: Your dynamic batching LLM server runs perfectly for the first 6 hours, processing requests with varying lengths (100 to 8000 tokens). However, around hour 12, the service consistently crashes with CUDA OOM, despite Prometheus metrics showing the theoretical peak memory usage never exceeding 45GB on an 80GB A100 GPU. Contiguous memory allocators (like PyTorch's default caching allocator) struggle when allocating and freeing variable-sized KV cache tensors over long periods. When a large contiguous block is requested for an 8000-token sequence, the allocator fails because available memory is scattered in small chunks. The architectural fix is to implement a paged memory manager (e.g., PagedAttention). Pre-allocate a single massive contiguous block of HBM at startup. Divide it into fixed-size physical blocks (e.g., 16 or 32 tokens). Maintai
napkin_math: Assume batch size 64. Pre-allocating max length (8000 tokens) naively requires 8000 * 64 * 1MB (approx per token KV) = 512GB (impossible). Dynamic allocation of varying sizes (e.g., 100 to 8000) causes gaps. If 45GB is allocated but fragmented into 50MB chunks, an attempt to allocate a contiguous 1GB tensor for a new long sequence fails. With a block size of 16 tokens (approx 2MB), a 40GB KV pool provides 20,000 perfectly reusable slots, ensuring 100% utilization regardless of sequence length variance.

title: SRAM Tiling for Custom Attention
scenario: You are writing a custom fused CUDA kernel for a novel sliding-window attention mechanism used in DNA sequence modeling (seq_len=131,072). The naive PyTorch implementation achieves only 5% Model Flops Utilization (MFU) because it is entirely memory-bandwidth bound. The intermediate N x N attention matrix (Q*K^T) is too large to materialize in HBM, let alone SRAM. We must tile the computation into blocks that fit entirely within the SM's shared memory (SRAM). Load a block of K and V into SRAM. Then, stream blocks of Q from HBM to SRAM, compute the partial attention scores, update the running maximum and denominator for the softmax (using the online safe softmax trick), multiply by V, and accumulate the result in registers. Finally, write the output block back to HBM.

Reading/writing this to HBM at 3 TB/s takes 34.3 / 3000 * 2 = 22.8 ms per layer, yielding terrible MFU. An A100 has 164 KB of shared memory per SM. We can choose a block size of 128x128. Block of K (128x128x2 bytes = 32 KB) and V (32 KB) fit comfortably in the 164 KB SRAM, leaving room for Q blocks and intermediate register spills, keeping all O(N^2) operations on-chip.

title: MoE Weight Fetching Bottleneck
scenario: Your startup is serving an 8x7B Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model for code generation. At batch size 1 (interactive local testing), the token generation rate is 8 tokens/sec, far below the 40 tokens/sec SLA. Profiling reveals the GPU compute units are idle 90% of the time. Evaluate the memory hierarchy bottleneck and propose an architectural solution to hit the SLA.

napkin_math: 8x7B MoE. Total params = 56B (~112GB in FP16). Active params per token (2 experts + dense layers) = ~14B params = 28GB in FP16. HBM bandwidth on an A100 is ~2000 GB/s. Quantizing weights to 4-bit reduces active payload to 7GB. 7 / 2000 = 3.5ms per token, raising the theoretical ceiling to 285 tokens/sec, easily meeting the 40 tokens/sec SLA.

title: High-Density LoRA Adapter Swapping

title: Multi-Tier Embedding Lookups
scenario: Your recommendation system uses an enormous Deep Learning Recommendation Model (DLRM) with 5TB of embedding tables. The hardware is a single node with 8x A100 (80GB) GPUs, 2TB Host RAM, and 8x 3.2TB NVMe drives. You need to process 10,000 QPS with p99 latency < 50ms. Architect the embedding lookup system across this memory hierarchy.

Implement a background prefetching thread that predicts upcoming lookups (e.g., using user session context) to promote embeddings from NVMe -> DRAM -> HBM befo

DRAM latency is ~100ns. HBM latency is ~1us (but massive bandwidth). If cache hit rates are 80% L1, 15% L2, 5% L3: we do 50,000 NVMe reads/sec. 50k reads * 128 bytes (embedding size) = 6.4 MB/s PCIe traffic, which is trivial. title: 3D CNN Activation Checkpointing
scenario: Training a 3D UNet on 512x512x512 CT scans (batch size 1) requires 180GB for forward pass activations, but you only have an 80GB A100 GPU. Using full activation checkpointing (recomputing all layers) drops throughput to 0.5 iter/sec. Goal is 1.5 iter/sec. Propose a hybrid memory architecture balancing recompute and PCIe offloading.

The architecture must evaluate layer characteristics: Convolutional layers are compute-heavy but produce relatively small activations. Normalization and Activation layers (e.g., BatchNorm, ReLU) are compute-light but memory-bandwidth heavy (large activations). Strategy: Use Activation Checkpointing (recompute) for memory-heavy/compute-light layers, saving massive HBM without much compute penalty. For compute-heavy layers, asynchronously offload the activations to Host DRAM over PCIe u
napkin_math: 1 volume = 512^3 pixels = 134M pixels. A single 32-channel FP32 activation tensor = 134M * 32 * 4 bytes = 17.1GB. PCIe Gen4 x16 bandwidth = 32GB/s. Offloading one such tensor takes 17.1 / 32 = ~0.53 seconds. If we offload 5 such tensors, it takes 2.65s (bottlenecking the 1.5 iter/sec goal). By selectively discarding and recomputing ReLU/Norm (which takes <10ms to recompute but 0.5s to transfer) and only offloading the bottleneck convolutions, we stay under the 80GB HBM limit and bypass the PCIe bottleneck.

title: Edge LLM Unified Memory Sizing
scenario: Deploying a local 8B parameter coding assistant on an Apple Silicon laptop with a Unified Memory Architecture (16GB RAM, 100GB/s bandwidth). The OS and IDE constantly consume 6GB. The target is 20 tokens/sec, supporting a 32,000 token context window. Architect the precision and memory layout to prevent catastrophic SSD swapping.

realistic_solution: With Unified Memory, the CPU and GPU share the same physical RAM, but exceeding physical capacity invokes swap space (SSD), which destroys LLM inference speed. Total available RAM = 16GB - 6GB (OS) = 10GB. An 8B model in FP16 requires 16GB (OOM). The architecture must aggressively compress both weights and KV cache. Step 1: Quantize weights to 4-bit (e.g., AWQ/GGUF), reducing weight footprint to ~4.5GB. Step 2: Quantize the KV cache to 8-bit. A 32k context KV cache in FP16 takes ~4.2GB. At 8-bit, it takes ~2.1GB. Total memory footprint: 4.5GB (weights) + 2.1GB (KV) + 1GB (workspace) = 7.6GB. T
napkin_math: Available Memory = 10GB. 8B model at 4-bit = 8 billion * 0.5 bytes = 4.0GB + metadata = ~4.5GB. Memory bandwidth is 100GB/s. Generating 1 token requires reading all weights. 100GB/s / 4.5GB = 22.2 tokens/sec theoretical maximum, which perfectly aligns with the 20 tokens/sec SLA. KV Cache calculation: 32,000 tokens * 32 layers * 8 heads * 128 dim * 2 bytes (FP16) * 2 (K+V) = 4,194,304,000 bytes (~4.2GB). Quantizing to 8-bit halves this to 2.1GB, leaving a 2.4GB safety buffer for IDE spikes.

title: The FP8 Autoregressive Decoding Fallacy
scenario: Your team upgrades a Llama-3-70B inference service on 8x H100 GPUs (3.35 TB/s, 1979 TFLOPS FP8, 989 TFLOPS FP16 per GPU) from FP16 to FP8 weight-and-activation quantization. You expect a 2x latency reduction for single-user autoregressive decoding (batch size 1) because FP8 TFLOPS are double FP16 TFLOPS. However, Time Per Output Token (TPOT) only improves from 45ms to 24ms (~1.8x), while prefill latency improves by nearly 2x. Why did decoding not scale with the 2x compute increase?
common_mistake: Assuming decoding latency is compute-bound and bottlenecked by the FP8 Tensor Core throughput ceiling.
realistic_solution: Autoregressive decoding at batch size 1 is strictly memory-bandwidth bound, meaning it operates on the sloped portion of the roofline model. The doubling of the compute ceiling (TFLOPS) from FP16 to FP8 is completely irrelevant because the GPUs are starved for data. The ~1.8x speedup observed in decoding comes entirely from halving the weight memory footprint (from ~140GB to ~70GB), which halves the memory load time across the HBM bandwidth. Prefill, however, uses large matrix multiplications (Q x K^T) and is compute-bound, so it fully benefits from the doubled TFLOPS ceiling.
napkin_math: H100 Bandwidth = 3.35 TB/s per GPU x 8 = 26.8 TB/s total. FP16 weights = 140GB. Memory load time = 140GB / 26.8 TB/s = ~5.2ms per layer/operation overhead (ignoring NVLink overhead, practically ~45ms end-to-end). FP8 weights = 70GB. Memory load time = 70GB / 26.8 TB/s = ~2.6ms. Compute time for BS=1 is 140B MACs / (8 * 1979 TFLOPS) = ~0.008ms. Compute time (0.008ms) << Memory time (2.6ms).

title: The A10G vs V100 CNN Regression
scenario: A cloud vision API processes 4K images using a highly optimized MobileNetV3 (heavy on depthwise separable convolutions). To cut costs, you migrate from AWS p3.2xlarge (V100: 130 TFLOPS FP16, 900 GB/s bandwidth) to g5.2xlarge (A10G: 250 TFLOPS FP16, 600 GB/s bandwidth). Based on the 92% increase in TFLOPS, the team expects a massive speedup. Instead, end-to-end inference latency increases by exactly 50%.
common_mistake: Assuming the new PyTorch version or CUDA drivers on the A10G are unoptimized or lacking cuDNN tuning.
realistic_solution: Depthwise separable convolutions have extremely low Arithmetic Intensity (AI). For a 3x3 depthwise conv, the AI is roughly 0.5 FLOPs/Byte, placing the workload firmly on the memory-bandwidth bound sloped region of the roofline model for both GPUs. Because the workload is completely memory-bound, performance is dictated strictly by memory bandwidth, not the compute ceiling. The A10G has 33% less memory bandwidth than the V100 (600 GB/s vs 900 GB/s). Therefore, execution time scales inversely with bandwidth, resulting in a 50% regression.
napkin_math: Depthwise conv AI: 1 MAC per weight. Total ops for 1 output pixel = 9 MACs (18 FLOPs). Memory read = 9 weights + 9 input pixels. At FP16 (2 bytes), that's ~36 bytes. AI = 18 / 36 = 0.5 FLOPs/Byte. V100 ridge point = 130,000 GFLOPS / 900 GB/s = 144 FLOPs/Byte. A10G ridge point = 250,000 GFLOPS / 600 GB/s = 416 FLOPs/Byte. Since 0.5 << 144, both are deeply memory bound. V100 time ~ 1/900. A10G time ~ 1/600. Ratio = 900/600 = 1.5x slower.

title: The DLRM Vertical Fusion Trap
scenario: Optimizing a DLRM (Deep Learning Recommendation Model) on Cloud TPU v4. The model has massive sparse embedding lookups followed by a dense MLP. Profiling shows TPU compute utilization is < 15%. An engineer writes a custom XLA pass to vertically fuse the embedding lookup with the first dense MLP layer to 'eliminate intermediate HBM roundtrips'. After deployment, overall step latency doubles.
common_mistake: Believing that eliminating HBM roundtrips always improves performance regardless of the fused operators' individual arithmetic intensities.
realistic_solution: This creates a 'poisoned' execution profile. Embedding lookups are strictly memory-bandwidth bound with an Arithmetic Intensity near 0 (scatter/gather operations). Dense MLPs are heavily compute-bound matrix multiplications. Fusing them forces the TPU's Matrix Multiply Units (MXUs) to sit idle, waiting for the sparse, irregular memory gathers to return from HBM before computing each row. This artificially lowers the Arithmetic Intensity of the MLP to match the embedding. Unfused, the TPU compiler can pipeline execution: fetching embeddings for batch N+1 asynchronously while the MXU churns thro
napkin_math: Unfused: Gather takes 5ms (bandwidth bound), MLP takes 1ms (compute bound). Pipelined throughput = max(5, 1) = 5ms per step. Fused: MXU compute is stalled by memory fetches. The operations serialize at the vector level, destroying pipeline latency hiding. Total time becomes memory fetch time (5ms) + compute time (1ms) + fusion overhead = ~6ms. Throughput drops from 1/5ms to 1/6ms.

title: The RoPE Recomputation Paradox
scenario: Your team is optimizing a custom Transformer for a 128k context window on an A100 GPU (80GB). The original code reads pre-computed sine and cosine matrices for Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) from HBM, taking 15% of the forward pass time. You rewrite the Triton kernel to dynamically recompute the trigonometric functions (sin/cos) on the fly for every token. FLOPs increase by 400%, but the kernel executes 4x faster.
common_mistake: Assuming that executing trigonometric functions (which are computationally expensive) will always be slower than reading pre-computed values from RAM.
realistic_solution: The original RoPE implementation was deeply memory-bandwidth bound. Reading massive pre-computed sin/cos caches for 128k context tokens from HBM saturated the memory bus. By recomputing on the fly, you drastically increased the Arithmetic Intensity, shifting the operation from the memory-bound slope up towards the compute-bound ceiling. Because the A100 has a massive compute-to-bandwidth ratio (312 TFLOPS vs 2 TB/s), the extra trig FLOPs are computed essentially for 'free' in the shadow of the necessary memory loads for the Q and K tensors.
napkin_math: Pre-computed: For d=128, context N=128k. HBM read per token = 4 x 128 x 2 bytes (Q, K, Sin, Cos) = 1 KB. Total read = 128 MB. Time at 2 TB/s = ~64 µs. Recompute: Read only Q, K (512 bytes). Time = ~32 µs. Compute for sin/cos = ~20 FLOPs per element x 128 x 128k = 327 MFLOPs. Compute time at 312 TFLOPS = ~0.001 µs. The compute time is completely hidden by the 32 µs memory load time, resulting in a 2x-4x speedup depending on overheads.

title: The GQA Prefill Illusion
scenario: A cloud provider serves a 70B LLM. To improve throughput, they switch from Multi-Head Attention (MHA) to Grouped-Query Attention (GQA), reducing KV heads by 8x. They observe a 3x throughput increase in the decoding phase. However, for a prominent enterprise customer who sends massive 50k-token prompts and only generates 10 tokens per request, the end-to-end latency drops by a mere 2%.
common_mistake: Assuming reducing KV cache size by 8x will uniformly speed up all attention operations by reducing memory traffic.
realistic_solution: Decoding is heavily memory-bandwidth bound because the model must read the entire KV cache from HBM for every single generated token. GQA reduces this memory payload by 8x, directly speeding up decoding. Prefill (prompt processing), however, is compute-bound. It performs a dense matrix multiplication of Q x K^T. Even though the K matrix is smaller in GQA, the number of FLOPs required to compute the attention scores (N x N) remains dominated by the sequence length N and is bounded by the GPU's compute ceiling. Reducing memory traffic yields negligible latency benefits when the operation is bott
napkin_math: Prefill attention FLOPs for N=50k, d=4096: 2 x 50k x 50k x 4096 = 20.4 TFLOPs. On H100 (989 TFLOPS FP16), compute time = ~20ms. Memory to load MHA K, V for 50k tokens = 50k x 4096 x 2 x 2 = ~800 MB. Loading 800 MB at 3.35 TB/s takes ~0.24 ms. GQA reduces the 0.24 ms to 0.03 ms. The operation is entirely dominated by the 20ms compute time, so saving 0.21 ms of memory transfer yields a ~1% speedup.

title: The CSR Sparsity Performance Collapse
scenario: You deploy a pruned BERT-Large model on A100 GPUs (312 TFLOPS Tensor Core, 19 TFLOPS CUDA Core, 2 TB/s bandwidth). The weights are pruned to 70% unstructured sparsity. To 'skip the zeros', a junior engineer replaces cuBLAS dense matrix multiplications with a Sparse Matrix-Vector (SpMM) kernel using Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) format. Instead of a speedup, latency increases by 15x compared to the dense model.
common_mistake: Assuming that doing 70% fewer FLOPs automatically translates to faster execution, regardless of the hardware execution units or memory access patterns.
realistic_solution: Two catastrophic roofline shifts occur. First, dense matrix multiplications on A100 utilize highly optimized Tensor Cores (312 TFLOPS). CSR SpMM kernels map poorly to Tensor Cores and typically fall back to standard CUDA cores, dropping the theoretical compute ceiling to ~19 TFLOPS. Second, CSR introduces irregular memory access patterns (fetching row pointers and column indices), drastically lowering Arithmetic Intensity. The workload shifts from a compute-bound operation on a high ceiling (Tensor Cores) to a memory-bound operation on a low ceiling (CUDA cores with irregular gathers).
napkin_math: Dense BERT layer (4k x 4k): 32 MFLOPs. Dense AI = 4000 / 2 = 2000 FLOPs/B. A100 Tensor Core Ridge Point = 312,000 / 2000 = 156 FLOPs/B. (Compute bound). Dense time = 32M / 312T = 0.1 µs. Sparse CSR (70% sparse): FLOPs = 9.6 MFLOPs. Memory to load CSR structure (values + indices) ~ 15 MB. AI drops to < 1 FLOP/B. Sparse time is bounded by bandwidth: 15 MB / 2 TB/s = 7.5 µs. 7.5 µs is 75x slower than 0.1 µs.

title: The Over-Batched T4 Throughput Plateau
scenario: A cloud service generates embeddings using a BERT-base model on T4 GPUs (300 GB/s bandwidth, 65 TFLOPS FP16). To maximize throughput, the team increases the dynamic batch size from 32 to 256. Throughput (requests/sec) scales linearly up to batch 32. However, from batch 32 to 256, throughput flatlines completely at ~4,000 req/sec, while P99 latency spikes drastically from 10ms to 80ms.
common_mistake: Assuming that increasing batch size will always increase hardware utilization and throughput by hiding memory latency.
realistic_solution: The workload has crossed the roofline inflection point (the ridge point). At small batch sizes, the workload is memory-bandwidth bound. Increasing batch size increases Arithmetic Intensity (reusing the same loaded weights across multiple inputs), moving the workload up the sloped roofline and increasing throughput. Around batch size 32, the Arithmetic Intensity hits the ridge point of the T4 GPU, and the workload becomes strictly compute-bound. Further increasing the batch size to 256 provides no additional throughput because the GPU is already operating at its maximum 65 TFLOPS ceiling. It me
napkin_math: T4 Compute Ceiling = 65 TFLOPS. At 4,000 req/sec, FLOPs per request = 65 TFLOPS / 4000 = 16.25 GFLOPs. At BS=32, total compute = 32 x 16.25 GFLOPs = 520 GFLOPs. Compute time = 520 GFLOPs / 65 TFLOPS = 8ms. Memory load (220MB weights) = 220MB / 300 GB/s = 0.73ms. Since 8ms > 0.73ms, BS=32 is compute-bound. At BS=256, compute time = 256 x 16.25 / 65T = 64ms. Throughput remains (256 req / 0.064 sec) = 4,000 req/sec, but latency degrades from 8ms to 64ms.

title: LLM Decoding Bandwidth Wall
scenario: A team is deploying a 30B parameter LLM (FP16) on a single A100 80GB GPU (1.5 TB/s HBM bandwidth, 312 TFLOPS FP16). They want to achieve a strict SLA of 100 tokens/sec at batch size 1. They calculate that 30B parameters require roughly 60 GFLOPs per token. 60 GFLOPs * 100 tokens = 6 TFLOPS. Since the A100 has 312 TFLOPS, they assume this SLA is trivial and guarantee it to the client. In production, they only achieve roughly 25 tokens/sec.
common_mistake: Confusing peak TFLOPS with attainable throughput, completely ignoring that decoding is severely memory-bandwidth bound.
realistic_solution: The team failed to analyze the workload using the roofline model. The arithmetic intensity of generating a single token (batch size 1) is roughly 2 FLOPs per byte of weights loaded (60 GFLOPs / 30 GB = 2 FLOPs/Byte). The A100 roofline ridge point is 312 TFLOPS / 1.5 TB/s = 208 FLOPs/Byte. At 2 FLOPs/Byte, the workload is extremely deep in the memory-bound regime. The attainable compute is dictated entirely by memory bandwidth, not the ALUs. To fix this, they must either use a GPU with higher memory bandwidth, quantize the model to reduce bytes read, or utilize speculative decoding.
napkin_math: Weights size = 30B * 2 bytes = 60 GB. Max read rate from HBM = 1.5 TB/s = 1500 GB/s. Max theoretical tokens/sec = 1500 GB/s / 60 GB/token = 25 tokens/sec. Time per token = 40ms. Attainable TFLOPS = 1.5 TB/s * 2 FLOPs/byte = 3 TFLOPS (only ~1% of peak compute!).

title: The Long-Context Attention Bottleneck
scenario: You are serving a 13B LLM with a 64k token context window on an A100 (1.5 TB/s HBM, 312 TFLOPS). Profiling the prefill phase shows the standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA) layers take 800ms per request, with ALU utilization at a dismal 12%. The tech lead suggests upgrading to an H100 (3.35 TB/s, 989 TFLOPS) to get a 3x speedup on the prefill phase.
common_mistake: Assuming the H100's 3x compute will proportionally speed up a workload that is actually memory-capacity and bandwidth bound.
realistic_solution: Standard MHA materializes the $N \times N$ attention scores in HBM. For N=64k, this causes massive HBM read/write traffic, dragging the arithmetic intensity far below the roofline ridge point. Upgrading to H100 only provides a ~2.2x bandwidth increase, so you won't get a 3x speedup, and you are wasting expensive ALUs. Instead of new hardware, the architecture needs FlashAttention, which tiles the computation in SRAM, eliminating the $O(N^2)$ HBM accesses and shifting the workload back to the compute-bound regime.
napkin_math: Standard MHA HBM traffic per layer: $O(N^2)$. At 64k, QK^T matrix is $64000 \times 64000 \times 2$ bytes $\approx 8$ GB per attention head. With FlashAttention, HBM traffic drops to $O(N \times d)$. Arithmetic Intensity increases from $\approx 1$ FLOP/byte to $>200$ FLOPs/byte, moving the workload safely into the compute-bound region.

title: The Quantization Throughput Paradox
scenario: A Deep Learning Recommendation Model (DLRM) processes 5,000 QPS on an NVIDIA L4 GPU (300 GB/s bandwidth, 120 TOPS INT8, 60 TFLOPS FP16). The model has 50GB of embedding tables and small MLP layers (20 GFLOPs total compute per pass). To reach a target of 10,000 QPS, the team quantizes the MLP layers from FP16 to INT8, doubling peak compute. However, throughput remains flat at 5,100 QPS.
common_mistake: Believing that quantizing compute-heavy layers will speed up a model that is fundamentally bottlenecked by memory bandwidth.
realistic_solution: Recommendation models are predominantly memory-bandwidth bound due to sparse embedding lookups. The arithmetic intensity of reading an embedding vector to perform a simple sum/concat is incredibly low (often < 0.5 FLOPs/byte). The L4 ridge point for FP16 is 200 FLOPs/byte. The model is vastly below the ridge point. Quantizing the MLPs to INT8 raises the compute ceiling (and the ridge point to 400 FLOPs/byte), but does absolutely nothing to alleviate the HBM bandwidth ceiling that dictates the total pipeline latency. Optimization must focus on embedding compression or higher bandwidth memory.
napkin_math: L4 FP16 Ridge Point = 60 TFLOPS / 300 GB/s = 200 FLOPs/Byte. DLRM AI = 20 GFLOPs / 50 GB read = 0.4 FLOPs/Byte. Attainable throughput = 300 GB/s * 0.4 = 120 GFLOPs/s. Increasing the compute ceiling to 120 TOPS does not change the 120 GFLOPs/s attainable throughput, hence QPS remains flat.

title: Graph Neural Network Hardware Selection
scenario: You are architecting inference for a 50-layer GraphSAGE fraud detection model. You must choose between an A100 GPU (312 TFLOPS, 2.0 TB/s HBM) and a TPU v4 (275 TFLOPS, 1.2 TB/s HBM). The model requires 100 TFLOPs per batch. A financial analyst recommends the TPU v4 because it costs 30% less per hour and has comparable TFLOPS, projecting identical latency.
common_mistake: Comparing hardware based on peak TFLOPS per dollar for a workload that will never reach peak TFLOPS due to extremely low arithmetic intensity.
realistic_solution: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) spend the majority of their time in the message-passing phase, which involves sparse scatter/gather memory access patterns over graph structures. This results in incredibly low arithmetic intensity (often 2-5 FLOPs/byte). Therefore, GNNs are strictly memory-bandwidth bound. Evaluating the hardware requires looking at the memory bandwidth, not the peak compute. The A100 provides 66% more memory bandwidth, meaning it will execute the GNN significantly faster, easily offsetting the 30% cost difference.
napkin_math: Assume GNN AI = 4 FLOPs/Byte. TPU v4 Attainable Compute = 1.2 TB/s * 4 FLOPs/Byte = 4.8 TFLOPS (1.7% util). A100 Attainable Compute = 2.0 TB/s * 4 FLOPs/Byte = 8.0 TFLOPS (2.5% util). The A100 yields 8.0/4.8 = 1.66x the throughput of the TPU v4.

title: Continuous Batching Arithmetic Shift
scenario: An API serves a 15B parameter LLM (FP16, 30GB weights) on an L40S GPU (864 GB/s HBM, 362 TFLOPS). With a static batch size of 2, decoding latency is 35ms per token. They upgrade to continuous batching, increasing average batch size to 16. A PM is concerned that processing 16 tokens simultaneously will increase the per-token generation latency to roughly 280ms (8x longer), violating SLA.
common_mistake: Assuming latency scales linearly with batch size, fundamentally misunderstanding that low-batch LLM decoding is memory-bound.
realistic_solution: At batch size 2, the arithmetic intensity is extremely low, and the L40S is entirely bottlenecked by the time it takes to read the 30GB of weights from HBM. The massive 362 TFLOPS ALUs are mostly idle. By increasing the batch size to 16, the arithmetic intensity increases 8x. Because the workload remains in the memory-bound region of the roofline (below the ridge point), the time to read the weights stays exactly the same, but the ALUs process 16 tokens in that same window. Per-token latency remains nearly identical (~35ms), while system throughput increases 8x.
napkin_math: L40S Ridge Point = 362 TFLOPS / 864 GB/s = 418 FLOPs/Byte. BS=2 AI = (2 tokens * 30 GFLOPs) / 30 GB = 2 FLOPs/Byte. BS=16 AI = (16 * 30) / 30 = 16 FLOPs/Byte. Both AI values are ≪ 418, so attainable time is purely bounded by memory read: 30 GB / 864 GB/s = 34.7 ms. Latency remains ~35ms.

title: Tensor vs Pipeline Parallelism Roofline
scenario: Deploying a 70B parameter LLM (140GB weights) on an 8-GPU node (each GPU: 80GB HBM, 2 TB/s bandwidth). At batch size 1, you must choose between Pipeline Parallelism (PP=8) and Tensor Parallelism (TP=8). A system engineer argues for PP=8 because it requires zero intra-layer communication, saving NVLink bandwidth and lowering latency.
common_mistake: Prioritizing small communication savings over the massive memory-bandwidth aggregation benefits of Tensor Parallelism for memory-bound workloads.
realistic_solution: LLM decoding at batch size 1 is entirely memory-bandwidth bound. In PP=8, each token must sequentially traverse 8 GPUs. Each GPU loads its 17.5GB weight shard at 2 TB/s, taking ~8.75ms. Total sequential latency is $8 \times 8.75 = 70$ms per token. In TP=8, the matrix multiplications are split. All 8 GPUs load their 17.5GB shards *simultaneously*. The aggregate memory bandwidth becomes 16 TB/s. The weight loading takes 8.75ms total. Even with an AllReduce communication overhead of ~1ms per layer, TP=8 delivers drastically lower latency because it aggregates memory bandwidth.
napkin_math: PP=8 Latency = \sum (Shard Size / Single GPU BW) = 8 * (17.5 GB / 2 TB/s) = 70 ms. TP=8 Latency = (Total Size / Aggregate BW) + Comm = (140 GB / 16 TB/s) + 1 ms = 8.75 + 1 = 9.75 ms. TP=8 is roughly 7x faster for latency.

title: The MoE Memory Bandwidth Tax
scenario: A team replaces a 40B dense model with an 8x40B MoE model (Top-1 routing, 40B active parameters per token). Both models require ~80 GFLOPs per token. They are deployed on an 8x H100 cluster (3.35 TB/s per GPU). At batch size 128, the dense model achieves 500 tokens/sec. The team expects the MoE to match this since the active compute is identical. Instead, MoE throughput drops to roughly 120 tokens/sec.
common_mistake: Assuming that equal active FLOPs implies equal throughput, ignoring the severe arithmetic intensity drop caused by loading multiple experts.
realistic_solution: Throughput at batch size 128 is determined by the roofline model. The dense 40B model loads 80GB of weights, yielding a high arithmetic intensity. The MoE model has 320B total parameters (640GB). At BS=128, tokens are distributed across all 8 experts, meaning the GPUs must load almost the entire 640GB of weights from HBM to process the batch. The arithmetic intensity plummets, shifting the workload deep into the memory-bound region and drastically reducing attainable throughput.
napkin_math: H100 Ridge Point = 989 TFLOPS / 3.35 TB/s = 295 FLOPs/Byte. Dense AI = (128 tokens * 80 GFLOPs) / 80 GB = 128 FLOPs/Byte. MoE AI (assuming all experts loaded) = (128 tokens * 80 GFLOPs) / 640 GB = 16 FLOPs/Byte. Dense Attainable = 3.35 TB/s * 128 = 428 TFLOPS. MoE Attainable = 3.35 TB/s * 16 = 53 TFLOPS. The MoE is processing at 1/8th the compute efficiency.

title: The Operator Fusion Trap
scenario: You are optimizing a custom inference chip (32 GB/s HBM, 16 TFLOPS). You fuse a sequence of 4 element-wise operations (Add, Mul, ReLU, Add) operating on a 100M element FP32 tensor. Unfused latency was 100ms. You expected fusion to drop latency to 12.5ms by eliminating HBM roundtrips. Instead, the fused kernel takes 150ms. Profiling shows ALU utilization is < 5%.
common_mistake: Assuming operator fusion always strictly reduces memory traffic without side effects like register pressure.
realistic_solution: The failure is caused by catastrophic register spilling. While operator fusion theoretically increases arithmetic intensity by keeping intermediate values in registers, fusing too many operations (or operations with high variable counts) exceeds the physical register file size per thread block. When registers are exhausted, the compiler spills variables to local memory, which is physically backed by the slow HBM. Because these spills are often uncoalesced, the effective memory bandwidth drops significantly below the theoretical maximum. The workload, already possessing low arithmetic intensity
napkin_math:
Unfused: 100M * 4 bytes = 400MB per read/write. 4 ops = 4 reads, 4 writes. Total traffic = 3.2GB. 3.2GB / 32 GB/s = 100ms.
Fused (Expected): 1 read, 1 write. Total traffic = 800MB. 800MB / 32 GB/s = 25ms.
Fused (Actual): Spilling causes ~12 uncoalesced reads/writes per element. Traffic = 4.8GB. Uncoalesced access drops effective bandwidth to ~32 GB/s. 4.8GB / 32 GB/s = 150ms.

title: Depthwise Separable Disappointment
scenario: Your mobile vision team replaced standard 3x3 convolutions with depthwise separable convolutions in a custom ResNet, reducing theoretical compute from 2.5 GMACs to 0.3 GMACs per frame. Deployed on an edge NPU (4 TOPS, 20 GB/s bandwidth), latency surprisingly degraded from 1.25ms to 2.5ms. The memory footprint (5MB of activations/weights) remained identical.
common_mistake: Calculating latency using only theoretical MACs and peak TOPS, ignoring the Roofline memory wall.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a massive drop in arithmetic intensity, shifting the workload from compute-bound to severely memory-bound. Standard convolutions heavily reuse weights and activations, resulting in high arithmetic intensity that allows the NPU to approach its 4 TOPS ceiling. Depthwise convolutions, however, only apply one filter per input channel, resulting in an arithmetic intensity of roughly 1 MAC/byte. On the Roofline model, the NPU's ridge point is 200 MACs/byte (4 TOPS / 20 GB/s). Depthwise convolutions operate far to the left of the ridge point. The NPU is starved for data, and the ove
napkin_math:
NPU Ridge Point: 4 TOPS / 20 GB/s = 200 MACs/byte.
Standard Conv: 2.5 GMACs / 5MB = 500 MACs/byte. (Compute bound: 2.5 GMACs / 4 TOPS = 0.625ms. Actual 1.25ms implies ~50% ALU utilization).
Depthwise Conv: 0.3 GMACs / 5MB = 60 MACs/byte. (Memory bound: 5MB / 20 GB/s = 0.25ms theoretical). The degradation to 2.5ms means effective bandwidth collapsed to 2 GB/s due to poor NPU DMA utilization with depthwise access patterns.

title: The H100 Speedup Discrepancy
scenario: You upgraded an LLM serving cluster from A100 (312 TFLOPS, 2 TB/s) to H100 (989 TFLOPS, 3.3 TB/s). For a 70B parameter model at batch size 1, the prefill phase latency (2048 tokens) dropped from 916ms to 295ms (3.1x speedup). However, decode latency per token only dropped from 70ms to 42ms (1.6x speedup). The team suspects a PyTorch bug.
common_mistake: Assuming a 3x TFLOPS upgrade will uniformly speed up all phases of LLM generation.
realistic_solution: There is no bug; this is a textbook demonstration of the Roofline model. LLM generation consists of two distinct phases with radically different arithmetic intensities. The prefill phase performs a matrix-matrix multiplication (weights * sequence of tokens), resulting in high arithmetic intensity. It sits on the flat, compute-bound ceiling of the Roofline. Therefore, prefill scales perfectly with the 3.1x increase in TFLOPS (989/312). The decode phase, however, performs a matrix-vector multiplication (weights * 1 token), which has an arithmetic intensity of ~1 FLOP/byte. It sits entirely on th
napkin_math:
Prefill (Compute Bound): A100 Compute = 286 TFLOPs / 312 TFLOPS = 916ms. H100 Compute = 286 TFLOPs / 989 TFLOPS = 289ms (~3.1x).
Decode (Memory Bound): 70B FP16 weights = 140GB. A100 Memory Time = 140GB / 2.0 TB/s = 70ms. H100 Memory Time = 140GB / 3.3 TB/s = 42ms (~1.65x).

title: The FlashAttention Paradox
scenario: You serve a Vision Transformer on an A100 (40MB L2 cache, 2TB/s HBM). The sequence length is 196 (14x14 patches), with embedding dim 1024. Standard PyTorch attention takes 0.8ms. You upgrade to FlashAttention-2, expecting a 2x speedup by eliminating HBM read/writes. Instead, latency increases to 1.2ms.
common_mistake: Assuming FlashAttention is always faster because it eliminates HBM reads/writes, regardless of sequence length.
realistic_solution: FlashAttention optimizes the memory roofline by tiling the attention matrix to avoid writing the O(N^2) intermediate attention matrix to HBM. It trades memory bandwidth for extra compute (recomputation in backward, and extra ALU instructions for tiling/scaling in forward). However, for very short sequence lengths like 196, the attention matrix is extremely small. It fits entirely within the A100's massive 40MB L2 cache. Therefore, standard attention is not HBM-bandwidth bound; it runs at L2 cache bandwidth speeds (~5-10 TB/s). Because the memory bottleneck FlashAttention solves doesn't exist f
napkin_math:
Attention matrix size = 196 * 196 * 4 bytes (FP32) = 153.6 KB. 
153.6 KB << 40 MB (A100 L2 Cache). 
Standard Attention HBM traffic = 0. FlashAttention extra FLOPs overhead = ~20%. 0.8ms * 1.2 = ~0.96ms compute + tiling overhead = 1.2ms.

title: The Power-Clock Anomaly
scenario: To save datacenter power, you underclock the GPU cores of an A100 cluster running Whisper (audio transcription) at batch size 1. You drop the core clock by 40% (1410 MHz -> 846 MHz), reducing peak TFLOPS by 40%. You expect a 40% drop in throughput. Surprisingly, throughput only drops by 1%, while GPU power consumption drops by 35%.
common_mistake: Assuming workload throughput scales linearly with core clock frequency (compute capacity) for all deep learning tasks.
realistic_solution: The Whisper workload at batch size 1 has an extremely low arithmetic intensity (dominated by memory fetches for autoregressive decoding), placing it far to the left of the Roofline ridge point. It is entirely memory-bandwidth bound. The GPU ALUs spend the vast majority of their time idle, waiting for data from HBM. Lowering the core clock reduces the horizontal compute ceiling of the Roofline model but leaves the sloped memory bandwidth ceiling unchanged. Because the workload's operating point is on the slope, dropping the compute ceiling has almost zero effect on performance. However, power c
napkin_math:
A100 Ridge Point = 312 TFLOPS / 2 TB/s = 156 FLOPs/Byte.
Whisper BS=1 Decode Arithmetic Intensity ≈ 2 FLOPs/Byte.
2 << 156. The workload is completely memory-bound.
Underclocked Ridge Point = (312 * 0.6) / 2 TB/s = 93 FLOPs/Byte.
2 << 93. The workload remains completely memory-bound, so throughput is unaffected.

title: Mobile NPU Utilization Collapse
scenario: Deploying a 4K Super-Resolution model on a mobile SoC (NPU: 10 TOPS, 30 GB/s main memory bandwidth, 2MB NPU SRAM). The model requires 100 GMACs per frame. Based on 10 TOPS, expected latency is 10ms (100 FPS). Actual latency is 333ms (3 FPS). Profiling shows NPU ALU utilization is below 3%.
common_mistake: Ignoring intermediate activation sizes and assuming theoretical peak NPU TOPS can be reached regardless of SRAM capacity.
realistic_solution: The root cause is aggressive SRAM spilling to main memory. A 4K feature map with even a modest number of channels is massive. Because the intermediate activations far exceed the NPU's 2MB SRAM, every layer must write its output to main memory and the next layer must read it back. This drops the arithmetic intensity of the model drastically. The workload hits the bandwidth roofline ceiling of the mobile SoC. The NPU ALUs process the data almost instantly but then sit idle waiting hundreds of milliseconds for the memory controller to fetch the next layer's inputs from LPDDR.
napkin_math:
4K Image (3840x2160) = 8.3M pixels.
Activations (e.g., 64 channels, FP16) = 8.3M * 64 * 2 bytes = 1.06 GB per layer.
1.06 GB >> 2MB SRAM.
For a 10-layer model, memory traffic = 10 * 1.06 GB (read) + 10 * 1.06 GB (write) ≈ 20 GB.
Time spent on memory = 20 GB / 30 GB/s = 666ms (or 333ms if fused partially). Compute time = 10ms. Total time is dominated by memory IO.

title: The INT4 Quantization Plateau
scenario: Serving Llama-2 13B on an RTX 4090 (1 TB/s bandwidth). FP16 decode latency is 26ms/token. You apply INT8 weight-only quantization, and latency drops to 13ms/token. Next, you apply INT4 weight-only quantization, expecting latency to drop to 6.5ms. Instead, latency plateaus at 12ms/token.
common_mistake: Assuming memory bandwidth scales linearly with precision reduction without accounting for ALU unpacking overhead or hardware fetch limits.
realistic_solution: The workload experienced a bottleneck shift on the Roofline model. At FP16 and INT8, the decode phase was strictly memory-bandwidth bound, so halving the weight size halved the latency. However, standard GPUs do not have native 4-bit memory load instructions; they load 32-bit or 8-bit registers. INT4 weights must be explicitly unpacked and dequantized into FP16 using bitwise shifts and masks on the ALUs before the matrix multiplication can occur. This decompression requires significant compute instructions per byte. At INT4, the compute required for unpacking exceeds the time saved by fetching
napkin_math:
FP16: 26GB / 1 TB/s = 26ms.
INT8: 13GB / 1 TB/s = 13ms.
INT4: 6.5GB / 1 TB/s = 6.5ms (Memory theoretical).
BUT, Unpacking INT4 requires ~5 instructions per weight. 13B weights = 65 G-Ops. Due to dependency chains and register pressure in the unpacking kernel, effective ALU utilization is low, taking ~12ms. max(6.5ms, 12ms) = 12ms.

title: The KV Cache Roofline Collapse
scenario: Serving an LLM on an A100 (40MB L2 cache, 2TB/s HBM). At batch size 16 (Sequence Length = 1024), throughput is 500 tokens/s. To maximize throughput, you increase batch size to 128. You expect throughput to scale linearly as arithmetic intensity increases. Instead, throughput drops to 300 tokens/s.
common_mistake: Assuming increasing batch size strictly increases arithmetic intensity and throughput without encountering architectural memory cliffs.
realistic_solution: The system hit an L2 cache capacity cliff, causing the effective memory bandwidth roofline to collapse. At smaller batch sizes, the entire KV cache for the batch fits within the A100's massive 40MB L2 cache. The attention mechanism can read the KV cache at L2 bandwidth speeds (~5 TB/s). When the batch size is increased to 128, the KV cache size exceeds the L2 capacity. The KV cache spills into HBM. Because attention is a memory-bound operation, forcing it to fetch from HBM (2 TB/s) instead of L2 drops the effective bandwidth by over 2.5x. The roofline ceiling shifts downward abruptly, throttli
napkin_math:
KV Cache per token = 2MB (assuming specific hidden dim/layers).
BS=16, Seq=1024: 16 * 1024 * 2KB = 32MB. 32MB < 40MB (Fits in L2 @ 5 TB/s).
BS=128, Seq=1024: 128 * 1024 * 2KB = 256MB. 256MB >> 40MB (Spills to HBM @ 2 TB/s).
Bandwidth drops by 2.5x. Arithmetic intensity increase from batching weights doesn't offset the massive latency penalty of reading 256MB from HBM for every generation step.

title: MobileNet vs VGG NPU Bottleneck
scenario: You are porting an image classifier to an Edge NPU with 2 TOPS (INT8) peak compute and 4 GB/s memory bandwidth. A custom VGG-like model has 8 GOPS and requires 12MB of memory traffic per inference. MobileNetV2 has only 0.6 GOPS but requires 30MB of memory traffic due to unfused depthwise layers. Surprisingly, MobileNetV2 yields 133 FPS, while the larger VGG model yields 250 FPS. Optimize the model choice to achieve a strict 200+ FPS requirement.
