common_mistake: Assuming fewer MACs always means faster inference, and trying to prune MobileNet further to improve FPS.
realistic_solution: The NPU performance is strictly governed by the Roofline model. The NPU's ridge point (the Arithmetic Intensity where compute-bound meets memory-bound) is 500 OPS/Byte. MobileNetV2 relies heavily on depthwise separable convolutions which lack data reuse, giving it a very low arithmetic intensity that makes it severely memory-bound. VGG uses dense convolutions with high data reuse, pushing its AI well above the ridge point, thus achieving near-peak TOPS. To get 200+ FPS, you must choose the VGG model, or heavily optimize MobileNet's memory bandwidth by fusing depthwise and pointwise layers to s
napkin_math:
NPU Ridge Point: (2 * 10^12 OPS) / (4 * 10^9 B/s) = 500 OPS/B.
MobileNet AI: 0.6 GOPS / 30MB = 20 OPS/B (Memory Bound). Inference time = 30MB / 4 GB/s = 7.5ms (133 FPS).
VGG AI: 8 GOPS / 12MB = 666 OPS/B (Compute Bound). Inference time = 8 GOPS / 2 TOPS = 4.0ms (250 FPS).
VGG easily meets the 200 FPS (5ms) requirement.

title: Drone Power-Precision Tradeoff
scenario: A battery-powered drone runs obstacle avoidance at 30 FPS. The model requires 10 GOPS/frame. The SoC has an FP16 GPU (2 TFLOPS peak, drawing 4W) and an INT8 NPU (8 TOPS peak, drawing 1W). Quantizing the model to INT8 increases the required compute to 12 GOPS due to quantization-aware scaling layers, and drops accuracy by 1%. The product manager suggests using the GPU to avoid the 20% compute penalty and accuracy drop. Evaluate the impact on flight time to justify your architectural choice.
common_mistake: Focusing solely on the 20% increase in GOPS and assuming the INT8 model will draw more total energy because it requires more operations.
realistic_solution: Although the INT8 model requires 20% more operations (12 GOPS vs 10 GOPS), the NPU's power efficiency (TOPS/Watt) is vastly superior to the GPU. We must calculate the energy consumed per frame for both accelerators to determine the battery drain. The GPU consumes 20mJ per frame, while the NPU consumes only 1.5mJ per frame. Despite the compute overhead from quantization scaling layers, the INT8 NPU is over 13x more energy-efficient per frame. For a battery-constrained edge device, the NPU is the only viable choice to maximize flight time.
napkin_math:
GPU efficiency: 2 TFLOPS / 4W = 500 GFLOPS/W. 
GPU Frame Energy: 10 GOPS / 500 GFLOPS/W = 0.02 Joules = 20 mJ.
NPU efficiency: 8 TOPS / 1W = 8000 GOPS/W. 
NPU Frame Energy: 12 GOPS / 8000 GOPS/W = 0.0015 Joules = 1.5 mJ.
At 30 FPS, GPU draws 0.6W average, NPU draws 0.045W average. The NPU saves 0.555W continuously.

title: LLM KV Cache Bandwidth Ceiling
scenario: You are deploying a 2B parameter LLM (INT4, 1GB weights) on an edge device with a 50 TOPS NPU and 25 GB/s memory bandwidth. During the prefill phase (prompt length 1024), you achieve 200 tokens/s. During decoding, token generation drops abruptly to 22 tokens/s. The KV cache size averages 128MB during decoding. Diagnose the bottleneck and propose an optimization strategy.
common_mistake: Assuming the NPU compute units are overheating or underutilized due to software bugs, and trying to optimize the matrix multiplication kernels.
realistic_solution: The decoding phase of an LLM is heavily memory-bound because it operates with an effective batch size of 1. For every single generated token, the entire 1GB of model weights must be streamed from DRAM, plus the KV cache. The theoretical maximum decoding speed is dictated entirely by memory bandwidth, not compute TOPS. The roofline ceiling is strictly enforced by the 25 GB/s bus. To optimize decoding on the edge, you must reduce memory traffic. Techniques include Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) to shrink the KV cache, or Speculative Decoding, which evaluates multiple tokens per weight load, artif
napkin_math:
Decoding 1 token requires loading 1GB (weights) + 0.128GB (KV cache) = 1.128GB.
Max memory-bound throughput = 25 GB/s / 1.128 GB/token = 22.16 tokens/s.
Compute required per token: ~4 GOPS. Compute ceiling = 50 TOPS / 4 GOPS = 12,500 tokens/s.
The system is operating exactly at its theoretical memory bandwidth limit (22 tokens/s).

title: Operator Fusion for Arithmetic Intensity
scenario: An edge TPU runs a custom Transformer encoder for anomaly detection. SLA requires 500 inferences/sec (QPS). The NPU has 2 TOPS compute and 4 GB/s memory bandwidth. The model requires 4 GOPS per inference. Currently, the system maxes out at 200 QPS. Profiling shows unfused MatMul, LayerNorm, and GELU operations are causing 20MB of intermediate activation read/writes to DRAM per inference. Design an optimization to meet the SLA.
common_mistake: Attempting to prune the MatMul weights to reduce the 4 GOPS, assuming the model is too large for the 2 TOPS NPU.
realistic_solution: The low QPS is a classic symptom of a memory-bound workload on the Roofline model. Un-fused operations force the accelerator to write intermediate activations back to DRAM and read them again for the next layer, destroying Arithmetic Intensity. At 20MB per inference, the memory bandwidth limits the system to 200 QPS. By fusing the MatMul, LayerNorm, and GELU into a single kernel, the intermediate results stay in the NPU's SRAM (scratchpad). This drastically reduces DRAM traffic, shifting the workload to the right on the Roofline plot towards the compute-bound regime. Reducing traffic to 5MB pe
napkin_math:
Current memory time = 20MB / 4 GB/s = 5ms (max 200 QPS).
Compute time = 4 GOPS / 2 TOPS = 2ms (max 500 QPS).
Ridge point = 2 TOPS / 4 GB/s = 500 OPS/Byte.
Current AI = 4 GOPS / 20MB = 200 OPS/Byte (Memory Bound).
To hit 500 QPS, we need memory time <= 2ms, meaning max traffic = 2ms * 4 GB/s = 8MB. Fusing operators removes the 15MB of intermediate read/writes, reducing traffic to 5MB. New memory time = 1.25ms. Workload becomes compute-bound at 2ms (500 QPS).

title: Quantization Roofline Shift
scenario: Optimizing a dense neural network on an edge DSP. DSP specs: FP32 peak is 64 GFLOPS, INT8 peak is 64 GOPS. Memory bandwidth is 12.8 GB/s for both data types. The FP32 model requires 100 MOPS per inference and loads 50MB of weights/activations. You quantize the model to INT8 (data footprint becomes 12.5MB, operations remain 100 MOPS). Calculate the inference time before and after quantization, and identify how the bottleneck shifts.
common_mistake: Assuming that since peak compute (64 GOPS) and total operations (100 MOPS) remain the same, quantization will not improve inference time if the device was compute-bound.
realistic_solution: Quantization affects both the Y-axis (peak compute) and X-axis (Arithmetic Intensity) of the Roofline model. In FP32, the workload has a very low Arithmetic Intensity and is heavily memory-bound, resulting in high latency due to the 50MB data transfer. Quantizing to INT8 reduces the data transfer by 4x. Because the operation count stays the same, the Arithmetic Intensity increases by 4x. This shift pushes the workload past the ridge point on the Roofline plot, changing it from a memory-bound workload to a compute-bound workload, drastically reducing inference time.
napkin_math:
Ridge Point (both FP32/INT8) = 64 GOPS / 12.8 GB/s = 5 OPS/B.
FP32 Model AI = 100 MOPS / 50 MB = 2 OPS/B (Memory Bound). 
FP32 Mem Time = 50MB / 12.8 GB/s = 3.9ms.
INT8 Model AI = 100 MOPS / 12.5 MB = 8 OPS/B (Compute Bound). 
INT8 Compute Time = 100 MOPS / 64 GOPS = 1.56ms.
Inference time drops from 3.9ms to 1.56ms, and the bottleneck shifts from memory bandwidth to compute.

title: Hardware-Aware Batching for Edge NLP
scenario: A smart home hub processes 4 concurrent audio streams using an NLP intent model. The model requires 10 GOPS, 20MB of weights, and 1MB of activations per stream. The NPU has 5 TOPS compute and 5 GB/s memory bandwidth. You can execute them sequentially (Batch=1, 4 times) or batch them together (Batch=4, 1 time). The latency SLA is 15ms total for all streams to finish. Evaluate both strategies using the roofline model to determine which meets the SLA.
common_mistake: Calculating compute time only (40 GOPS / 5 TOPS = 0.008ms) and assuming both batching strategies will easily meet the 15ms SLA.
realistic_solution: Batching fundamentally alters Arithmetic Intensity by reusing loaded weights across multiple inputs. For Batch=1, the 20MB weights are loaded 4 separate times from DRAM, making the workload highly memory-bound and exceeding the SLA due to cumulative memory transfer latency. For Batch=4, the 20MB weights are loaded only once, shifting the workload's Arithmetic Intensity significantly higher. The total execution time for Batch=4 is dictated by a single memory load, which easily meets the 15ms SLA for all streams.
napkin_math:
Batch=1 (Sequential): Data = 20MB (Weights) + 1MB (Acts) = 21MB. 
Mem time = 21MB / 5 GB/s = 4.2ms per stream. Compute time = 10 GOPS / 5 TOPS = 0.002ms. 
Total time for 4 streams = 4 * max(4.2ms, 0.002ms) = 16.8ms (Fails 15ms SLA).
Batch=4 (Batched): Data = 20MB (Weights) + 4 * 1MB (Acts) = 24MB. 
Mem time = 24MB / 5 GB/s = 4.8ms. Compute time = 40 GOPS / 5 TOPS = 0.008ms. 
Total time = max(4.8ms, 0.008ms) = 4.8ms (Passes 15ms SLA).

title: Shared Bus Bottleneck in SoC Pipelines
scenario: An edge vision system targets 30 FPS. The pipeline runs concurrently: 1) CPU decodes frame (demands 2 GB/s BW), 2) NPU runs Object Detection (demands 5 GB/s BW), 3) GPU runs depth estimation (demands 15 GB/s BW). The CPU, NPU, and GPU share a unified LPDDR4 memory bus with a peak bandwidth of 17 GB/s. The system is currently dropping frames. A junior engineer notes the NPU is at 99% compute utilization and suggests upgrading to an NPU with 2x the TOPS. Evaluate this proposal.
common_mistake: Agreeing to upgrade the NPU because 99% utilization typically implies a compute-bound bottleneck in isolated microbenchmarks.
realistic_solution: Upgrading the NPU will not increase the system FPS because the SoC is facing a global memory bandwidth bottleneck, not an isolated compute bottleneck. While the NPU might be fully utilized locally, the CPU, NPU, and GPU are concurrently demanding a total of 22 GB/s from a shared bus that can only provide 17 GB/s. The bus is over-subscribed by 129%, causing severe memory throttling across all components. Upgrading the NPU's compute capacity (TOPS) does nothing to alleviate the bandwidth starvation. You must optimize the memory traffic—specifically the GPU's massive 15 GB/s demand—via quantizati
napkin_math:
Total Bandwidth Demand = CPU (2 GB/s) + NPU (5 GB/s) + GPU (15 GB/s) = 22 GB/s.
Available SoC Bandwidth = 17 GB/s.
Deficit = 5 GB/s. The memory controller will stall requests, artificially inflating the execution time of all accelerators. The NPU's 99% utilization is likely due to stall cycles waiting on memory (memory-bound), not actual compute saturation.

title: SRAM vs DRAM Energy Roofline
scenario: A wearable health monitor runs an anomaly model continuously. The power budget is strictly 5mW. The SoC has an MCU (100 MFLOPS/W) and 1MB of SRAM. A proposed Transformer-based model requires 5 MFLOPs per inference at 100 Hz, and 2MB of weights. Because it exceeds SRAM, the weights must be streamed from off-chip DRAM for every inference. Evaluate if the new model fits within the 5mW budget.
common_mistake: Calculating only the compute power (5 MFLOPs * 100 Hz / 100 MFLOPS/W = 5mW) and concluding the model perfectly fits the budget.
realistic_solution: The compute power alone consumes the entire 5mW budget, but the true failure point is the memory access energy. Fetching data from off-chip DRAM consumes orders of magnitude more energy per byte than on-chip SRAM or the compute operations themselves. Streaming 2MB of weights from DRAM at 100 Hz requires massive memory bandwidth, which will draw significantly more power than the compute units. The Transformer model is completely infeasible not because of compute FLOPs, but because the memory-bound nature of the larger weights catastrophically breaches the energy roofline.
napkin_math:
Compute power: 5 MFLOPs * 100 Hz = 500 MFLOPS. 
Compute power draw = 500 MFLOPS / 100 MFLOPS/W = 5mW.
Memory bandwidth: 2MB * 100 Hz = 200 MB/s.
Assuming typical LPDDR DRAM access costs ~100 pJ/byte:
Memory power draw = 200 * 10^6 bytes/s * 100 * 10^-12 J/byte = 0.02 W = 20mW.
Total power = 5mW + 20mW = 25mW. This is 5x the strict 5mW budget.

title: The Unstructured Pruning Illusion
scenario: Your team is serving a 14B parameter LLM (FP16) on an A10G GPU (900 GB/s memory bandwidth, 125 TFLOPS FP16 compute). To reduce the latency of autoregressive generation at batch size 1, a researcher applies 50% unstructured pruning to the model. Profiling confirms the total FLOPs required per token generation drop by exactly 50%. However, the measured latency per token remains identically stuck at ~31ms. The researcher suspects a PyTorch overhead bug.
common_mistake: Assuming that a 50% reduction in FLOPs will linearly translate to a 50% reduction in latency without checking the roofline model.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a fundamental misunderstanding of the roofline model and arithmetic intensity. Autoregressive token generation at batch size 1 is strictly memory-bandwidth bound. The arithmetic intensity is approximately 1 FLOP/byte (2 operations per parameter, 2 bytes per FP16 parameter). The A10G's roofline ridge point is roughly 138 FLOPs/byte (125 TFLOPS / 900 GB/s). Because 1 << 138, the workload is deep in the memory-bound regime. Unstructured pruning zeros out 50% of the weights, reducing FLOPs, but it does not compress the dense tensor loaded from HBM. The GPU still must transfer the
napkin_math: Model size = 14B params * 2 bytes = 28 GB. Arithmetic Intensity = 14B MACs (28G FLOPs) / 28 GB = 1 FLOP/byte. Memory Latency = 28 GB / 900 GB/s = 31.1 ms. Compute Latency (Dense) = 28 GFLOPs / 125 TFLOPS = 0.22 ms. Compute Latency (Pruned) = 14 GFLOPs / 125 TFLOPS = 0.11 ms. Total time = max(Compute, Memory) = max(0.11ms, 31.1ms) = 31.1 ms.

title: Batched Prefill Throughput Collapse
scenario: You are serving a 70B model on an A100 (80GB, 2000 GB/s, 312 TFLOPS). During the prefill phase for 2048-token prompts, your system processes 1 request at a time, achieving ~150,000 tokens/sec. To maximize throughput, the team updates the serving engine to batch 16 of these 2048-token prompts together. They expect a massive throughput (tokens/sec) increase, similar to the gains seen when batching autoregressive decoding. Instead, the prefill throughput barely increases (less than 5%).
common_mistake: Assuming that increasing batch size always increases GPU utilization and throughput, regardless of the current arithmetic intensity.
realistic_solution: The diagnosis is that the prefill phase for large prompts is already heavily compute-bound, meaning it has saturated the 'flat roof' of the roofline model. In decoding (batch size 1), the arithmetic intensity is ~1 FLOP/byte, which is memory-bound, so increasing batch size linearly increases intensity and throughput. However, in prefill, the arithmetic intensity is roughly equal to the sequence length. For a 2048-token prompt, the intensity is ~2048 FLOPs/byte. The A100's ridge point is 156 FLOPs/byte. Because 2048 >> 156, the GPU's Tensor Cores are already operating at near 100% utilization.
napkin_math: A100 Ridge Point = 312 TFLOPS / 2000 GB/s = 156 FLOPs/byte. Prefill AI (BS=1, Seq=2048) = (2 * 70B * 2048) / (70B * 2 bytes) = 2048 FLOPs/byte. Since 2048 > 156, peak throughput is capped at 312 TFLOPS. Tokens/sec = 312 TFLOPS / (2 * 70B FLOPs/token) = ~2.2M tokens/sec theoretically (achieved ~150K due to MFU). Batching to 16 increases AI to 32768, but the ceiling remains 312 TFLOPS.

title: FlashAttention Head Dimension Spillage
scenario: A researcher designs a custom Transformer on H100s (3.35 TB/s HBM, 228KB SRAM per SM). They change the attention head dimension from $d=128$ to $d=256$, while halving the number of heads to keep the total hidden dimension and FLOPs exactly identical to the baseline. They use FlashAttention-2. Despite identical FLOPs and parameter counts, the training step time increases by 45%.
common_mistake: Assuming that keeping total FLOPs constant will guarantee identical performance without considering the SRAM capacity and tiling constraints of fused kernels.
realistic_solution: The root cause is SRAM capacity overflow leading to a degradation in effective arithmetic intensity. FlashAttention achieves its speedup by tiling the Q, K, and V blocks into the fast but limited SRAM on the GPU's Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), avoiding roundtrips to HBM. The size of these tiles is dictated by the head dimension $d$. When $d$ is doubled to 256, the minimum memory required for a single token's Q, K, V vectors doubles. To fit within the strict 228KB SRAM limit of an H100 SM, the block size (sequence dimension chunk) must be significantly reduced. Smaller block sizes mean the o
napkin_math: HBM accesses in FlashAttention scale with O(N^2 * d / M), where N is sequence length, d is head dimension, and M is SRAM size. If d doubles from 128 to 256, and M is fixed at 228KB, the memory traffic (HBM reads/writes) approximately doubles. Effective Arithmetic Intensity drops by 50%, hitting the 3.35 TB/s HBM bandwidth ceiling.

title: The Activation Checkpointing Paradox
scenario: You are fine-tuning a 30B model on 8x A100 GPUs. To fit a larger batch size, you enable full activation (gradient) checkpointing. You calculate that recomputing the forward pass should add exactly 33% overhead to the total compute time (1 FWD + 2 BWD -> 2 FWD + 2 BWD = 4/3). However, the measured step latency increases by 75%, severely degrading training throughput.
common_mistake: Assuming that step time scales perfectly linearly with FLOP count, ignoring the varying arithmetic intensities of different neural network layers.
realistic_solution: The 33% overhead rule of thumb assumes the entire forward pass is compute-bound. While the large matrix multiplications (QKV projections, FFNs) are compute-bound and scale directly with FLOPs, operations like LayerNorm, GeLU, RoPE, and Dropout are heavily memory-bandwidth bound. They contribute negligibly to the total FLOP count but take up a significant fraction of the execution time because they operate on the steep slope of the roofline model. When you enable full activation checkpointing, you force the GPU to re-fetch all the high-dimensional activations from HBM for these memory-bound ope
napkin_math: Let a layer have 100 TFLOPs of MatMul (compute bound) taking 0.32ms, and 0.01 TFLOPs of LayerNorm/GeLU (memory bound) taking 0.3ms. Total time = 0.62ms. FLOPs are 99.99% MatMul. Recomputing adds 100.01 TFLOPs (33% of FWD+BWD FLOPs). But in time, you add another 0.62ms. If BWD was 1.24ms, original total = 1.86ms. New total = 2.48ms. 2.48 / 1.86 = 1.33x. Wait, if memory-bound ops dominate more, e.g., MatMul 0.32ms, MemOps 1.0ms. Orig = 1.32 FWD + 2.64 BWD = 3.96ms. Recompute adds 1.32ms. New = 5.28ms. Still 33%. The real paradox occurs when BWD doesn't re-read as much for MemOps. Recomputing for

title: MoE Routing at High Batch Size
scenario: You replace a dense 30B parameter LLM with a 16x10B Mixture of Experts (MoE) model. Top-1 routing is used, so active parameters per token is 10B. You serve this at a high batch size of 128 on an 8xH100 cluster. You expect the MoE to be 3x faster than the dense model because active FLOPs per token drop from 30B to 10B. Instead, the MoE model is 30% slower than the 30B dense model.
common_mistake: Calculating performance purely based on active FLOPs while ignoring how routing shatters the batch size and destroys arithmetic intensity.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a massive drop in arithmetic intensity caused by the MoE routing mechanism. At batch size 128, a dense 30B model processes all 128 tokens against the same 30B parameters. This yields an arithmetic intensity of ~128 FLOPs/byte, placing it firmly in the compute-bound regime on the H100 roofline. In the MoE model, the 128 tokens are routed across 16 different experts. Assuming an even distribution, each 10B expert processes only 8 tokens. The arithmetic intensity for the expert layers plummets to 8 FLOPs/byte. This plunges the workload deep into the memory-bound regime. Furtherm
napkin_math: Dense 30B at BS=128: AI = 128. Compute time = (30B * 2 MACs * 128 tokens) / 989 TFLOPS = 7.7 ms. Memory time = 60GB / 3.35 TB/s = 17.9 ms. (Memory bound, takes ~17.9ms). MoE 16x10B at BS=128: 8 tokens/expert. AI = 8. If all 16 experts are hit, we load 160B params (320GB). Memory time = 320GB / 3.35 TB/s = 95.5 ms. 95.5 ms > 17.9 ms, hence it is significantly slower.

title: The W16A8 Quantization Trap
scenario: Your team is optimizing a 70B parameter LLM for single-batch autoregressive decoding on an H100 GPU (3.35 TB/s, 989 TFLOPS FP16, 1978 TOPS INT8). They implement W16A8 quantization (weights remain FP16, activations are dynamically quantized to INT8) to leverage the 2x faster INT8 Tensor Cores. They expect a noticeable decrease in token generation latency. Instead, latency remains at exactly ~42ms per token, with a slight CPU overhead increase.
common_mistake: Assuming that doubling the compute throughput (TOPS) via activation quantization will speed up all operations, regardless of where they sit on the roofline.
realistic_solution: The failure occurs because autoregressive decoding at batch size 1 is fundamentally weight-memory-bandwidth bound, not compute-bound. The arithmetic intensity of BS=1 decoding is approximately 1 FLOP/byte. The H100's ridge point for FP16 is ~295 FLOPs/byte (989 T / 3.35 T). Since 1 << 295, the time is entirely dictated by reading the weights from HBM. Because the implementation used W16A8, the weights are still stored and loaded in FP16. The memory traffic (140GB) is completely unchanged. Speeding up the math units from 989 TFLOPS to 1978 TOPS does absolutely nothing for end-to-end latency bec
napkin_math: FP16 Memory Time = 70B params * 2 bytes = 140 GB. 140 GB / 3.35 TB/s = 41.7 ms. FP16 Compute Time = 140 GFLOPs / 989 TFLOPS = 0.14 ms. Total Time ~ max(41.7, 0.14) = 41.7 ms. W16A8 INT8 Compute Time = 140 GFLOPs / 1978 TOPS = 0.07 ms. Total Time ~ max(41.7, 0.07) = 41.7 ms. 0% improvement.

title: The MQA Prefill Disappointment
scenario: To optimize the prefill phase for 16K-token documents on an L4 GPU (300 GB/s, 120 TFLOPS), your team replaces standard Multi-Head Attention (MHA) with Multi-Query Attention (MQA). They know MQA drastically reduces KV cache memory reads. They expect the prefill latency for a single 16K prompt to drop by at least 30%. Instead, the prefill latency is nearly identical to the original MHA model.
common_mistake: Conflating the memory-bound nature of the decode phase with the compute-bound nature of the prefill phase.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a misclassification of the workload bound during the prefill phase. While autoregressive decoding is memory-bandwidth bound (reading the KV cache token by token), the prefill phase of a long prompt is heavily compute-bound. During prefill, the dominant operation in attention is the $Q \times K^T$ matrix multiplication. For a 16K sequence length, the arithmetic intensity of this operation is proportional to the sequence length, pushing it far to the right of the L4's roofline ridge point. MQA reduces the memory footprint of the KV cache, which solves a memory-bandwidth bottlen
napkin_math: L4 Ridge Point = 120 TFLOPS / 300 GB/s = 400 FLOPs/byte. For MHA prefill of 16K tokens, Attention AI ~ O(SeqLen) = 16000 FLOPs/byte. 16000 >> 400, so it is deeply compute-bound. Time is dictated by FLOPs / 120 TFLOPS. MQA reduces KV memory loaded by factor of 'h' (heads), but memory load time was already hidden behind the massive compute time.

title: The Embedding Layer Bandwidth Illusion
scenario: An ML engineer profiles an embedding lookup table for a recommendation model on an H100 GPU (3.35 TB/s peak bandwidth). The batch size is 65,536 and the embedding dimension is 128 (FP32). Profiling shows the operation takes 1.5ms. The engineer calculates the throughput at ~22.3 GB/s. Seeing this is less than 1% of the H100's peak bandwidth, they conclude the PyTorch kernel is terribly unoptimized and spend weeks rewriting it in Triton, achieving zero speedup.
common_mistake: Applying the theoretical peak memory bandwidth from the roofline model to random access (gather) workloads.
realistic_solution: The engineer is confusing sequential/coalesced memory bandwidth with random access (gather) memory bandwidth. The roofline model's peak memory bandwidth (3.35 TB/s) assumes perfect, contiguous, coalesced reads where the memory bus is fully saturated. Embedding lookups require gathering non-contiguous rows from HBM based on random indices. For random accesses of 128 floats (512 bytes), the GPU must fetch entire cache lines (often 32-128 bytes each, depending on architecture) from disparate locations, breaking coalescing rules and causing massive cache thrashing. The memory controllers cannot sa
napkin_math: Data moved = 65,536 indices * 128 floats * 4 bytes/float = 33,554,432 bytes (~33.5 MB). Measured Bandwidth = 33.5 MB / 0.0015 s = 22.3 GB/s. 22.3 GB/s / 3350 GB/s = 0.66% utilization of peak bandwidth. However, random access latency for HBM is ~200-300ns. The bottleneck is memory latency and transaction limits, not bus width.

title: The Long Prompt Prefill Cliff
scenario: You are deploying a 3B parameter LLM (4-bit quantized) on a mobile NPU. For prompts under 500 tokens, the Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) is a swift 250ms. However, when the prompt reaches 1500 tokens, TTFT non-linearly spikes to 4.5 seconds. In both cases, the decode latency remains completely flat at 20 tokens/sec.
common_mistake: Assuming the NPU is thermally throttling due to the longer sustained compute phase of the larger prefill.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the exhaustion of the NPU's limited on-chip SRAM. Prefill is heavily compute-bound, performing large matrix multiplications. For short prompts, the intermediate activations and the generated KV cache fit entirely within the NPU's SRAM (e.g., 4MB). However, at 1500 tokens, the working set size exceeds the SRAM capacity. The NPU is forced to spill intermediate attention tensors and the KV cache to the device's LPDDR5 DRAM. This shifts the prefill phase from being compute-bound (running at peak NPU TFLOPs) to memory-bandwidth bound, drastically reducing utilization and causing t
napkin_math: SRAM capacity = 4MB. KV cache for 500 tokens: 500 tokens * 32 layers * 64 dim * 8 heads * 2 (K+V) * 2 bytes = ~3.2MB (fits in SRAM). For 1500 tokens, KV cache = 9.8MB. This forces DRAM round-trips. Writing 9.8MB to DRAM at 30GB/s is fast, but the O(N^2) attention intermediate activations for 1500 tokens require continuous paging in and out of SRAM, dropping effective compute utilization from 80% to under 15%.

title: Speculative Decoding Performance Regression
scenario: You implement speculative decoding on a flagship phone to accelerate a 4B target model (running on the NPU at 15 tok/s). You use a 100M draft model running on the CPU. Despite achieving a strong draft acceptance rate of 65% (gamma=4), the overall effective throughput drops from 15 tok/s to 10 tok/s.
common_mistake: Assuming the draft model is too slow, when in reality a 100M model on a modern mobile CPU can generate tokens extremely fast.
realistic_solution: The performance regression is caused by the heterogeneous compute synchronization overhead between the CPU and the NPU. Speculative decoding requires the draft model (CPU) to send its generated token IDs and KV data to the target model (NPU) for validation. Mobile SoCs do not have cache-coherent unified memory between the CPU and the NPU in the same way server GPUs do. The OS must perform expensive cross-device buffer synchronizations, cache flushes, and driver API calls for every single speculative step. This latency completely negates the compute savings of skipping target model decode steps
napkin_math:
Baseline target: 1000ms / 15 = 66ms/token. 
Draft step (4 tokens): CPU takes 4 * 10ms = 40ms. 
Target validation (4 tokens): NPU takes 70ms. 
Hardware Sync overhead (CPU->NPU->CPU): 150ms per step. 
Total time per step: 40 + 70 + 150 = 260ms. 
Accepted tokens per step: 1 (base) + (4 * 0.65) = 3.6 tokens. 
Effective speed: 3.6 tokens / 0.26s = ~13.8 tok/s (plus additional overheads dropping it to 10 tok/s). The 150ms sync cost destroys the theoretical speedup.

title: The Context Length Latency Spike
scenario: A local mobile chat app runs a 2B parameter model using a mobile GPU. Decode latency is perfectly stable at 35ms/token. However, exactly when the conversation history reaches 1024 tokens, decode latency instantly jumps to 110ms/token and remains there. Device thermals are normal and there is no OS memory paging.
common_mistake: Assuming the device ran out of RAM and started swapping to flash storage, which would cause a much more erratic latency profile.
realistic_solution: The step-function increase in latency is caused by a hardware-specific FlashAttention tile size limit. Mobile GPUs have strictly limited threadgroup shared memory (e.g., 32KB per workgroup). The FlashAttention kernel is heavily optimized to keep the K and V blocks in this fast SRAM. At exactly 1024 tokens, the required tile size for the sequence length exceeds the maximum shared memory allocation for the shader. The inference engine's fallback mechanism silently switches to a standard, unoptimized attention kernel, which requires O(N) reads and writes to the slower LPDDR5 DRAM for every single
napkin_math:
For a standard head dimension of 64 (FP16): 1024 tokens * 64 dim * 2 bytes = 128KB. If the GPU's max shared memory is 32KB, the tile cannot fit. 
Standard Attention Memory Traffic at 1025 tokens: Read K (1025*64*2 = 131KB), Read V (131KB), Write Logits (1025*2 = 2KB) = ~264KB per head, per layer, per token. Across 32 layers and 8 heads, this is ~67MB of DRAM traffic per token just for attention, bottlenecking the GPU.

title: Multi-Turn Chat Degradation
scenario: In a mobile voice assistant using a 3B LLM, the 1st turn (100 tokens) has a TTFT of 150ms. By the 10th turn (accumulated 2000 tokens), TTFT degrades to 4500ms. Decode speed remains perfectly constant at 18 tok/s throughout all turns. The profiler shows 0% thermal throttling.
common_mistake: Assuming the decode phase is slowing down due to the larger KV cache, which contradicts the constant decode speed metric.
realistic_solution: The degradation is caused by the lack of a continuous/appendable KV cache mechanism in the mobile inference framework. Because the mobile OS cannot guarantee contiguous memory allocation for dynamically growing tensors, the framework recalculates the prefill for the entire conversation history from scratch on every single turn. While decode latency remains constant because it only processes one token at a time, the prefill phase must perform an O(N^2) attention computation over an ever-growing prompt, causing the TTFT to scale quadratically with the number of turns.
napkin_math: Prefill compute roughly scales as O(N^2) for attention. Turn 1 (100 tokens): 100^2 = 10,000 operations. Turn 10 (2000 tokens): 2000^2 = 4,000,000 operations. The attention compute workload increased by 400x. If the base prefill compute (linear layers + attention) took 150ms, the quadratic scaling of the attention component easily drives the latency to 4.5 seconds for 2000 tokens when recomputed entirely.

title: The Code Generation Stall
scenario: Your speculative decoding setup (3B Target, 60M Draft) yields 25 tok/s for general chat on-device. When a user asks the app to generate a Python script, throughput suddenly drops to 6 tok/s. Disabling speculative decoding entirely restores Python generation to a flat 12 tok/s.
common_mistake: Assuming the target model struggles to generate code and requires more compute per token.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a catastrophic drop in the draft model's acceptance rate due to a domain shift. The heavily pruned 60M draft model was likely distilled exclusively on natural language data to save space on-device, meaning it lacks basic Python syntax knowledge. When generating code, the draft model consistently guesses wrong (acceptance rate near 0%). Consequently, the system pays the latency penalty for the draft model's generation AND the target model's validation, but rejects all draft tokens, making it strictly slower than running the target model alone.
napkin_math:
Target-only latency: 1000ms / 12 = 83ms/token. 
Speculative step (4 draft tokens): Draft generation takes 4 * 10ms = 40ms. Target validation takes 90ms. Total step time = 130ms. 
If acceptance rate is 5%, expected accepted tokens per step = 1 (the forced correction token) + 0.05*4 = ~1.2 tokens. 
Effective latency = 130ms / 1.2 tokens = 108ms/token. 
Throughput = 1000 / 108 = 9.2 tok/s (plus rejection overhead, dropping it to 6 tok/s).

title: The Background Batching Bottleneck
scenario: Your mobile OS runs a 3B LLM (INT4 quantized). A foreground chat runs at 20 tok/s (batch size 1). When the OS schedules a background notification summarizer to run concurrently (batch size 2), the total system throughput plummets to 12 tok/s (6 tok/s per task), rather than achieving higher total throughput.
common_mistake: Assuming the NPU ran out of compute ALUs, when in reality decode is almost entirely memory-bound, not compute-bound.
realistic_solution: The system hits a severe memory bandwidth wall. At batch size 1, the LLM streams the 1.5GB of quantized weights from LPDDR5 perfectly linearly. At batch size 2, the continuous batching engine must fetch the KV cache for two completely disparate sequences simultaneously. This disrupts the contiguous memory access patterns. The mobile memory controller experiences high page miss rates in the DRAM banks, causing the effective memory bandwidth utilization to drop precipitously. Total throughput decreases because the memory bus is stalling.
napkin_math:
INT4 3B model = 1.5GB weights. At 20 tok/s, weight bandwidth = 1.5GB * 20 = 30 GB/s (near the ~34 GB/s max of LPDDR5). 
At BS=2, we need to read 1.5GB of weights + 2 separate KV caches. If the scattered reads drop effective bandwidth to 18 GB/s due to DRAM page misses, the max decode speed becomes 18 GB/s / 1.5 GB = 12 tok/s total. Divided by 2 tasks, each task gets exactly 6 tok/s.

title: The INT8 KV Cache Penalty
scenario: To fit a 4B model in 4GB of mobile RAM, you quantize the KV cache from FP16 to INT8, successfully saving 600MB. However, you observe that decode latency increases from 35ms per token to 55ms per token, and overall NPU utilization drops from 80% to 50%.
common_mistake: Assuming INT8 memory fetches are slower than FP16 memory fetches.
realistic_solution: The latency increase is caused by the lack of native INT8-to-FP16 mixed-precision attention support on the mobile NPU. To perform the attention mechanism, the inference engine must insert an explicit dequantization kernel that reads the INT8 KV cache, casts it back to FP16, and writes it to an intermediate buffer in SRAM (or DRAM) before the actual attention kernel can run. This extra kernel launch, combined with the intermediate memory round-trip on every single decode step, adds pure latency overhead that stalls the NPU.
napkin_math:
With a 1000-token context, 32 layers, 128 dim, 8 heads: KV cache size is ~3.2MB in INT8. 
Dequantization kernel must read 3.2MB and write 6.4MB (FP16) per token. 
At 30GB/s bandwidth, this memory movement takes ~0.3ms per layer, or ~10ms total across 32 layers. 
Kernel launch overheads on mobile OS (e.g., 0.3ms per layer) add another ~10ms. Total added latency = 20ms per token. 35ms + 20ms = 55ms.

title: The PagedAttention System Call Trap
scenario: You port a PagedAttention implementation to an Android flagship to prevent KV cache fragmentation. With a block size of 16, a 2000-token prompt causes severe decode latency jitter (varying wildly from 40ms to 180ms per token) and massive CPU overhead (peaking at 100% on one core), even though the LLM is running entirely on the GPU.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU is struggling to read from non-contiguous memory blocks due to cache misses.
realistic_solution: The root cause is CPU-bound driver API overhead. Mobile GPUs lack the advanced hardware scatter-gather Memory Management Units (MMUs) found in data center GPUs. To emulate PagedAttention, the inference engine must bind hundreds of separate memory descriptors (one for each 16-token block) to the GPU command buffer for every layer, on every token. Submitting these tiny, fragmented buffer bindings overwhelms the Android graphics driver (e.g., Vulkan/OpenCL), causing the CPU to bottleneck the GPU. The GPU sits idle waiting for the CPU to construct and submit the command buffers.
napkin_math:
2000 tokens / 16 block size = 125 blocks. 
32 layers * 125 blocks = 4,000 separate buffer bindings per token generated. 
If the mobile graphics driver takes 0.02ms per descriptor binding, 4000 * 0.02ms = 80ms of pure CPU overhead per token. 
Because the OS scheduler preempts the thread, this latency is highly erratic, causing the observed 40-180ms jitter.

title: Speculative Decoding Draft Model Sizing
scenario: You are deploying a 70B parameter LLM for a code generation API. The strict latency budget is 20ms Time Per Output Token (TPOT). Currently, TPOT on 4x A100 80GB GPUs is 35ms at batch size 1. You propose speculative decoding and must choose between two draft models: a 1.5B model (acceptance rate 0.6, 5ms/token draft latency) and a 7B model (acceptance rate 0.85, 12ms/token draft latency). The 70B target model evaluation step takes 15ms for verifying up to 5 tokens.
common_mistake: Choosing the 7B model because its 85% acceptance rate seems vastly superior and will lead to fewer target model evaluations.
realistic_solution: The correct choice is the 1.5B draft model. Speculative decoding performance is a strict tradeoff between the computational overhead of the draft model and the acceptance rate of the target model. The goal is to minimize the expected time per generated token. A highly accurate draft model is useless if its latency overhead consumes the time saved by skipping target model autoregressive steps. By calculating the expected latency per generated token, the 1.5B model yields ~10.3ms/token, comfortably beating the 20ms budget. The 7B model yields ~14.3ms/token. While both meet the budget, the 1.5B m
napkin_math:
Let gamma = 4 tokens drafted per step. Target evaluation = 15ms.
1.5B Draft: Draft time = 4 * 5ms = 20ms. Total step time = 20ms + 15ms = 35ms. Expected tokens generated = 1 + (4 * 0.6) = 3.4 tokens. Expected TPOT = 35ms / 3.4 = 10.29 ms/token.
7B Draft: Draft time = 4 * 12ms = 48ms. Total step time = 48ms + 15ms = 63ms. Expected tokens generated = 1 + (4 * 0.85) = 4.4 tokens. Expected TPOT = 63ms / 4.4 = 14.31 ms/token.

title: Prefill and Decode Cluster Disaggregation
scenario: Your high-traffic chatbot service uses Llama-3-70B. Average input is 2000 tokens; average output is 50 tokens. Prefill takes 400ms, which starves continuous batching decode requests, causing TPOT spikes up to 100ms. You propose disaggregating the architecture into dedicated 'Prefill' and 'Decode' GPU nodes. The nodes are connected via a 100 Gbps RoCE network.
common_mistake: Assuming disaggregation always improves latency without calculating the network transfer overhead of the KV cache.
realistic_solution: Disaggregation solves the compute-bound (prefill) vs. memory-bandwidth-bound (decode) interference, eliminating decode TPOT spikes. However, the strict tradeoff is the network transfer latency of the KV cache from the prefill node to the decode node. For a 2000-token prompt on a 70B model, the KV cache is substantial. The transfer time over a 100 Gbps link adds directly to the Time To First Token (TTFT). You must evaluate if the added TTFT is acceptable to the user in exchange for stable, low-variance TPOT. Given the math, a ~26ms network penalty on TTFT is highly favorable compared to 100ms T
napkin_math: KV cache size per token (Llama-3-70B: 80 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 dim, FP16): 2 bytes * 80 * 8 * 128 = ~163 KB/token. For 2000 tokens, total KV cache = 2000 * 163 KB = ~326 MB. Network bandwidth: 100 Gbps = 12.5 GB/s. Transfer time = 326 MB / 12.5 GB/s = 26.08 ms. TTFT increases by ~26ms, but TPOT stabilizes at the baseline (e.g., 30ms) instead of spiking to 100ms.

title: Paged Attention Block Size Fragmentation
scenario: You are serving a 13B model on a single A100 40GB GPU. Max context is 8192 tokens. You implemented Paged Attention to manage the KV cache. Initially, you set the block size to 256 tokens to maximize memory-fetch efficiency. However, you hit OOM limits at a batch size of 32, despite average request lengths being only 300 tokens.
common_mistake: Decreasing the maximum context length or reducing the batch size to avoid OOM, rather than addressing internal memory fragmentation.
realistic_solution: The root cause is massive internal memory fragmentation due to an oversized block size. In Paged Attention, memory is allocated in fixed-size blocks. A request of 300 tokens requires two 256-token blocks (512 tokens capacity), wasting 212 tokens worth of KV cache memory. The tradeoff in block size selection is internal fragmentation versus page table lookup overhead. Large blocks reduce page table size and kernel launch overhead but waste memory for unfilled blocks. Small blocks (e.g., 16 tokens) virtually eliminate internal fragmentation, allowing much higher batch sizes, but slightly increas
napkin_math: Block size = 256. Request = 300 tokens -> 2 blocks allocated = 512 tokens. Wasted = 212 tokens per request. Internal fragmentation = 212 / 512 = 41.4%. At batch size 32, wasted tokens = 32 * 212 = 6,784 tokens. KV size per token (13B model): ~100KB. Wasted memory = 6784 * 100KB = ~678 MB. By reducing block size to 16, a 300-token request takes 19 blocks (304 tokens). Wasted = 4 tokens (1.3% fragmentation), saving ~670 MB and allowing the batch size to increase.

title: Chunked Prefill for Latency Jitter
scenario: Your continuous batching server runs a 7B model. The target TPOT is 30ms. When a new 4000-token prompt arrives, the prefill phase takes 400ms, blocking the GPU. Existing decode requests in the batch are forced to wait, causing severe P99 TPOT degradation (latency spikes up to 430ms). You are evaluating Chunked Prefill to mitigate this.
common_mistake: Assuming chunked prefill will improve both Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) and Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) simultaneously.
realistic_solution: Chunked prefill requires analyzing the tradeoff between compute efficiency (TTFT) and latency jitter (TPOT). Standard prefill processes all tokens in one massive matrix multiplication, which maximizes GPU Tensor Core utilization. Chunking the prefill (e.g., into 512-token blocks) allows interleaving the prefill chunks with the decode steps of other requests. This preserves the 30ms TPOT for existing requests (eliminating the 430ms spike). However, smaller chunks lower the arithmetic intensity of the GEMM operations, slightly increasing the total time required to process the 4000 tokens, thereb
napkin_math: Unchunked: 4000-token prefill takes 400ms. Decode requests wait 400ms + 30ms = 430ms TPOT spike. Chunked (512 tokens): 8 chunks. Each 512-token chunk takes ~60ms (due to lower GPU utilization compared to a single 400ms monolithic block). Interleaved step = 60ms (prefill chunk) + 30ms (decode) = 90ms. Max TPOT jitter is now 90ms (down from 430ms). However, TTFT for the new request = 8 chunks * 90ms = 720ms (up from 400ms).

title: KV Cache PCIe Offloading
scenario: To support long-context chat sessions (up to 100K tokens) on an 8x H100 GPU node, you frequently hit KV cache OOM errors. You propose offloading inactive KV cache blocks to the host CPU's DDR5 RAM over PCIe Gen5 x16, fetching them back to the GPU only when the user sends a follow-up message.
common_mistake: Offloading synchronously during the attention computation phase, assuming PCIe bandwidth is fast enough to not impact the decode latency.
realistic_solution: The tradeoff here is between GPU memory capacity (supporting larger context/more users) and the massive latency penalty of PCIe bandwidth limits. PCIe Gen5 x16 has a theoretical bandwidth of 64 GB/s. Fetching a large KV cache from CPU to GPU will severely bottleneck the prefill/decode phases if not hidden via asynchronous prefetching. The solution requires a predictive swap-in mechanism. When a request arrives at the router, the system must trigger an asynchronous DMA transfer of the KV cache from CPU to GPU before scheduling the compute kernel. If done synchronously, the TTFT will be dominate
napkin_math: KV cache size for 100K tokens (70B model, FP16): ~160 KB/token * 100,000 = 16 GB. PCIe Gen5 x16 practical bandwidth = ~50 GB/s. Transfer time = 16 GB / 50 GB/s = 320 ms. If fetched synchronously upon request, TTFT incurs a guaranteed 320ms penalty just for data movement. Asynchronous prefetching overlaps this 320ms with network routing and queue waiting.

title: Multi-LoRA Continuous Batching
scenario: A multi-tenant SaaS serves 100 distinct fine-tuned LoRA adapters over a single 13B base model. Traffic per tenant is 2 QPS. Deploying dedicated GPUs per tenant is cost-prohibitive. You multiplex them on a single GPU using continuous batching, but grouping requests by LoRA adapter results in tiny batch sizes (average 1.5) and 20% GPU utilization.
common_mistake: Dynamically swapping base model weights with LoRA weights in GPU memory per request, which destroys memory bandwidth.
realistic_solution: The tradeoff is between batching efficiency (GPU compute utilization) and kernel dispatch overhead. Standard batching requires all requests to use the same weights. To batch requests across *different* LoRAs, you must use grouped GEMM (e.g., Punica kernels). The base model weights are applied to the entire batch in one highly efficient large matrix multiplication. The LoRA-specific computations are handled by a custom CUDA kernel that applies different low-rank matrices to different rows of the batch simultaneously. This maximizes base-model throughput at the cost of slightly slower, custom at
napkin_math: Base model (13B, FP16) = 26GB. 100 LoRAs (rank 16) = 100 * 50MB = 5GB. Total 31GB fits in 40GB VRAM. Unbatched sequential execution: 15ms per request * 16 requests = 240ms. With grouped GEMM (batch size 16): Base model GEMM takes 20ms. The grouped LoRA GEMM (16 small parallel GEMMs) takes 5ms. Total time = 25ms. Throughput increases nearly 10x.

title: System-Prompt Prefix Caching
scenario: Your B2B summarization service prepends a complex 1500-token system prompt (instructions and few-shot examples) to every 500-token user document. The cluster handles 200 QPS. Profiling shows that computing the prefill for the 1500-token prompt dominates the cluster's GPU time. You decide to implement RadixAttention (Prefix Caching) to reuse the KV cache of the system prompt.
common_mistake: Assuming prefix caching has no downsides, ignoring that persistent cache blocks consume VRAM that could otherwise be used for dynamic request batching.
realistic_solution: Prefix caching presents a tradeoff between prefill compute reduction and GPU memory capacity. By retaining the KV cache of the 1500-token system prompt in VRAM, you completely bypass the compute-heavy prefill phase for those tokens, massively reducing TTFT and freeing up TFLOPs across the cluster. However, this cached prefix permanently occupies VRAM on every replica. This reduces the available pool of free memory blocks for the dynamic KV cache of active decode requests, slightly lowering the maximum possible continuous batch size. Given the heavy compute cost of a 1500-token prefill, caching
napkin_math: System prompt = 1500 tokens. KV cache size (70B model) = 160 KB/token. Memory consumed per replica = 1500 * 160 KB = 240 MB. Prefill compute saved per request = 1500 tokens * 70B params * 2 = 210 TFLOPs. At 200 QPS, this saves 42 PFLOPs of compute globally. The cost is 240 MB of VRAM per replica, which is roughly equivalent to losing the ability to batch 1-2 concurrent user generations, a highly favorable tradeoff.

title: Continuous Batching Decode Spikes
scenario: You operate a multi-turn chat service using a 13B model on A100 GPUs. The business SLA mandates a Time Between Tokens (TPOT) of < 50ms to ensure smooth text generation. With continuous batching enabled, average TPOT is 25ms. However, P99 TPOT frequently spikes to 200ms+. Telemetry shows these spikes correlate exactly with new users submitting long prompts (e.g., 4k tokens) into the system.
common_mistake: Assuming the GPU is simply overloaded and decreasing the maximum batch size to reduce compute pressure.
realistic_solution: Decompose the latency: Continuous batching mixes prefill and decode phases in the same forward pass. A 4k-token prefill requires massive matrix multiplications, taking ~150ms of compute. Because the GPU executes the batch synchronously, the decode steps for all other requests in the batch are blocked until the 150ms prefill completes, violating the 50ms TPOT SLA. The architectural requirement is to implement Chunked Prefill (e.g., Sarathi or Piggybacking). By chunking the 4k prompt into segments of 512 tokens, the prefill compute is bounded to ~20ms per step. This allows decode steps to interl
napkin_math: Compute time for 4k prefill = ~150ms. Decode time = ~20ms. Synchronous iteration time = max(150, 20) = 150ms (SLA violation). Chunked to 512 tokens: Chunk compute = ~18ms. Iteration time = max(18, 20) = 20ms (SLA met).

title: Speculative Decoding Economics
scenario: You are optimizing a code generation API using a 70B target model with a strict TPOT SLA of < 25ms. Currently, the target model achieves 30ms TPOT. To accelerate it, you deploy a 7B draft model for speculative decoding. The draft model generates 5 tokens in 50ms. The target model verifies them in 30ms. The domain acceptance rate is 1.5 tokens per step. The resulting TPOT worsens to 32ms.
common_mistake: Increasing the number of draft tokens generated per step (e.g., from 5 to 10) to increase the potential acceptance yield.
realistic_solution: Decompose the speculative decoding latency: T_step = (T_draft * K) + T_verify. The draft model takes 10ms per token (50ms/5). The verify step takes 30ms. Total step latency = 80ms. With an expected acceptance rate E(N) of 1.5 tokens + 1 guaranteed token = 2.5 tokens per step. The effective TPOT = 80ms / 2.5 tokens = 32ms. Speculative decoding only works if T_draft is negligible or E(N) is exceptionally high. A 7B model is architecturally too large/slow for a 70B target. You must replace the 7B draft model with a much smaller <1B model (T_draft < 2ms), or eliminate the draft model entirely by u
napkin_math: Current: T_step = 50ms + 30ms = 80ms. Yield = 2.5 tokens. TPOT = 80 / 2.5 = 32ms. (Worse than 30ms base). Proposed (<1B draft): T_draft = 2ms * 5 = 10ms. T_step = 10ms + 30ms = 40ms. Yield = 2.5. TPOT = 40 / 2.5 = 16ms (SLA met).

title: Paged Attention Internal Fragmentation
scenario: You are designing a high-throughput translation API. The system requires a batch size of 128 to meet the 2000 QPS throughput SLA. Average prompt + generation length is 80 tokens. You configure PagedAttention with a block size of 512 tokens to minimize pointer overhead. The system constantly OOMs at a batch size of just 32, failing the throughput requirement.
common_mistake: Adding more GPUs to increase total VRAM, or reducing the model's precision to INT4 to fit the batch size.
realistic_solution: Decompose the memory allocation latency and capacity. The architectural failure is massive internal memory fragmentation. With a block size of 512 tokens, every request, regardless of its actual length, reserves a minimum of one full block. Since the average request is 80 tokens, 432 tokens of KV cache memory per request are pre-allocated but completely wasted. This is an 84% memory waste. The architecture requirement is to reduce the PagedAttention block size to 16 or 32 tokens. This drastically reduces internal fragmentation, freeing up enough VRAM to easily support the required batch size o
napkin_math: KV cache per token (e.g., Llama-2 13B) = ~100KB. Block size 512 = 51.2MB per block. For batch size 128: 128 * 51.2MB = 6.5GB reserved. Actual needed: 128 * 80 * 100KB = 1.02GB. Wasted: 5.5GB per layer. Across 40 layers, wasted = 220GB (OOM on 80GB GPU). Block size 16: reserved = 128 * 5 blocks * 1.6MB * 40 = 40GB (fits in VRAM).

title: Disaggregated Prefill Network Bottleneck
scenario: To optimize your Llama-3 70B serving cluster, you implement disaggregated architecture: separating Prefill (Compute-bound) onto A100s and Decode (Memory-bound) onto H100s. For a 10k token prompt, the Prefill TTFT is 150ms and Decode TPOT is 20ms. However, end-to-end TTFT measured by the client spikes to ~400ms. The nodes are connected via a 100 Gbps Ethernet network.
common_mistake: Assuming the prefill compute is bottlenecked and buying faster GPUs for the prefill cluster.
realistic_solution: Decompose the end-to-end TTFT latency: Client TTFT = Prefill Compute + KV Cache Network Transfer + Initial Decode Step + Network RTT. The hidden bottleneck is the KV cache transfer. Llama-3 70B has 80 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 dim. A 10k token prompt generates ~3.27 GB of KV cache. Over a 100 Gbps (12.5 GB/s theoretical, ~10 GB/s actual) network, transferring this cache takes ~327ms. 150ms compute + 327ms transfer = 477ms TTFT. To fix this architecture, you must upgrade the inter-node network to Infiniband (400 Gbps / 800 Gbps) with RDMA to reduce transfer time to <50ms, or abandon disaggregatio
napkin_math: KV Cache size per token = 2 (K,V) * 80 layers * 8 heads * 128 dim * 2 bytes = 327,680 bytes (~327 KB). For 10,000 tokens = 3.27 GB. Network speed = 100 Gbps = 12.5 GB/s. Transfer latency = 3.27 GB / 10 GB/s (effective) = 327ms. Total TTFT = 150ms + 327ms = 477ms.

title: Prompt Caching PCIe Bottleneck
scenario: You are building a multi-tenant RAG application using a 70B model. Every request shares a massive 50k token system prompt. TTFT SLA is 500ms. Standard prefill takes 1.5s. You implement RadixAttention prompt caching, storing the 50k KV cache on host CPU RAM (NVMe offload) and loading it on demand over PCIe Gen4 x16. The TTFT drops, but only to ~700ms, failing the 500ms SLA.
common_mistake: Optimizing the CPU RAM speed or trying to compress the KV cache using standard ZIP/LZ4 algorithms before transfer.
realistic_solution: Decompose the cached TTFT latency: TTFT = KV Cache PCIe Load Time + User Prompt Prefill + Decode Step. The 50k token system prompt generates a ~16.3 GB KV cache for a 70B model. PCIe Gen4 x16 has a theoretical max bandwidth of 32 GB/s (realistically ~26 GB/s). Transferring 16.3 GB takes ~620ms. The architecture is bound by PCIe bandwidth, not compute. To meet the 500ms SLA, the architecture must change: the shared 50k KV cache must be pinned as a resident block directly in GPU VRAM (sacrificing some batch size capacity), or the model must use Multi-Query Attention/Grouped-Query Attention to dr
napkin_math: KV size: 50,000 tokens * 327 KB/token (Llama-3 70B) = 16.35 GB. PCIe Gen4 x16 effective bandwidth = ~26 GB/s. Transfer time = 16.35 GB / 26 GB/s = 628ms. 628ms + 50ms (user prompt prefill) = 678ms > 500ms SLA.

title: Memory Bandwidth Bound Decode
scenario: You deploy a 70B parameter model (FP16) for a real-time voice agent. Target TPOT is 20ms. The model is deployed on 2x A100 80GB GPUs (Tensor Parallelism, 1.5 TB/s bandwidth each). Even with a batch size of 1, the best TPOT achieved is ~48ms, severely violating the 20ms requirement.
common_mistake: Attempting to optimize compute with FlashAttention or fusing CUDA kernels, which only helps compute-bound workloads.
realistic_solution: Decompose the decode step latency: In autoregressive generation (batch size 1), the operation is entirely Memory Bandwidth Bound. For every single token generated, the entire 140 GB model weight matrix must be read from HBM to SRAM. Compute time is negligible. T_token = Model_Size / Total_Memory_Bandwidth. 140 GB / 3000 GB/s = 46.6ms. It is physically impossible to hit 20ms on this hardware in FP16. The architectural requirement is to either quantize the model weights to INT8/INT4 (reducing weights to 70GB/35GB -> 23ms/11ms latency), or double the hardware footprint to 4x A100s to aggregate 6.
napkin_math: Model size = 70 billion parameters * 2 bytes (FP16) = 140 GB. Hardware bandwidth = 2 * 1.5 TB/s = 3.0 TB/s. Minimum physical time to read weights = 140 GB / 3000 GB/s = 0.0466 seconds = 46.6ms. 46.6ms > 20ms SLA.

title: Continuous Batching Queueing Death Spiral
scenario: Your summarization service handles exactly 2 QPS. Prompts are 8k tokens, outputs are 1k tokens. The business SLA is 45s end-to-end latency. You cap the batch size at 8 to strictly prevent KV cache OOMs. TPOT is a flat 30ms. Prefill is 1s. Under sustained 2 QPS load, P99 latency slowly climbs to over 5 minutes.
common_mistake: Blaming continuous batching overhead and reverting to static batching, or trying to optimize the TPOT slightly.
realistic_solution: Decompose latency using Queueing Theory: End-to-End Latency = Queue Time + Prefill Time + (Tokens * TPOT). Service time per request = 1s + (1000 * 30ms) = 31 seconds. With a max batch size of 8, the system's maximum service rate (mu) = 8 requests / 31 seconds = 0.258 QPS. The arrival rate (lambda) is 2 QPS. Because lambda > mu (2 > 0.258), the system is in a queueing death spiral; the queue grows infinitely. The architecture must be redesigned to increase capacity: either scale horizontally by adding 8x more replicas (2 / 0.258 = 7.7), or implement KV cache quantization (e.g., FP8/INT4) to saf
napkin_math: Request service time = 1s + 30s = 31s. Max throughput at batch 8 = 8 / 31s = 0.258 QPS. System is receiving 2 QPS. Deficit = 1.74 requests per second added to queue. After 10 minutes, queue has 1044 requests. Queue wait time = 1044 / 0.258 = 4046 seconds.

title: Long Context Attention FLOPs Wall
scenario: You are deploying an 8B parameter model to process massive 500k-token document contexts. The SLA requires a TTFT of < 10 seconds. You use FlashAttention-2, which successfully prevents OOM on your 80GB A100 GPU. However, the prefill TTFT is over 6 minutes, completely failing the SLA.
common_mistake: Assuming FlashAttention reduces compute complexity, and trying to tune the block size to speed up the operation.
realistic_solution: Decompose the latency of the prefill step: FlashAttention reduces memory complexity from O(N^2) to O(N), but the compute complexity remains O(N^2). For N = 500,000, the number of FLOPs required just for the attention mechanism is astronomically high. A single A100 delivers ~312 TFLOPs in BF16. The attention compute alone takes hundreds of seconds. The architectural requirement is to break the O(N^2) compute bound. You must either implement RingAttention/Striped Attention to distribute the sequence across a cluster of GPUs (parallelizing the O(N^2) compute), or switch the model architecture to
napkin_math: Attention FLOPs per layer = 4 * N^2 * d. Let N=500k, d=128, heads=32, layers=32. N^2 = 2.5e11. FLOPs/layer = 4 * 2.5e11 * 128 * 32 = ~4e15 (4 PetaFLOPs). Total FLOPs = 32 * 4 PetaFLOPs = 128 PetaFLOPs. A100 BF16 = 312 TFLOPs/s. Time = 128,000 / 312 = ~410 seconds (> 6 minutes).

title: The Edge Fleet Compilation Dilemma
scenario: Your cloud platform manages a fleet of 50,000 retail smart cameras across 4 different hardware generations (varying NPUs and OS versions). You push daily updates for a 45MB object detection model. Currently, the cloud pushes the raw ONNX model, and the edge device compiles it locally using TensorRT/TFLite. This causes a 10-minute downtime per device, spiking edge CPU to 100% and causing thermal throttling. You want to move compilation to the cloud, but your initial test of cloud-side Ahead-Of-Time (AOT) compilation resulted in a massive CI/CD bottleneck and deployment failures on 15% of the fl
common_mistake: Assuming cloud AOT compilation just means running the compiler once in a Docker container and serving the binary.
realistic_solution:
Edge compilers like TensorRT and TFLite NPU delegates optimize heavily for the specific micro-architecture, OS kernel, and driver version of the target device. When you compile AOT in the cloud, you must cross-compile for the EXACT hardware/driver matrix. The deployment failures occurred because a binary compiled for NPU Gen 2 with Driver v1.2 will crash or fallback to CPU on Driver v1.1. 

To solve this, you must analyze the tradeoff between CI/CD complexity and edge downtime. The optimal hardware-compiler co-design approach is a 'Device Farm' or 'Hardware-in-the-Loop' (HITL) compilation pipe
napkin_math: Fleet combinations: 4 NPU gens * 3 driver versions = 12 targets. Cloud compilation time per target: 5 mins. Total CI/CD time: 60 mins (easily parallelizable). Edge payload shifts from 45MB ONNX to 12 distinct 60MB compiled binaries. Storage overhead in cloud: 720MB. Savings on edge: 10 mins of 10W CPU power (6,000 Joules) per device * 50,000 devices = 300 MJ saved per deployment.

title: The NPU Fallback Latency Cliff
scenario: You deploy an upgraded vision model (YOLOv8-nano, 3.2M parameters) to your edge devices via your cloud management platform, replacing YOLOv5n (1.9M parameters). Although YOLOv8n has slightly more parameters, its FLOP count is highly optimized (8.7 GFLOPs vs 4.5 GFLOPs). You expect inference latency to roughly double from 15ms to 30ms. Instead, device telemetry shows the latency skyrocketing to 185ms, and the edge CPU utilization spikes from 10% to 85%. Both models are compiled targeting the exact same edge NPU.
common_mistake: Assuming the latency increase is purely due to the increased parameter count or memory bandwidth saturation on the NPU.
realistic_solution:
This is a classic hardware-compiler-algorithm mismatch causing an Operator Fallback. Newer algorithms often introduce activation functions or operations that edge NPU compilers do not natively support. For example, YOLOv8 uses the SiLU (Swish) activation function, whereas YOLOv5 used SiLU in later versions but older versions used LeakyReLU or ReLU, which are universally supported by NPUs. 

When the NPU compiler encounters an unsupported op like SiLU, it partitions the computation graph. It runs the layers before the op on the NPU, copies the intermediate tensor back to the CPU DRAM, computes
napkin_math: YOLOv8n has ~60 convolutions, each followed by a SiLU. If the graph shatters 60 times: 60 syncs * 2 memory copies (NPU->CPU->NPU). Let average intermediate tensor size be 1MB. Copying 1MB over a 4GB/s bus = 0.25ms. 60 * 2 * 0.25ms = 30ms pure transfer time. Add OS context switch overhead (~1ms per sync * 60 = 60ms) + CPU compute time for SiLU (~60ms). Total overhead: 30 + 60 + 60 = 150ms. 150ms overhead + ~35ms NPU compute = ~185ms total.

title: The Hybrid Cascade Bandwidth Trap
scenario: Your smart security system uses a cloud-edge cascade. The edge device runs a tiny INT8 person detector (10ms latency). If a person is detected, it sends the 1080p JPEG frame (150KB) to a cloud API for heavy facial recognition (200ms compute latency). During a busy street festival, your cloud API latency monitoring shows the end-to-end latency (from edge detection to cloud response) spiking from a normal 300ms to over 2500ms for 99th percentile requests, despite cloud auto-scaling adding plenty of GPU instances.
common_mistake: Blaming cloud-side GPU initialization or autoscaling lag, and attempting to keep cloud instances permanently warm.
realistic_solution:
The bottleneck is the edge network uplink bandwidth and the resulting queuing delay, not the cloud compute. During the festival, the edge detector triggers continuously (e.g., 10 frames per second). Sending 150KB at 10 FPS requires 1.5 MB/s (12 Mbps) of sustained uplink bandwidth. Most residential/edge internet connections have highly constrained and asymmetric uplinks (often 5-10 Mbps max). 

When the generation rate exceeds the uplink capacity, frames queue up in the edge device's network buffer, leading to massive tail latencies before the frame even reaches the cloud. The system-level trad
napkin_math: Uplink capacity: 10 Mbps = 1.25 MB/s. Frame size: 150 KB. Max frames per second the network can handle: 1.25 MB / 0.15 MB = 8.33 FPS. If the edge model triggers at 10 FPS, the deficit is 1.66 frames per second. After 10 seconds of continuous triggering, 16.6 frames are stuck in the queue. Queue delay = 16.6 frames * (150 KB / 1.25 MB/s) = ~2.0 seconds. Total latency = 2000ms (queue) + 120ms (transmit) + 200ms (cloud compute) = ~2320ms.

title: The Unfused Dequantization Tax
scenario: You are deploying a 3B parameter LLM to premium mobile devices via your cloud app. An FP16 version (6GB) exceeds the 4GB mobile RAM limit. You apply INT4 weight-only quantization (W4A16), successfully shrinking the model to 1.5GB. However, when benchmarked on the mobile GPU, the token generation speed drops from 15 tokens/sec (measured on a 1.5GB FP16 dummy model) to a dismal 3 tokens/sec.
common_mistake: Assuming the mobile GPU lacks the compute power (ALUs) to perform INT4 arithmetic efficiently.
realistic_solution:
The severe performance degradation is caused by unfused dequantization operations leading to memory bandwidth saturation, not ALU limits. In W4A16 quantization, weights are stored in INT4 but computations are done in FP16. Mobile GPUs typically lack native INT4 matrix-multiply hardware. 

If the compiler doesn't have a fused kernel (where INT4 weights are loaded into SRAM registers, converted to FP16 on the fly, and immediately multiplied), it will materialize the dequantization as a separate graph node. This means the GPU reads 1.5GB of INT4 weights from DRAM, converts them to 6GB of FP16 wei
napkin_math: Mobile GPU Memory Bandwidth: ~50 GB/s. Fused W4A16: Read 1.5GB INT4 per token. 1.5GB / 50GB/s = 30ms per token -> ~33 tokens/sec. Unfused W4A16: Read 1.5GB (INT4) + Write 6GB (FP16) + Read 6GB (FP16) = 13.5GB of memory traffic per token. 13.5GB / 50GB/s = 270ms per token -> ~3.7 tokens/sec.

title: The Cloud-to-Edge Calibration Shift
scenario: Your cloud pipeline trains an FP32 semantic segmentation model for autonomous delivery robots. Before pushing to the edge, you run Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) to convert it to INT8 for the edge NPU. You calibrate the activation ranges using 5,000 randomly selected images from your massive global cloud dataset. On the edge, the INT8 model's accuracy is identical to FP32 during the day, but at night, the INT8 model fails completely to detect pedestrians, whereas the FP32 model handles night scenes perfectly.
common_mistake: Assuming the edge NPU has lower precision in low-light conditions due to hardware noise or thermal throttling.
realistic_solution:
The failure is due to a mismatch between the PTQ calibration dataset distribution and the specific edge operational domain, exacerbated by the mechanics of static INT8 quantization. Static quantization maps the continuous FP32 activation range [min, max] to discrete INT8 bins [-128, 127]. 

By randomly sampling from a global dataset, the calibration set is overwhelmingly dominated by bright daytime images, resulting in a large activation max value (e.g., 20.0). Nighttime images produce very small activations (e.g., max 1.5). Because the INT8 bins are scaled to accommodate the 20.0 max, the ent
napkin_math: Min-Max calibration: Range [0, 20.0]. INT8 has 256 bins (0 to 255 for uint8). Bin size = 20.0 / 255 = 0.078 per bin. Nighttime pedestrian feature activation range: [0, 0.6]. Number of bins used for nighttime features: 0.6 / 0.078 = ~7.6 bins. The model is effectively operating at 3-bit precision (8 states) during the night, leading to catastrophic information loss.

title: The NPU Context-Switching Tax
scenario: A cloud-managed edge gateway in a smart retail store processes camera streams by running 3 models sequentially per frame: Detection (10ms), Tracking (5ms), and Pose Estimation (15ms). The sum of individual model latencies is 30ms. All three models are perfectly compiled for the edge NPU. However, the measured end-to-end pipeline latency per frame is 80ms, limiting throughput to 12 FPS instead of the expected 33 FPS.
common_mistake: Blaming Python GIL overhead, image preprocessing, or PCIe transfer times for the camera frames.
realistic_solution:
The hidden latency comes from NPU SRAM weight swapping (Context Switching). Edge NPUs typically have very small on-chip SRAM (e.g., 4MB to 8MB) to save power and die area. 

If the combined weights of the 3 models (e.g., 15MB total) exceed the NPU's SRAM capacity, the NPU driver must constantly swap model weights in and out of main DRAM for every single frame. This requires tearing down the NPU context, flushing SRAM, copying new weights over the memory bus, and initializing the next model. The hardware-algorithm tradeoff requires restructuring the serving pattern: instead of running the pipel
napkin_math: Let Model sizes be 5MB each. NPU SRAM = 6MB. Swapping a model requires reading 5MB from DRAM. Memory bus bandwidth = 4 GB/s. Transfer time = 5MB / 4GB/s = 1.25ms. However, NPU driver overhead (kernel interrupts, context teardown/setup) typically adds ~15ms per swap. 3 swaps per frame * (1.25ms + 15ms) = ~49ms of pure overhead. 30ms compute + 49ms overhead = 79ms total latency per frame.

title: The Asymmetric Heterogeneous Offload
scenario: You deploy a Vision Transformer (ViT-B, 12 blocks) to a mobile SoC via your cloud platform. The compiler analyzes the graph and offloads the heavy Linear (MatMul) layers to the powerful NPU, while mapping the LayerNorm and Softmax operations to the CPU, as the NPU lacks hardware support for them. The resulting latency is 140ms. If you force the compiler to run the entire model on the much weaker mobile GPU, the latency is actually better at 90ms.
common_mistake: Assuming the mobile CPU is just too slow to calculate LayerNorm and Softmax compared to the GPU.
realistic_solution:
The performance degradation is driven by graph partitioning synchronization overhead, not compute capability. Because a ViT has 12 blocks, and each block contains LayerNorm and Softmax interspersed between Linear layers, the compiler fractures the computation graph into dozens of tiny subgraphs. 

Execution bounces back and forth between the NPU and CPU (NPU -> CPU for LayerNorm -> NPU for Matmul -> CPU for Softmax...). Each boundary crossing requires CPU-NPU synchronization, cache flushing, and tensor copying via shared memory. The hardware-compiler-algorithm co-design tradeoff is to either m
napkin_math: ViT-B has 12 transformer blocks. Each block has 2 LayerNorms and 1 Softmax. Total boundaries crossed = 12 * 3 = 36 round trips (72 context switches). If an NPU-CPU sync takes 1.5ms: 72 * 1.5ms = 108ms of pure synchronization overhead. NPU compute might only be 20ms, and CPU compute 12ms. Total: 108 + 20 + 12 = 140ms. GPU executes everything in a single kernel launch: 90ms compute, 0ms sync.

title: The Paged Attention OOM
scenario: You are deploying a high-throughput translation service using an 8B parameter model on a single 80GB A100. Average requests are extremely short: 15 prompt tokens and 15 decode tokens. To maximize contiguous memory reads, you configure PagedAttention with a block size of 512 tokens. However, the system OOMs at just 64 concurrent requests, falling drastically short of the expected >1000 QPS theoretical capacity.
common_mistake: Assuming the OOM is caused by the model weights or activation memory during the forward pass.
realistic_solution: The root cause is severe internal memory fragmentation due to an oversized PagedAttention block size. PagedAttention allocates memory in fixed-size blocks. If a request only needs a few tokens, it still consumes an entire block. With a block size of 512 tokens and an average sequence length of 30 tokens, each request allocates a full 512-token block, leaving 482 tokens of KV cache capacity completely wasted. At 64 concurrent requests, this internal fragmentation wastes gigabytes of VRAM, prematurely exhausting the KV cache pool and causing an OOM. The solution is to reduce the block size to 16
napkin_math: KV size per token (8B model, 32 layers, 8 KV heads, 128 dim) = 32 * 8 * 128 * 2 (K+V) * 2 bytes = 131 KB. Block size = 512 tokens. Memory per block = 512 * 131 KB = ~67 MB. For a 30-token request, we use 30 * 131 KB = 3.9 MB, but allocate 67 MB. Wasted memory per request = 63.1 MB. For 64 requests, wasted memory = 64 * 63.1 MB = ~4 GB. While 4 GB doesn't seem like enough to OOM an 80GB card on its own, if the KV cache pool was conservatively sized (e.g., 5GB allocated after weights/activations), this internal fragmentation instantly depletes it.

title: The Speculative Decoding Paradox
scenario: You serve LLaMA-3-70B on an 8xH100 cluster. Baseline throughput is 2000 tokens/sec. To improve latency, you enable speculative decoding with a 7B draft model. At low load (1 QPS), Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT) drops impressively from 40ms to 20ms. However, during peak traffic (50 QPS), the total system throughput collapses to 1200 tokens/sec, and request queues build up rapidly.
common_mistake: Assuming the draft model has a low acceptance rate, causing redundant compute without checking system utilization.
realistic_solution: Speculative decoding fundamentally trades compute (FLOPS) and memory bandwidth for latency. Under low load, the GPU is underutilized, so the draft model's extra computations are effectively 'free', yielding lower latency. However, under high load with continuous batching, the system becomes compute and memory-bandwidth bound. The 7B draft model consumes precious SM cycles and HBM bandwidth to generate its tokens, competing directly with the 70B model. Furthermore, the draft model's KV cache consumes VRAM, reducing the maximum possible batch size for the target model. This overhead reduces the
napkin_math: Assume baseline 70B decode takes 15ms/token at BS=128 (memory bound). Draft 7B generates 4 tokens. Target 70B verifies 4 tokens in one forward pass. At low load (BS=1): Draft takes 4 * 3ms = 12ms. Target verifies in 15ms. Total 27ms for ~3 accepted tokens = 9ms/token (better than 15ms). At high load (BS=128): HBM bandwidth is saturated. Draft model requires 4 separate memory-bound passes, consuming bandwidth. Target model requires 1 pass. The extra FLOPS and bandwidth consumed by the draft model reduce the maximum sustainable batch size from 128 to, say, 64, cutting overall throughput (tokens/

title: Chunked Prefill Latency Spikes
scenario: Serving Mixtral 8x7B on 2xH100s, your decode TPOT is a stable 30ms. To prevent long prompts (up to 16k tokens) from blocking decodes, you enable chunked prefill with a chunk size of 4096 tokens. P99 TTFT improves, but active decode requests suddenly experience severe jitter, with TPOT repeatedly spiking to 120ms whenever a chunked prefill is processed alongside them.
common_mistake: Thinking the chunk size is too small and causing attention overhead, or that the KV cache is fragmenting.
realistic_solution: The latency spikes are caused by co-scheduling a heavily compute-bound task (the 4096-token prefill chunk) with memory-bound tasks (the decode steps). While chunked prefill prevents a massive 16k token prefill from stalling the system for seconds, a 4096-token chunk still represents a massive dense matrix multiplication. The GPU's Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) become completely saturated executing the prefill GEMMs. Because the decode tokens are batched together with this prefill chunk, they must wait for the entire prefill chunk's forward pass to complete before the next iteration can begin
napkin_math: Mixtral 8x7B active parameters per token = ~14B. FLOPs for a 4096 token prefill chunk = 2 * 14B * 4096 = ~114 TFLOPs. On H100 (assuming ~1000 TFLOPS achievable BF16 compute), this prefill chunk takes exactly 114 / 1000 = 114ms to compute. Because the decode step (which only takes 30ms of memory-bound work) is fused in the same execution engine step as the prefill chunk, the decode tokens are delayed by the 114ms prefill execution, resulting in the observed 120ms TPOT spikes.

title: The Poisoned Prefix Cache
scenario: A multi-turn customer support chatbot on 4xA100s uses RadixAttention (prefix caching). The average conversation has 10 turns, accumulating up to 8k tokens. You expect a high KV cache hit rate (~80%) because the massive 2k-token system prompt and previous history are reused. However, metrics show the prefix cache hit rate is stuck at 0%, and VRAM is exhausted at just 20 concurrent chats.
common_mistake: Assuming RadixAttention is evicting the cache too early due to a small LRU cache size.
realistic_solution: The root cause is 'prefix poisoning' due to dynamic header injection. RadixAttention (and prefix caching in general) relies on strict, exact token-by-token prefix matching starting from the very first token (index 0). If the application layer prepends a unique identifier—such as a UUID, timestamp, or unique user ID—at the very beginning of the prompt for logging or tracing, it instantly breaks the prefix match for the entire sequence. Because the first token differs, the Radix tree branches immediately, and none of the subsequent 2000 tokens of the system prompt or history can be shared. Movin
napkin_math: Without prefix caching: 20 concurrent chats * 8000 tokens * 1 MB/token (approx for 70B model) = 160 GB of KV cache required. This exceeds available VRAM, causing OOM. With successful prefix caching (assuming UUID is moved): The 2000-token system prompt is stored exactly once. Shared memory = 2000 * 1 MB = 2 GB. The unique portions = 20 chats * 6000 tokens * 1 MB = 120 GB. Total memory = 122 GB, which safely fits in the KV cache pool, preventing the OOM.

title: Continuous Batching Generation Deadlock
scenario: Your vLLM instance serving LLaMA-3-8B allocates 40GB to the KV cache pool. The scheduler admits 100 concurrent requests, which initially fits fine during prefill. However, 10 seconds into generation, throughput drops to zero and the engine stalls. Profiling shows no OOM error, but all active requests are paused and no new tokens are being generated.
common_mistake: Suspecting a hardware NVLink hang or a NCCL timeout during tensor parallelism.
realistic_solution: This is a classic KV cache oversubscription deadlock in continuous batching systems. The scheduler admitted 100 requests based on their *prefill* length, but did not reserve enough KV cache blocks for their maximum potential *decode* lengths. As the 100 requests generated tokens simultaneously, they consumed the remaining free KV cache blocks. Once the pool was fully depleted, no request could allocate the block needed for its next token. Because all requests are active and waiting for blocks, and none can finish to release blocks, the system deadlocks. The fix is configuring the scheduler to
napkin_math: Total KV pool = 40GB. Block size = 16 tokens (approx 2MB). Total blocks = 20,000. 100 requests are admitted. Average prefill = 500 tokens. Initial blocks used = 100 * (500/16) = 3,125 blocks. Remaining blocks = 16,875. If all 100 requests attempt to generate 3000 tokens, they will need 100 * (3000/16) = 18,750 blocks.
