To prevent stale updates from destabilizing the global model, the cloud applies a staleness penalty function to the learning rate of delayed updates. Furthermore, the cloud orchestrator should implement a power-aware device selection heuristic, caching the global model on edge nodes during high-power states and allowing them to train offline.
napkin_math: Synchronous FedAvg requires a quorum (e.g., 1000 devices). If probability of staying online for 5 mins is 0.15, expected completions = 150. Quorum fails. With FedAsync, if 150 devices complete every 5 minutes, the global model updates 150 times. If a device takes 60 minutes to return an update, staleness is 12 global steps. Cloud applies penalty: lr = base_lr * (1 / (1 + staleness)) = base_lr * 0.076, safely incorporating the stale gradient without divergence.

title: Edge-Cloud Partitioning for Acoustic Monitoring
scenario: An acoustic monitoring fleet uses Cortex-M0+ devices (128KB SRAM) to classify 50 complex bird calls. The M0+ cannot run the full 2MB ResNet model. Transmitting raw audio to the cloud costs 200mJ per event. Running a local lightweight model costs 5mJ but only achieves 70% accuracy on rare birds. The system detects 1000 events/day.
common_mistake: Sending all 1000 audio events to the cloud to guarantee high accuracy, ignoring the massive energy cost.
realistic_solution: Co-design a cascaded early-exit architecture. The edge device runs a lightweight binary classifier (bird vs. noise) or a small N-way classifier for the most common birds. If the local model's softmax confidence is below a defined threshold (e.g., 0.8), the edge device extracts compressed audio features (e.g., MFCCs) and transmits them to the cloud for the heavy ResNet inference. This dramatically reduces communication power while maintaining overall system accuracy, as only uncertain or rare events incur the 200mJ transmission penalty.
napkin_math: Send all to cloud: 1000 events * 200mJ = 200,000 mJ = 200 Joules/day. Cascaded approach: Assume 90% of events are high-confidence common birds or noise. Edge only energy: 900 * 5mJ = 4.5 J. Edge + Cloud energy: 100 * (5mJ + 200mJ) = 20.5 J. Total energy = 25 Joules/day. This represents an 87.5% reduction in energy consumption while retaining high accuracy for complex calls.

title: Memory Bottleneck in Edge Personalization
scenario: A cloud backend orchestrates on-device personalization for gesture recognition on smart rings (Cortex-M4F, 256KB SRAM). The base model is 100KB INT8. To train, standard backpropagation requires storing intermediate activations for 3 convolutional layers, which takes 150KB. The OS and BLE stack take 64KB, causing an Out-Of-Memory (OOM) crash during training.
common_mistake: Attempting to implement gradient checkpointing or moving activations to slow external flash memory during the backward pass.
realistic_solution: The cloud must orchestrate Partial Federated Learning (Transfer Learning). The architecture is modified so the cloud freezes the entire convolutional feature extractor. The edge device only computes gradients for a tiny fully connected head (e.g., 10 classes). Furthermore, the edge device runs a single forward pass over the local dataset to compute and cache the output vectors of the frozen feature extractor. During training, the edge only loads these cached 1D vectors and updates the final layer, completely bypassing the need to store massive 3D conv activations.
napkin_math: SRAM capacity: 256KB. Base model: 100KB. OS/BLE: 64KB. Available SRAM: 92KB. Full backprop activations: 150KB (OOM). Transfer learning approach: Feature extractor output vector is 1x256 INT8 = 256 bytes per sample. Training a 256 -> 10 FC layer requires 2.5KB of weights + gradients. Caching a mini-batch of 32 vectors = 8KB. Total memory needed for training: 100KB + 64KB + 2.5KB + 8KB = 174.5KB. Leaves ~81KB of SRAM free.

title: Cloud Drift Detection via Edge Proxies
scenario: You deploy 1M industrial vibration sensors running a CMSIS-DSP/TFLite Micro anomaly detection model. The cloud must monitor for concept drift to trigger retraining. However, uploading raw 16kHz vibration data requires 256kbps continuous bandwidth, which is impossible over the available 10kbps LoRa link.
common_mistake: Sampling raw data randomly (e.g., 1 second of audio per day) and sending it to the cloud, which misses transient anomalies and still consumes high bandwidth.
realistic_solution: Co-design the edge inference graph to compute a lightweight statistical drift proxy alongside the main model. Instead of sending raw data, the edge device calculates the autoencoder reconstruction error, output softmax entropy, or feature embedding distances over a rolling window. It then transmits a tiny summary statistic (e.g., a 4-byte float representing daily average entropy) to the cloud. The cloud aggregates these telemetry proxies from the 1M devices to identify fleets experiencing drift and selectively triggers targeted data collection or FL rounds only on those specific devices.
napkin_math: Raw data: 16,000 samples/sec * 16-bit = 256 kbps. Over 1 day = 2.7 GB per device (impossible on LoRa). Drift proxy: Edge computes mean reconstruction MSE every minute. Averages it hourly. Sends 24 floats (96 bytes) per day. 96 bytes at 10kbps takes < 0.1 seconds to transmit. Cloud processes 96 bytes * 1M devices = ~96 MB/day of highly actionable drift metadata.

title: Mixed-Precision Federated Aggregation
scenario: A cloud system aggregates weights for Federated Learning on Cortex-M0+ devices (no FPU). The edge model must be INT8 for inference speed and memory. However, computing gradients and updating weights strictly in INT8 leads to catastrophic catastrophic quantization error and training divergence. The cloud must orchestrate updates without requiring FP32 on the edge.
common_mistake: Forcing the edge device to emulate FP32 in software for the backward pass, causing inference latency to spike by 50x and draining the battery.
realistic_solution: The architecture must utilize mixed-precision co-design. The edge device uses INT8 for the forward pass (inference), but uses 16-bit fixed-point (INT16) or block floating-point arithmetic exclusively for the trainable layer's gradients and weight updates. The edge transmits these INT16 weight deltas to the cloud. The cloud, having abundant compute, receives the INT16 updates, casts them to FP32, performs the global aggregation (FedAvg), updates the global FP32 model, applies Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) to convert it back to INT8, and pushes the INT8 model back to the edge.
napkin_math: Software FP32 emulation on Cortex-M0+ takes ~100 cycles per instruction vs 1 cycle for INT8/INT16 ALU ops. If a backward pass requires 50,000 MACs, FP32 takes 5M cycles (100ms at 50MHz). INT16 takes 50k cycles (1ms). By using INT16 locally, we preserve accuracy (16 bits provides enough dynamic range for small gradients) while maintaining a 100x speedup over FP32 emulation. Cloud handles the heavy FP32 aggregation.

title: The Overclocking Energy Trap
scenario: Your team is training a 100B parameter model on 512 A100 GPUs. To meet a deadline, the infrastructure team applies a 15% GPU frequency boost (overclock). The training time successfully decreases from 30 days to 26 days. However, the total energy consumed for the run (in kWh) unexpectedly increases by nearly 40%.
common_mistake: Assuming energy consumption scales linearly with training time, so a shorter run should use less energy.
realistic_solution: The root cause lies in the CMOS power equation: Dynamic Power (P) = α · C · V² · f. To achieve a 15% increase in frequency (f), the GPU must operate at a higher voltage (V) to maintain stability. Because power scales linearly with frequency but *quadratically* with voltage, the dynamic power draw increases cubically. Furthermore, the higher power dissipation increases the silicon temperature, which exponentially increases the static (leakage) power. Total energy is Power × Time. Even though the time decreased to 0.86x, the total power draw increased to ~1.6x, resulting in a net increase in tot
napkin_math: Original: f=1.0, V=1.0 -> P=1.0. Time = 30 days. Energy = 1.0 × 30 = 30 units. Overclocked: f=1.15, requiring V=1.12 to be stable. New Dynamic Power = 1.12² × 1.15 ≈ 1.44x. Add 15% increase in leakage power due to heat -> Total Power ≈ 1.6x. New Time = 26 days (0.86x). New Energy = 1.6 × 26 = 41.6 units. 41.6 / 30 = 1.38 (38% increase in total energy).

title: The Quantization Cost Paradox
scenario: You serve a Llama-3-70B model. To cut costs, you switch weights from FP16 to W8A8 INT8 quantization, reducing memory from 140GB to 70GB. This allows you to serve the model on 2x A100s instead of 4x A100s per node. You expect serving costs per token to halve. Instead, the cost per 1K tokens increases from $0.0006 to $0.0010.
common_mistake: Assuming cost per token is strictly proportional to the number of GPUs or the total memory footprint of the model.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the collapse of serving throughput (tokens/sec) due to unoptimized INT8 compute kernels. Cost per token is determined by: (GPU Hourly Cost) / (Throughput × 3600). While you halved the GPU hourly cost by moving from 4 to 2 GPUs, the lack of optimized W8A8 kernels forces the hardware to continuously dequantize weights back to FP16 in SRAM before matrix multiplication. This makes the generation phase heavily memory-bandwidth bound and fails to utilize INT8 Tensor Cores. Throughput drops by more than 70%. Because the 2 GPUs are still drawing near-peak power (400W each) thrashing
napkin_math: FP16 Baseline: 4 GPUs @ $2/hr = $8/hr. Throughput = 4,000 tokens/sec. Cost per 1K tokens = $8 / (4000 × 3600 / 1000) = $0.00055. INT8 Setup: 2 GPUs @ $2/hr = $4/hr. Throughput plummets to 1,100 tokens/sec due to dequantization overhead. Cost per 1K tokens = $4 / (1100 × 3600 / 1000) = $0.00101.

title: The Federated Battery Drain
scenario: You deploy a Federated Learning (FL) system to 50,000 mobile devices. To save user battery life, you aggressively prune the model, reducing the update payload from 50MB to 5MB. To maintain convergence with the pruned model, you increase the sync frequency from once every 100 local steps to once every 10 local steps. Battery drain per FL round increases by 250%.
common_mistake: Assuming network energy consumption is strictly proportional to the number of bytes transferred over the cellular network.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the Radio Resource Control (RRC) tail energy penalty inherent to cellular networks (4G/5G). When a device transmits data, the cellular radio enters a high-power active state. After transmission finishes, the radio remains in a high-power 'tail' state for 10-15 seconds before dropping back to idle, to avoid the latency of waking up for subsequent packets. By reducing the payload size by 10x but increasing the frequency by 10x, the total bytes transferred remains the same, but you incur the 10-15 second RRC tail energy penalty 10 times more often. The energy spent idling in the
napkin_math: Active radio power = 2.5W. Tail power = 1.5W for 10s (15 Joules per transmission). Original: 1 transfer of 50MB ≈ 5s active (12.5J) + 1 tail (15J) = 27.5 Joules per 100 steps. New: 10 transfers of 5MB ≈ 0.5s active (1.25J) + 1 tail (15J) = 16.25 Joules per transfer. 10 transfers = 162.5 Joules per 100 steps. 162.5 / 27.5 = ~5.9x increase in network energy.

title: The Thermal Throttling Ring
scenario: You are training a MoE model on a 64-node cluster (8x H100s per node, air-cooled). You notice that every day between 2 PM and 6 PM, global training throughput drops by 25%. Node-level CPU and RAM metrics are normal, and power supply units are well within limits. The loss curve remains perfectly smooth.
common_mistake: Suspecting network congestion, noisy neighbors, or data loader bottlenecks without checking GPU temperatures and clock speeds.
realistic_solution: The root cause is thermal throttling triggering dynamic voltage and frequency scaling (DVFS) during peak afternoon ambient temperatures. In dense, air-cooled 8-GPU servers, the GPUs at the rear of the chassis ingest pre-heated air from the front GPUs. During the hottest part of the day, facility cooling (CRAC units) struggles, raising chassis inlet temperatures. The rear GPUs hit their thermal limits (e.g., 85°C) and automatically downclock to reduce heat. Because the training uses a synchronous AllReduce operation (e.g., NCCL Ring or Tree), the entire 512-GPU pipeline is forced to wait for th
napkin_math: Normal GPU clock: 1980 MHz. Time per step: 400ms. Throttled GPU clock drops to 1485 MHz (25% reduction). Throttled GPU time per step: 400ms / 0.75 = 533ms. Because AllReduce acts as a global barrier, the whole cluster's step time becomes 533ms. Throughput (steps/sec) goes from 2.5 to 1.87 (a 25% drop).

title: The Carbon-Aware Checkpoint Penalty
scenario: You implement a carbon-aware scheduler for a 256-GPU training job. It pauses training when grid carbon intensity is high (400 gCO2/kWh) and resumes when it is low (200 gCO2/kWh). Despite successfully shifting 30% of the compute hours to 'green' energy periods, the post-run sustainability audit shows the total carbon footprint of the job actually increased by 12%.
common_mistake: Ignoring the embodied carbon of data movement and the idle power consumption of paused hardware.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the massive overhead of pausing and resuming large distributed training jobs. When the job is 'paused', the 256 GPUs remain powered on and allocated, drawing significant idle power. Additionally, pausing requires checkpointing 2TB of optimizer state and model weights to cloud storage, and resuming requires reloading them. The carbon emissions from the idle power draw over a 6-hour pause, combined with the embodied energy of the network transfer and storage IO, plus the wasted compute re-warming the caches/pipeline, completely outweighed the grid carbon intensity savings. Carb
napkin_math: Training load: 256 GPUs × 700W = 179kW. 1 hour at 400g/kWh = 71.6 kgCO2. Shifted to 200g/kWh = 35.8 kgCO2 (Saved 35.8 kgCO2 per shifted hour). Overhead: 256 GPUs idle @ 150W = 38.4kW. 6 hour pause = 230.4 kWh. At 400g/kWh = 92.1 kgCO2. Checkpoint IO/Network energy ≈ 50 kWh ≈ 20 kgCO2. Total overhead = 112.1 kgCO2. Savings = 35.8 kgCO2. Net loss = +76.3 kgCO2.

title: The PUE Illusion
scenario: Your AI datacenter operates 1024 H100s. During standard Dense LLM training (high SM utilization), the facility PUE is 1.15. You switch the workload to a memory-bound Mixture of Experts (MoE) model. The sustainability dashboard triggers an alert: the PUE has worsened to 1.35. Management demands to know why the datacenter has become 'less efficient'.
common_mistake: Assuming a higher PUE means the datacenter is consuming more total energy or that the cooling system has failed.
realistic_solution: The root cause is a mathematical artifact of how Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) is calculated: Total Facility Power / IT Equipment Power. During the memory-bound MoE workload, the GPU Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs) are frequently starved waiting for memory fetches. Because compute is low, the IT power draw drops significantly (e.g., from 700W to 350W per GPU). However, the fixed facility power overheads (lighting, control systems, baseline cooling fans, network switches) do not drop proportionally. With a much smaller denominator (IT power) and a relatively stable fixed overhead in the numer
napkin_math: Dense run: IT Power = 1024 × 700W = 716 kW. Facility Overhead (cooling/lights) = 107 kW. Total Power = 823 kW. PUE = 823 / 716 = 1.15. MoE run: IT Power drops to 350W per GPU -> 358 kW. Facility overhead drops slightly to 125 kW (less cooling needed, but fixed overhead remains). Total Power = 483 kW. PUE = 483 / 358 = 1.35. Total power dropped by 340 kW, but PUE worsened.

title: The Power Capping Headroom Paradox
scenario: Your inference cluster serves a ResNet-50 model at 10,000 QPS with an average GPU utilization of 45%. To cut electricity costs, you apply a strict 200W power cap on the 400W GPUs. Suddenly, p99 latency spikes from 50ms to over 500ms, even though average GPU utilization remains at 45% and average power draw is only 150W.
common_mistake: Assuming that because the average power draw (150W) is below the cap (200W), the power cap should have no effect on performance.
realistic_solution: The root cause is the suppression of transient power spikes needed to clear bursty inference queues. Inference traffic is rarely perfectly uniform; it arrives in bursts. To process a sudden burst of requests and maintain low p99 latency, the GPU rapidly scales up its clock frequency, causing transient power spikes that can easily hit 300W+ for a few milliseconds. By enforcing a strict hardware power cap of 200W, the GPU is prevented from boosting its frequency during these micro-bursts. The service rate drops during the exact moments the queue is longest. According to queuing theory, failing t
napkin_math: Average arrival rate λ = 10,000 QPS. Uncapped GPU burst service rate μ = 20,000 QPS. Utilization ρ = 0.5. Capped GPU service rate drops to μ = 11,000 QPS during bursts. Utilization ρ = 0.9. M/M/1 queue wait time = 1 / (μ - λ). Uncapped wait time = 1 / (20000 - 10000) = 0.1ms. Capped wait time = 1 / (11000 - 10000) = 1.0ms. In reality, bursty traffic (high variance) pushes p99 queue depths exponentially higher when ρ approaches 1 during the burst window.

title: AR Glasses Thermal Budgeting
scenario: Your team is designing ML pipelines for AR glasses. To prevent skin burns, the device has a strict continuous thermal dissipation limit (TDP) of 1.5W. The baseline OS and display draw a constant 0.8W. You need to run an eye-tracking CNN (5ms execution time at 800mW active power) and a hand-tracking transformer (15ms execution time at 1.2W active power). Both models must run at 30Hz. The ML accelerator draws 100mW when idle.
common_mistake: Adding the peak power of both models (0.8W + 1.2W + 0.8W system = 2.8W) and concluding it instantly violates the TDP.
realistic_solution: To determine if the thermal envelope is violated, we must calculate the time-averaged power consumption over a 1-second window, as thermal limits (TDP) on mobile devices are bound by sustained heat dissipation rather than instantaneous spikes. First, calculate the duty cycle (active time per second) for each model at 30Hz. The eye tracker runs for 150ms/sec, and the hand tracker runs for 450ms/sec. The NPU is active for 600ms total and idle for 400ms. We sum the energy consumed during these states and add the continuous baseline system power. The total average power comes out to 1.5W exactly,
napkin_math:
Eye-tracking: 5ms * 30 frames = 150ms active/sec. Energy = 0.8W * 0.15s = 0.12 Joules.
Hand-tracking: 15ms * 30 frames = 450ms active/sec. Energy = 1.2W * 0.45s = 0.54 Joules.
Idle time: 1000ms - 150ms - 450ms = 400ms. Idle energy = 0.1W * 0.40s = 0.04 Joules.
Total ML Energy/sec = 0.12 + 0.54 + 0.04 = 0.70 Joules (0.7W average).
Baseline system power = 0.8W.
Total average power = 0.8W + 0.7W = 1.50W. This exactly meets the 1.5W TDP limit.

title: Federated Learning Carbon Trade-offs
scenario: You are evaluating Federated Learning (FL) vs Centralized Cloud training. 1,000,000 mobile devices perform 1 FL epoch daily. Local compute takes 100 Joules/device. Each device uploads/downloads a 500MB payload over 5G (0.5 Joules/MB). Cloud aggregation uses 50 kWh total. The average device grid carbon intensity is 400 gCO2/kWh, while the cloud datacenter uses renewable energy at 50 gCO2/kWh. We need to calculate the total daily carbon footprint of the FL approach.
common_mistake: Ignoring the 5G communication energy, which heavily dominates the local compute energy in modern FL workloads.
realistic_solution: In mobile Federated Learning, the energy bottleneck is rarely the local compute; it is almost always the radio transmission cost for large model weights. To find the total carbon footprint, we must quantify the energy used by the 1 million devices (compute + 5G communication) and the energy used by the cloud aggregator. We calculate the local energy in Joules, convert it to kWh (since 1 kWh = 3.6 million Joules), and multiply by the local grid's carbon intensity. Then we add the cloud's carbon footprint. The communication energy (250J) dwarfs the compute (100J). The total system footprint is 3
napkin_math:
Local compute per device: 100 Joules.
Comm energy per device: 500MB * 0.5 J/MB = 250 Joules.
Total energy per device = 350 Joules.
Total for 1M devices = 350,000,000 Joules.
Convert to kWh: 350,000,000 J / 3,600,000 J/kWh = 97.22 kWh.
Device Carbon = 97.22 kWh * 400 gCO2/kWh = 38,888 gCO2 = ~38.9 kg CO2.
Cloud Carbon = 50 kWh * 50 gCO2/kWh = 2,500 gCO2 = 2.5 kg CO2.
Total Carbon = 38.9 + 2.5 = 41.4 kg CO2.

title: Always-On Wake Word Battery Drain
scenario: A smartphone has a 3000mAh battery operating at 3.8V. We are deploying an always-on wake-word model. Running it on the main CPU consumes a continuous 40mW. Offloading to a dedicated DSP consumes only 4mW continuously, but requires waking up the main LPDDR memory for 10ms every 1 second to flush audio buffers, consuming 200mW during that 10ms spike. Calculate the percentage of battery drained over 24 hours for the DSP approach.
common_mistake: Calculating battery capacity incorrectly (ignoring voltage) or forgetting to average the LPDDR memory spike over the 1-second window.
realistic_solution: To calculate battery drain percentage, we first need the total energy capacity of the battery in Watt-hours (Wh). A battery's capacity in mAh must be multiplied by its nominal voltage to get mWh. Next, we determine the average power consumption of the DSP approach. While the DSP uses 4mW continuously, the periodic 10ms memory wake-ups at 200mW add a time-averaged power overhead. We sum the continuous DSP power and the duty-cycled memory power to find the true average power. Finally, we multiply this average power by 24 hours to get the total energy consumed in mWh, and divide by the battery ca
napkin_math:
Battery Capacity = 3000 mAh * 3.8V = 11,400 mWh.
DSP continuous power = 4 mW.
Memory flush duty cycle = 10ms / 1000ms = 0.01 (1%).
Average memory power = 200 mW * 0.01 = 2 mW.
Total average system power = 4 mW + 2 mW = 6 mW.
Energy consumed in 24 hours = 6 mW * 24 h = 144 mWh.
Percentage drained = (144 mWh / 11,400 mWh) * 100 = 1.26%.

title: LLM DVFS and Static Power Wall
scenario: Running a 4GB mobile LLM at max memory frequency (50GB/s bandwidth) draws 4W dynamic power for RAM and 1W static system power, taking 80ms per token. To save battery, you propose dropping frequency by 50%. Due to V^2f scaling, RAM dynamic power drops to 1W, but bandwidth drops to 25GB/s. The 1W static system power remains constant. Calculate the energy per token for both the max frequency and 50% frequency states to determine if downclocking actually saves energy.
common_mistake: Assuming that because dynamic power drops by 75%, the total energy per token will also drastically decrease, ignoring the increased execution time and static power leakage.
realistic_solution: Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) reduces dynamic power significantly (proportional to V^2 * f), but it also increases the time required to complete a fixed workload (like generating one token). Because mobile SoCs have static power components (leakage current, display, background OS) that remain constant regardless of the ML workload's frequency, running slower means this static power is drawn for a longer duration. To evaluate the trade-off, we calculate the total energy (Joules = Watts * seconds) per token for both states. At max frequency, total energy is 0.40J per token. At hal
napkin_math:
Max Freq:
Time per token = 4GB / 50GB/s = 0.08s (80ms).
Total Power = 4W (dynamic) + 1W (static) = 5W.
Energy per token = 5W * 0.08s = 0.40 Joules.

Half Freq:
Time per token = 4GB / 25GB/s = 0.16s (160ms).
Total Power = 1W (dynamic) + 1W (static) = 2W.
Energy per token = 2W * 0.16s = 0.32 Joules.
Energy saved per token = 0.40 - 0.32 = 0.08 Joules (20% savings).

title: Super-Res NPU vs 5G Streaming Energy
scenario: A mobile app plays a 2-hour 4K movie. Option A: Stream native 4K. The 5G radio draws 3.0W for 4K bandwidth, and the hardware decoder draws 0.9W. Option B: Stream 1080p and use an on-device NPU for ML super-resolution to 4K. The 5G radio draws 0.8W for 1080p, the decoder draws 0.5W, and the NPU draws 1.5W for 60FPS upscaling. Calculate the total energy saved (in Joules) by using the ML super-resolution approach over the 2-hour movie.
common_mistake: Focusing only on the high NPU power (1.5W) and assuming on-device ML is worse, failing to factor in the massive energy savings from the 5G radio step-down.
realistic_solution: In mobile systems, radio transmission (especially 5G) is one of the most power-hungry components. Offloading bandwidth to local compute (like ML super-resolution) is a classic energy optimization technique if the local compute is highly efficient. We evaluate this by comparing the sum of subsystem power draws for both options. Option A (Native 4K) relies heavily on the 5G modem, resulting in a total system power of 3.9W. Option B utilizes the NPU but drastically reduces the 5G and decoding power, resulting in a total system power of 2.8W. By multiplying the power difference by the total durati
napkin_math:
Option A Power (Native 4K) = 3.0W (5G) + 0.9W (Decoder) = 3.9W.
Option B Power (ML Super-Res) = 0.8W (5G) + 0.5W (Decoder) + 1.5W (NPU) = 2.8W.
Power Savings = 3.9W - 2.8W = 1.1W.
Movie duration = 2 hours = 7200 seconds.
Total Energy Saved = 1.1 Watts * 7200 seconds = 7,920 Joules.

title: Thermal Throttling in Background Indexing
scenario: A smartphone indexes local photos using a Vision Transformer. The SoC has a 2.5W continuous TDP. The ViT runs at 30 FPS, consuming 4.0W (burst mode). To prevent overheating, the thermal governor enforces a rule: the 1-minute average power cannot exceed the 2.5W TDP. When not bursting, the SoC enters a cool-down state drawing 1.0W (processing 0 FPS). Calculate the maximum number of images the device can index per minute without violating the thermal limit.
common_mistake: Calculating throughput purely based on the 4.0W burst without realizing the duty cycle is constrained by the 1.0W cool-down floor.
realistic_solution: Mobile thermal management relies on managing energy over a sliding window. The device can burst above its TDP (4.0W > 2.5W) as long as it compensates with a low-power cool-down phase (1.0W) such that the integral of power over the window (energy) equals the TDP's maximum energy allowance. For a 60-second window, the maximum allowed energy is 150 Joules. We set up an equation where the time spent bursting plus the time spent cooling equals 60 seconds, and their combined energy equals 150 Joules. Solving this gives the maximum allowable burst time per minute. Multiplying this burst time by the m
napkin_math:
Max energy per minute = 2.5W * 60s = 150 Joules.
Let T_burst = time spent at 4.0W, T_cool = time spent at 1.0W.
T_burst + T_cool = 60s.
Energy equation: (4.0 * T_burst) + (1.0 * T_cool) = 150.
Substitute T_cool: 4.0 * T_burst + 1.0 * (60 - T_burst) = 150.
3.0 * T_burst + 60 = 150.
3.0 * T_burst = 90  =>  T_burst = 30 seconds.
The device can run the ViT for 30 seconds per minute.
Throughput = 30 seconds * 30 FPS = 900 images per minute.

title: On-Device vs Cloud LLM Energy Cost
scenario: Generating 50 tokens using an on-device 7B model takes 2.5 seconds at 5W (NPU + Memory). Alternatively, an API call to a cloud server takes 0.5s of 5G active radio time at 2.0W, followed by 1.0s of idle waiting for the response at 0.5W. The cloud server itself consumes 0.05 Joules per token. Compare the total system energy (Device + Cloud) of both approaches. How much more total energy (in Joules) does the on-device approach use compared to the cloud approach?
common_mistake: Only comparing the device energy to the cloud server energy, forgetting that the cloud approach also costs the device radio and idle energy.
realistic_solution: To determine the true energy efficiency of edge vs cloud LLM serving, we must calculate the end-to-end energy for both scenarios. For the on-device approach, all energy is consumed locally by the SoC (NPU and memory bandwidth). For the cloud approach, the energy is split: the mobile device spends energy transmitting the prompt and keeping the radio active waiting for the response, while the datacenter spends energy computing the tokens. We calculate the Joules for each component. The local generation uses 12.5 Joules. The cloud generation uses 1.5 Joules locally and 2.5 Joules in the datacente
napkin_math:
On-Device Approach:
Device Energy = 5W * 2.5s = 12.5 Joules.
Total System Energy = 12.5 Joules.

Cloud Approach:
Device Radio Energy = 2.0W * 0.5s = 1.0 Joules.
Device Idle Wait Energy = 0.5W * 1.0s = 0.5 Joules.
Total Device Energy = 1.5 Joules.
Cloud Server Energy = 50 tokens * 0.05 J/token = 2.5 Joules.
Total System Energy = 1.5J + 2.5J = 4.0 Joules.

Difference = 12.5J - 4.0J = 8.5 Joules more energy used by the on-device approach.

title: Dynamic Batching on IoT Edge Gateways
scenario: A smart home edge gateway processes security camera frames at 10 frames per second. Unbatched (Option A): Processing 1 frame takes 50ms at 3.0W. Batched (Option B): Waiting to batch 5 frames takes 200ms total at 3.5W (better vectorization efficiency). When not processing, the gateway sleeps at 0.5W. Calculate the average power consumption (Watts) over a 1-second window for both options to find the energy savings of batching.
common_mistake: Calculating only the active power (3.0W vs 3.5W) and concluding unbatched is better, completely ignoring the sleep state duty cycle.
realistic_solution: In edge ML devices, maximizing sleep time (race-to-sleep) is critical for reducing average power. Unbatched processing keeps the device waking up constantly; computing 10 frames individually at 50ms each keeps the device active for 500ms per second. Dynamic batching increases the instantaneous power draw (due to higher utilization of ALUs/vector units, drawing 3.5W instead of 3.0W) but significantly reduces the total active compute time (400ms per second for two batches of 5). By calculating the energy consumed during both the active and sleep phases over a 1-second interval, we can find the t
napkin_math:
Option A (Unbatched):
Active time = 10 frames * 50ms = 500ms (0.5s).
Active energy = 3.0W * 0.5s = 1.5 Joules.
Sleep time = 1000ms - 500ms = 500ms (0.5s).
Sleep energy = 0.5W * 0.5s = 0.25 Joules.
Total Energy/sec = 1.75 Joules (Average Power = 1.75W).

Option B (Batched):
10 frames = 2 batches of 5.
Active time = 2 batches * 200ms = 400ms (0.4s).
Active energy = 3.5W * 0.4s = 1.4 Joules.
Sleep time = 1000ms - 400ms = 600ms (0.6s).
Sleep energy = 0.5W * 0.6s = 0.30 Joules.
Total Energy/sec = 1.70 Joules (Average Power = 1.70W).

Savings = 1.75W - 1.70W = 0.05W.

title: Carbon-Aware Scheduling Tradeoffs
scenario: Your 2,000-GPU cluster in Texas runs flexible offline RLHF training. The grid's carbon intensity fluctuates daily from 200g CO2/kWh (high wind/solar) to 600g CO2/kWh (gas/coal peaks). The sustainability team proposes pausing all training for 6 hours daily during peak carbon hours to meet ESG goals. A hardware engineer objects, citing idle power and TCO. Evaluate the tradeoffs of this pause strategy.
common_mistake: Assuming pausing jobs completely during high-carbon periods will strictly reduce total carbon footprint without factoring in idle power draw.
realistic_solution: Pausing jobs is often suboptimal because a suspended GPU node still draws significant idle power (often 20-30% of peak) while accomplishing zero useful work, degrading the overall energy-efficiency metrics. Furthermore, prolonging the training time reduces the amortization of the embedded carbon (manufacturing footprint) of the GPUs over useful jobs. Instead of a full pause, a better tradeoff is implementing DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling) during peak carbon hours. Capping power to 60% of peak TDP might retain 80% of throughput due to non-linear power-to-frequency scaling (CMOS po
napkin_math: Active node: 8 GPUs * 400W + 1000W host = 4.2kW. Idle node: 8 GPUs * 75W + 500W host = 1.1kW. Pause strategy for 6h: burns 1.1kW * 6h = 6.6 kWh of dirty energy (600g/kWh) for 0 tokens. Total CO2 = 3.96 kg CO2 per node just idling! DVFS cap at 250W/GPU: Node draws 8*250 + 1000 = 3.0kW. You burn 3.0kW * 6h = 18 kWh, but retain ~80% throughput, radically improving the carbon-per-token compared to idling.

title: LLM Serving Batch Size Energy
scenario: You are serving Llama-3-70B on 8xH100 instances. At batch size 1, Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) is 20ms, and the node draws 3.5 kW. At batch size 64, TTFT rises to 400ms, and the node draws 6.5 kW. The product team demands a strict 50ms TTFT SLA. Analyze the tradeoff between meeting this SLA and the serving cost per token, and propose a solution.
common_mistake: Optimizing solely for SLA by forcing batch size 1, ignoring that memory bandwidth dominates power draw at low batch sizes and destroys perf/watt.
realistic_solution: Enforcing a strict 50ms TTFT by keeping batch sizes near 1 leads to severe underutilization of the GPU's compute capacity while still incurring massive static and memory-transfer power costs. At BS=1, the operation is heavily memory-bandwidth bound; you are paying to power the entire HBM stack and interconnect just to process a single sequence. This results in an extremely high energy cost per token and poor TCO. To balance the tradeoff, you should implement continuous batching and decouple the prefill phase from the decode phase (chunked prefill). By processing prefill chunks in parallel with
napkin_math: BS=1: 3.5 kW. Throughput = 50 tokens/sec. Energy/token = 3500 W / 50 tps = 70 Joules/token. BS=64: 6.5 kW. Throughput = 2000 tokens/sec. Energy/token = 6500 W / 2000 tps = 3.25 Joules/token. Operating at BS=64 is ~21.5x more energy efficient per token. If you charge per token, BS=1 ruins unit economics.

title: Overprovisioning Under Power Caps
scenario: You are designing a new cluster with a hard 10 MW facility limit. You can either deploy 10,000 GPUs running at their full 1000W TDP, or overprovision by buying 14,000 GPUs and aggressively power-capping them to 700W each. Both scenarios max out the 10 MW limit. Evaluate the tradeoff between cluster throughput and TCO.
common_mistake: Assuming throughput scales linearly with power, leading to the false conclusion that 10,000 GPUs at 1000W yield the same throughput as 14,000 GPUs at 700W.
realistic_solution: Power and clock frequency scale non-linearly. Because dynamic power scales with the cube of frequency/voltage ($P \propto fV^2$), reducing the power cap by 30% (from 1000W to 700W) typically only reduces the GPU clock frequency and overall throughput by about 10-15%. Therefore, overprovisioning the data center with 14,000 GPUs running at 700W will yield significantly higher total cluster throughput than 10,000 GPUs at 1000W. The tradeoff, however, is Capital Expenditure (CapEx) and networking. You must purchase 4,000 additional GPUs, switches, and host nodes. You must calculate if the ~19% gai
napkin_math: Option A: 10,000 GPUs * 1000W = 10 MW. Perf = 10,000 units. Option B: 14,000 GPUs * 700W = 9.8 MW. At 700W (70% TDP), GPUs retain ~85% performance. Perf = 14,000 * 0.85 = 11,900 units. Option B yields 19% more total throughput for the same 10 MW power envelope, but increases CapEx by 40%.

title: Liquid Cooling Retrofit ROI
scenario: Your 5 MW (IT load) datacenter currently relies on air cooling, operating at a Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) of 1.5. A proposal suggests retrofitting to Direct-to-Chip (D2C) liquid cooling, which would lower the PUE to 1.15 but costs $8,000,000 in CapEx. Electricity costs $0.12 per kWh. Evaluate the financial and operational tradeoffs of this retrofit.
common_mistake: Calculating savings based on the IT load (5 MW) rather than the overhead power saved via the PUE delta, or ignoring the payback period entirely.
realistic_solution: Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) is the ratio of total facility power to IT equipment power. A PUE of 1.5 means for every 1 Watt of IT load, 0.5 Watts is spent on cooling and overhead. Retrofitting to liquid cooling drops the overhead from 50% to 15%. However, evaluating this tradeoff requires calculating the payback period against the CapEx. While liquid cooling vastly improves energy-efficiency metrics and allows for denser racks (reducing network cable lengths and latency), an $8M retrofit for a legacy facility might have a payback period that exceeds the remaining lifespan of the current ha
napkin_math: Current Total Power = 5 MW * 1.5 PUE = 7.5 MW. New Total Power = 5 MW * 1.15 PUE = 5.75 MW. Power Savings = 1.75 MW. Annual Energy Savings = 1,750 kW * 8760 hours/yr = 15,330,000 kWh/yr. Financial Savings = 15,330,000 * $0.12 = $1,839,600 / year. Payback Period = $8,000,000 / $1,839,600 ≈ 4.35 years.

title: Model Compression Carbon ROI
scenario: Your team serves a 175B parameter FP16 model, processing 5 million requests daily. An engineer proposes distilling and quantizing the model to INT8 to save inference power. The retraining/distillation process will require 512 A100 GPUs running continuously for 14 days. Evaluate the carbon footprint tradeoff: when do we break even on the carbon emitted during retraining?
common_mistake: Assuming quantization always yields an immediate carbon benefit without quantifying the massive upfront emission cost of the retraining and distillation process.
realistic_solution: Evaluating the carbon ROI requires comparing the one-time carbon cost of retraining against the recurring carbon savings during inference. Distillation and Quantization-Aware Training (QAT) are highly compute-intensive. While INT8 reduces memory bandwidth requirements and utilizes efficient tensor cores, lowering the per-token inference energy, the upfront training emissions are massive. If the model is updated frequently (e.g., retrained monthly) or has low inference traffic, the project will be carbon-negative overall. The tradeoff balances model accuracy, upfront compute cost (CapEx/Carbon)
napkin_math: Retraining: 512 GPUs * 400W * 1.5 PUE = ~307 kW. For 14 days (336h): 307 * 336 = 103,152 kWh. Inference savings: INT8 saves ~200W per 8-GPU node. Processing 5M requests takes ~10,000 node-hours/day. Savings = 10,000 * 200W = 2,000 kWh/day. Break-even = 103,152 kWh / 2,000 kWh/day = ~51.5 days. If the model lifespan > 2 months, it is carbon-positive.

title: Cloud vs Edge FL Energy Tradeoff
scenario: To improve data privacy, your team proposes moving a personalization model's training from a centralized cloud cluster to Federated Learning (FL) across 500,000 user smartphones. Cloud training takes 500 GPU-hours on H100s. FL requires 10 rounds of training, with each smartphone computing for 2 hours per round. Analyze the energy and carbon tradeoffs of this architectural shift.
common_mistake: Believing FL is greener because edge devices don't have cooling overhead (PUE=1.0), ignoring the severe compute inefficiency of mobile SoCs compared to datacenter GPUs.
realistic_solution: While Federated Learning avoids the energy cost of transferring massive datasets to the cloud and benefits from an effective PUE of 1.0 (smartphones passively cool into the air), edge processors are orders of magnitude less energy-efficient per FLOP than datacenter accelerators like the H100. Furthermore, FL introduces significant communication overhead; transmitting weight updates across cellular/WiFi networks consumes massive amounts of energy. The tradeoff heavily favors the centralized cloud for total energy efficiency. Shifting to FL is an active choice to prioritize data privacy and data
napkin_math: Cloud: 500 hours * 700W (GPU) * 1.3 PUE = ~455 kWh. FL Edge Compute: 500,000 phones * 2 hours/round * 10 rounds * 4W (SoC active power) = 40,000,000 Wh = 40,000 kWh. FL Network transfer: 500,000 phones * 100MB/round * 10 rounds = 500 TB transferred. At ~0.1 kWh/GB for mobile networks = 50,000 kWh. Total FL Energy = 90,000 kWh. FL consumes ~200x more energy than the cloud.

title: Rack-Level Thermal Colocation
scenario: Your infrastructure has 15 kW power limits per rack. You run two workloads: LLM inference (memory-bandwidth bound, low Tensor Core utilization, draws 350W/GPU) and offline batched MatMuls (compute-bound, heavily utilizes Tensor Cores, draws 650W/GPU). A standard node has 8 GPUs and uses ~2 kW of host overhead. Propose a scheduling strategy to maximize rack utilization without tripping the 15 kW breakers.
common_mistake: Provisioning racks homogeneously based on the worst-case TDP (650W), resulting in only 1 node per rack and severely underutilizing the facility footprint.
realistic_solution: If you deploy homogeneous compute-bound nodes, a single node draws (8 * 650W) + 2000W = 7.2 kW. You can only fit two nodes (14.4 kW) per 15 kW rack. If you deploy inference nodes, a node draws (8 * 350W) + 2000W = 4.8 kW. You could fit three nodes (14.4 kW). To maximize data center throughput and rack density, you should implement spatial workload colocation: scheduling a mix of memory-bound and compute-bound workloads on the *same* node or within the *same* rack. By pairing workloads that stress different hardware components (HBM vs. SMs), you smooth out the power draw. The optimal tradeoff i
napkin_math: Compute Node = 7.2 kW. Inference Node = 4.8 kW. If homogeneous: you get 2 compute nodes (16 GPUs) or 3 inference nodes (24 GPUs) per rack. Mixed Node: Schedule 4 inference GPUs (4*350=1.4kW) and 4 compute GPUs (4*650=2.6kW) per node. Node power = 1.4 + 2.6 + 2.0 (host) = 6.0 kW. You can now safely fit 2.5 nodes (or strictly 2 mixed + 1 inference) per rack, drastically improving fleet utilization without tripping the 15 kW breaker.

title: Federated Learning Energy Optimization
scenario: You are deploying a 10MB Federated Learning model on 100,000 edge devices. Local training costs 5mJ per batch (1,000 batches/epoch). Network transmission costs 500mJ/MB. The baseline uses 1 local epoch per round, converging in 200 rounds. A new proposal uses 5 local epochs per round, converging in 80 rounds. Evaluate the total energy cost per device to reach convergence.
common_mistake: Assuming that doing 5x more local compute per round will inevitably burn more total battery life.
realistic_solution: Calculate the energy per round for both strategies, then multiply by the number of rounds to find the total energy footprint. In Federated Learning, communication (transmitting weights) is often significantly more expensive than local computation. By doing more local work (epochs) per round, you reduce the total number of global syncs required. You must evaluate the sum of compute and communication energy over the entire convergence lifecycle. The proposal with 5 epochs actually saves total energy because the massive reduction in communication rounds more than offsets the increase in local com
napkin_math: Compute per epoch = 5mJ * 1000 = 5J. Comms per round = 10MB * 500mJ/MB * 2 (upload+download) = 10J. Baseline (1 epoch): 5J compute + 10J comms = 15J/round. Total = 15J * 200 rounds = 3000J. Proposal (5 epochs): 25J compute + 10J comms = 35J/round. Total = 35J * 80 rounds = 2800J. The proposal saves 200J per device.

title: AR Glasses Thermal Management via DVFS
scenario: An AR headset runs a hand-tracking CNN at 60 FPS (16.6ms budget). At max frequency (1.0 GHz, 1.0V), inference takes 10ms. Dynamic power is 1.5W and static power is 0.5W. The headset has a strict thermal design power (TDP) limit of 1.2W. You must evaluate Race-to-Sleep (run at max clock, then sleep) vs DVFS (downclock to 0.6 GHz, 0.6V) to meet the TDP constraint.
common_mistake: Believing race-to-sleep is always optimal because it maximizes the time spent in the low-power static state.
realistic_solution: Use the CMOS power equation (P_dyn ∝ V^2 * f) to analyze DVFS. Race-to-sleep averages the high active power with the low sleep power, but due to the V^2 factor, lowering frequency and voltage yields cubic power savings for the dynamic portion. DVFS stretches the execution to fill the deadline but drastically reduces dynamic power. In heavily thermally constrained edge environments, DVFS keeps the device well under the thermal limit without dropping frames, whereas race-to-sleep violates the TDP.
napkin_math: Race-to-Sleep: 10ms active (1.5W dyn + 0.5W static = 2.0W) + 6.6ms sleep (0.5W). Avg Power = ((10 * 2.0) + (6.6 * 0.5)) / 16.6 = 23.3 / 16.6 = 1.4W (Exceeds 1.2W TDP). DVFS: Freq drops to 0.6x, so time is 10/0.6 = 16.6ms. Voltage drops to 0.6x. P_dyn = 1.5W * (0.6)^2 * (0.6) = 0.324W. Total Power = 0.324W + 0.5W = 0.824W (Safely under 1.2W TDP).

title: Smart Camera Carbon Payback Period
scenario: A smart city operates 10,000 traffic cameras sending 1080p video to a cloud server. The continuous network transmission draws 6W per camera. A proposal suggests upgrading to edge AI modules (embodied carbon: 20 kg CO2e each) that draw 2W to process video locally and only transmit 1KB/s of metadata. The local grid carbon intensity is 400 gCO2/kWh. Evaluate the carbon payback period of this edge upgrade.
common_mistake: Only calculating operational energy savings and ignoring the embodied carbon cost of manufacturing the new edge hardware.
realistic_solution: Perform a lifecycle carbon assessment. Calculate the operational energy saved by eliminating the continuous video stream, convert it to carbon emissions using the grid's specific carbon intensity, and divide the embodied carbon of the new hardware by the annual operational carbon savings. The edge deployment is highly sustainable and justified if the carbon payback period is significantly shorter than the hardware's expected 3-5 year lifespan.
napkin_math: Power saved = 6W - 2W = 4W per camera. Annual energy saved = 4W * 24h * 365d = 35,040 Wh = 35.04 kWh. Annual carbon saved = 35.04 kWh * 0.4 kgCO2/kWh = 14.016 kg CO2e/year. Payback period = Embodied Carbon / Annual Savings = 20 kg / 14.016 kg/yr = 1.42 years.

title: Autonomous Vehicle Dynamic Power Capping
scenario: An autonomous vehicle edge cluster has 4 identical SoC nodes with a shared physical power budget of 200W. Normally, 3 perception nodes use 50W each (40W dynamic, 10W static) running at 30 FPS (20ms latency), and 1 planning node uses 30W. A routing failure causes the planning node to spike to 65W. Evaluate applying a 10% frequency reduction (and 10% voltage reduction) to the 3 perception nodes to prevent a system shutdown while maintaining 30 FPS.
common_mistake: Assuming a 10% frequency reduction only yields a 10% power reduction, which would fail to bring the system under the 200W budget.
realistic_solution: Applying a uniform 10% DVFS step to the perception nodes leverages the non-linear relationship between frequency/voltage and dynamic power. Since inference latency increases linearly but dynamic power decreases cubically (P ∝ V^2 * f), you can harvest massive power savings with minimal latency impact. By downclocking the perception nodes, the system safely absorbs the planning node's power spike while still comfortably meeting the 33.3ms deadline required for 30 FPS.
napkin_math: Current total = 3*50 + 65 = 215W (15W over budget). Reduce perception freq by 10% (0.9x). New latency = 20ms / 0.9 = 22.2ms (meets 33.3ms deadline). New dynamic power = 40W * (0.9)^3 = 29.16W. New node power = 29.16W + 10W = 39.16W. Savings per node = 10.84W. Total savings = 32.52W. New total = 3*39.16 + 65 = 182.48W (Safely under 200W).

title: Drone Edge Compute vs Flight Efficiency
scenario: A drone performs powerline inspection. The battery is 150 Wh. The motors draw 250W. Running the vision model in FP16 draws 30W at 30 FPS. Quantizing the model to INT8 reduces chip power to 10W, but due to slight accuracy degradation, the drone must fly 20% slower to capture more overlapping frames for reliable detection. Evaluate which precision maximizes the total distance inspected.
common_mistake: Optimizing solely for the AI chip's power consumption without considering the massive baseline power draw of the drone's mechanical systems.
realistic_solution: Calculate the total flight time for both configurations based on total system power (motors + compute). Then calculate the relative distance covered, factoring in the slower flight speed of the INT8 configuration. In robotics and drones, optimizing edge compute power is counterproductive if it significantly increases the runtime of the massive mechanical power draw. The FP16 model uses more compute power but allows faster flight, resulting in a net increase in total inspection distance.
napkin_math: FP16 Total Power = 250W + 30W = 280W. Flight Time = 150 Wh / 280W = 0.535 hours. Distance = 0.535 * Speed_V. INT8 Total Power = 250W + 10W = 260W. Flight Time = 150 Wh / 260W = 0.576 hours. Distance = 0.576 * (0.8 * Speed_V) = 0.461 * Speed_V. FP16 covers 16% more distance despite higher compute power.

title: Retail Edge vs Cloud LLM TCO
scenario: A retail chain evaluates adding a local voice assistant to 5,000 stores. Cloud API serving costs $0.50 per 1M tokens. Alternatively, an edge server costs $1,500 CapEx (amortized over 3 years), draws 150W continuously, and electricity costs $0.12/kWh. Average usage per store is 20,000 tokens per day. Evaluate the 3-year Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) per store to decide between edge and cloud.
common_mistake: Focusing only on the per-token inference cost and ignoring the continuous static power draw and hardware amortization of the edge server.
realistic_solution: Calculate the operational cost of the cloud API over 3 years based on daily token volume. Compare this to the sum of the edge server's CapEx and its 3-year operational power cost. For low-utilization, bursty edge deployments, the static power draw and upfront hardware amortization vastly outweigh the per-token cost of centralized cloud serving. The cloud deployment is orders of magnitude cheaper.
napkin_math: Cloud: 20k tokens/day * 365 = 7.3M tokens/yr. 3-year tokens = 21.9M. Cloud Cost = 21.9 * $0.50 = $10.95 over 3 years. Edge: CapEx = $1500. Power = 150W * 24h * 365d * 3yrs = 3942 kWh. Power Cost = 3942 * $0.12 = $473.04. Edge TCO = $1500 + $473.04 = $1973.04 over 3 years. Cloud ($10.95) is massively cheaper.

title: Mobile Speculative Decoding Energy Efficiency
scenario: A smartphone runs a 3B parameter LLM locally. Standard autoregressive generation draws 4W and produces 10 tokens/sec. Speculative decoding uses a 100M draft model (draws 1W) and verifies using the 3B model (draws 4W). A verification step takes 100ms, evaluates 4 draft tokens in parallel, and has an average acceptance rate of 2.5 tokens per step. Evaluate the energy efficiency (Joules per token) of both methods.
common_mistake: Assuming speculative decoding uses more energy because it runs two models and draws higher peak power (5W total).
realistic_solution: Calculate the energy consumed per token for standard generation. For speculative decoding, calculate the total energy per step (drafting + parallel verification) and divide by the expected number of accepted tokens per step. Speculative decoding not only reduces latency but can significantly improve energy efficiency on battery-constrained devices, provided the acceptance rate is high enough to offset the draft model's overhead and the higher peak power draw.
napkin_math: Standard: 10 tok/s = 100ms/token. Energy = 4W * 0.1s = 0.4 J/token. Speculative: Draft 4 tokens takes 100ms (1W * 0.1s = 0.1 J). Verification takes 100ms (4W * 0.1s = 0.4 J). Total step energy = 0.5 J. Tokens accepted per step = 2.5. Energy per token = 0.5 J / 2.5 tok = 0.2 J/token. Speculative decoding cuts energy consumption in half.

title: IoT Edge TPU Sleep State Optimization
scenario: An industrial IoT sensor runs an anomaly model every 200ms. Inference takes 10ms on an edge TPU (2.5W active). In "clock-gated" mode, it uses 0.5W and wakes in 1ms (drawing 1.0W during wake). In "power-gated" mode, it uses 0.05W but takes 40ms to wake (drawing 1.5W during wake). Evaluate which sleep state minimizes average energy consumption per cycle.
common_mistake: Choosing the power-gated mode solely because 0.05W is much lower than 0.5W, ignoring the massive energy penalty of the wake-up phase.
realistic_solution: Calculate the energy consumed over a single 200ms duty cycle for both sleep states. Break the cycle into active time, wake time, and sleep time. Power gating has a much lower static draw but incurs a significant energy penalty during its long wake-up sequence. The optimal state depends entirely on whether the idle duration is longer than the break-even time of the deep sleep penalty. In this case, the duty cycle is long enough that power gating saves more energy.
napkin_math: Cycle = 200ms. Active = 10ms. Clock-gated: Wake=1ms, Sleep=189ms. Energy = (10ms*2.5W) + (1ms*1.0W) + (189ms*0.5W) = 25 + 1 + 94.5 = 120.5 mJ. Power-gated: Wake=40ms, Sleep=150ms. Energy = (10ms*2.5W) + (40ms*1.5W) + (150ms*0.05W) = 25 + 60 + 7.5 = 92.5 mJ. Power-gated saves 28 mJ per cycle.
